[methods] Materials and methods Sample collection Postmortem brain tissue from a total of 72 subjects diagnosed as normal CTR, SZ, or BP were obtained from the Pritzker Brain Bank at the University of California, Irvine (UCI). All studies were approved by the Institutional Review Board of UCI and the University of Michigan. All subjects had zero agonal factors with postmortem interval (PMI) of <37 h and all but one subject had pH above 6.5 to maximize mRNA preservation 92 , 93 (see summaries Table 1 , also comprehensive demographics Supplementary Tables S1 and S2 ). Table 1 Demographic information for diagnostic groups studied in the aMCC and dPCC/RSC/ESC analyses. Diagnosis N s Age pH PMI Sex (m:f) Cause of death Race aMCC Control (CTR) 22 51 ± 13 32–73 6.90 ± 0.11 6.67–7.07 21.81 ± 6.64 10.00–36.50 19:3 Accident: 5 SMC: 16 LTMC: 1 Caucasian: 20 African American: 1 Pacific Islander: 1 Schizophrenia (SZ) 17 42 ± 10 24–58 6.80 ± 0.13 6.59–7.05 20.49 ± 8.27 6.25–34.00 14:3 Accident: 3 SMC: 8 Suicide: 3 UD/AOD: 3 Caucasian: 17 African American: 1 Asian: 1 Other: 1 Bipolar Disorder (BP) 21 50 ± 12 25–70 6.79 ± 0.15 6.52–7.05 22.05 ± 7.15 9.20–36.00 13:8 Accident: 5 SMC: 4 Suicide: 10 UD/AOD: 2 Caucasian: 20 Other: 1 dPCC/RSC/ESC Control (CTR) 21 50 ± 13 32–73 6.92 ± 0.12 6.67–7.10 22.97 ± 6.65 10.00–36.50 19:2 Accident: 5 SMC: 15 LTMC: 1 Caucasian: 18 African American: 1 Asian: 1 Pacific Islander: 1 Schizophrenia (SZ) 21 43 ± 11 24–63 6.80 ± 0.23 6.19–7.21 21.89 ± 9.31 6.25–44.25 18:3 Accident: 3 SMC: 11 Suicide: 4 UD/AOD: 3 Caucasian: 18 African American: 1 Asian: 1 Other: 1 Bipolar Disorder (BP) 21 47 ± 13 23–69 6.81 ± 0.17 6.52–7.12 21.08 ± 7.53 9.00–36.00 13:8 Accident: 4 SMC: 4 Suicide: 11 UD/AOD: 2 Caucasian: 20 Other: 1 Summary values are listed for age, pH, and PMI (mean ± standard deviation) for the aMCC and dPCC/RSC/ESC regions by diagnosis, with the range of values for each group italicized below (when appropriate; also, see Supplementary Tables S1 and S2 and Fig. S1 ). aMCC anterior midcingulate cortex, dPCC dorsal posterior cingulate cortex, RSC retrosplenial cortex, ESC ectosplenial cortex, n number, m male, f female, SMC sudden medical condition, UD/AOD undetermined/accidental overdose, LTMC long-term medical condition, PMI postmortem interval. Brain dissection and in situ hybridization (ISH) Slabs containing aMCC and dPCC/RSC/ESC regions were visually inspected to identify gross anatomical landmarks, then subdissected (see Supplementary Fig. S1 for a representative sagittal brain schematic illustrating gross cingulate dissection locations and Figs. 1 a and 2a for coronal GAD67 ISH representations of anterior and posterior divisions, respectively). Tissue processing methods have been previously described 94 . Briefly, following brain removal, coronal slabs of ~0.8 cm were cut, flash-frozen, and stored at -80 °C. Blocks were sectioned at 10 µm in a cryostat, thaw mounted onto Superfrost TM Plus glass slides (Fisher Scientific, Pittsburgh, PA), and stored at −80 °C. 35 S-labeled ISH was performed utilizing gene-specific [ 35 S]UTP and [ 35 S]ATP-labeled cRNA riboprobes for GAD67, PV, SST, and VIP. Slides containing the sections were exposed to film and developed at which point digitized images were quantified with ImageJ software (NIH) to measure signal intensity, defined as optical density (o.d.), for each gene. Additional details regarding ISH, quantitative assessment, and image preparation/manipulation are included in the Supplementary Materials. Statistical analysis To evaluate whether a diagnosis of SZ or BP resulted in gene expression changes in aMCC or dPCC/RSC/ESC subregions, a mixed model analysis for each gene was conducted by letting the intercept vary by subject (random effect on subject). The gene expression measurement of o.d. (optical density) was the dependent variable with diagnosis and cortical subregion and their interaction as the independent variables. In aMCC, a correlational analysis indicated that pH be included as an independent variable for SST and freezer storage time be included for GAD67, PV, SST and VIP expression (see Supplementary Figs. S3 – 6 for age, pH, PMI, and storage time, respectively). In dPCC/RSC/ESC, the correlational analysis led to the inclusion of pH as an independent variable for all genes and age for SST (see Supplementary Figs. S7 – 10 for age, pH, PMI, and storage time, respectively). The mixed model analysis was conducted using the lme4 package in R 95 with the marginal means calculated using the emmeans package 96 . Degrees of freedom were estimated using Satterthwaite’s method using the package lmerTest 97 . Gene expression in control subjects was examined using an ANOVA where anatomical subregion and o.d. were included as factors. Tukey post hoc correction was used following all mixed model ANOVAs when appropriate. All results were considered statistically significant at p < 0.05.

[results] Results Gene expression in control subjects Visual inspection of ISH signal demonstrated the anticipated qualitative distributions for each gene, including PV gradients in the aMCC and dPCC/RSC/ESC regions of CTR subjects (Figs. 1 and 2 ). The distribution of PV appeared, by eye, to be more intense in the dorsal aspects of the aMCC (Fig. 1c ) and stronger ventrally within the posterior regions (Fig. 2c ), particularly within the RSC/ESC. These observations and an effect of subregion (MCC; F (2,89.083) = 79.731, p < 0.0001 and dPCC/RSC/ESC; F (5,222.536) = 111.400, p < 0.0001) led to ANOVA analysis to determine potential subregional differences in control subjects. Indeed, PV expression was lower in BA24ab’ compared to BA32’ (Supplementary Fig. S11 ; p < 0.001) and BA24c’ (Supplementary Fig. S11 ; p = 0.017). In the dPCC/RSC/ESC, there were dorsoventral and mediolateral gradients of PV expression with the lowest PV levels measured dorsally in BAd23c, BAd23b, and BAd23a (Fig. 2c and Supplementary Table S3 ). Mixed model ANOVAs revealed that RSC BA30 had elevated PV expression vs. BAd23c, BAd23b, and BAd23a (Fig. 2c and Supplementary Table S3 ; p < 0.003). Furthermore, BA29 and BA26 were similar ( p = 0.753), and each displayed the highest PV o.d. signals compared to all other regions (Fig. 2c and Supplementary Table S3 ; p < 0.002). No variations in GAD67, SST, or VIP o.d. were found between subregions in the aMCC or dPCC/RSC/ESC of CTR groups (Supplementary Fig. S12 ). Fig. 1 Interneuron markers exhibit different distribution patterns in aMCC subregions as well as differences in gene expression between healthy control vs. psychiatric groups as revealed by radioactive in situ hybridization. a Representative GAD67 mRNA expression in the coronal plane is shown with quantified subregions 32’, 24c’ and 24ab’ of the aMCC. b , c , e , f Monocolor images of adjacent in situ hybridization sections were pseudocolored, aligned and d overlayed to examine the relative spatial distributions in gene expression as shown. Note that the distribution of PV within aMCC tissue sections was most varied across subregions ( c , d ). g BP subjects had reduced levels of GAD67 vs. CTR, specifically in region 24c’ ( p = 0.041). h A nonsignificant trend for decreased PV expression was observed in area 32’ for SZ subjects vs. CTR ( p = 0.096). i Reduced PV expression was also noted in 24c’, where SZ subjects had reduced expression vs. CTR ( p = 0.028). VIP expression was lower in the SZ group vs. CTR in all examined aMCC subregions ( j p = 0.036; k p = 0.042; l p = 0.044). Each dot represents one subject; data is presented as mean ± standard deviation. * p < 0.05. aMCC anterior midcingulate cortex, cc corpus callosum, D dorsal, GAD67 glutamic acid decarboxylase 67, M medial, PV parvalbumin, SST somatostatin, VIP vasoactive intestinal peptide. Fig. 2 Distribution and differential expression of interneuron marker genes within the dPCC/RSC/ESC of psychiatric and control subjects. a Representative coronal plane for subregional quantification of GAD67 mRNA expression is shown in the dPCC BAs (regions d23c, d23b, and d23a), the RSC (regions 30 and 29) and ESC (region 26). b – d Monochrome images of adjacent tissues sections processed for in situ hybridization were pseudocolored, aligned and e overlayed using Adobe Photoshop to visually examine the relative distribution of signal (VIP not shown). PV mRNA differed between diagnoses in RSC and ESC, where SZ subjects had decreased PV in both the RSC ( f SZ vs. CTR: p = 0.037; SZ vs. BP: p = 0.020; and g SZ vs. CTR: p < 0.0001) and ESC ( h SZ vs. CTR: p < 0.0001). PV expression was also decreased in BP subjects compared to CTRs in RSC region 29 ( g p = 0.021) and ESC ( h p = 0.008). A non-significant trend for decreased SST expression was observed in BAd23c, 30, and 29 ( i – k respectively). Each dot represents one subject; data is presented as mean ± standard deviation. *** p < 0.001; ** p < 0.01; * p < 0.05. BP Bipolar Disorder subject group, cc corpus callosum, CTR non-psychiatric control subject group, D dorsal, dPCC dorsal posterior cingulate cortex, ESC ectosplenial cortex, GAD67 glutamic acid decarboxylase 67, M medial, PV parvalbumin, RSC retrosplenial cortex, SST somatostatin, SZ schizophrenia subject group. Diagnosis-specific aMCC gene expression We first determined if aMCC gene expression was different between CTR and the SZ and BP groups. Although the effect of diagnosis was not significant for GAD67 ( F (2,53.255) = 2.145, p = 0.127) and PV ( F (2,54.302) = 2.768, p = 0.072), post-hoc analyses revealed that o.d. measurements were significantly lower versus the CTR group in BA24c’ of BP (Fig. 1g ; p = 0.041) and SZ subjects, respectively (Fig. 1i ; p = 0.028). SZ subjects also showed a trend for decreased PV expression in BA32’ (Fig. 1h ; p = 0.096). For VIP expression, the effect of diagnosis was significant ( F (2,54.150) = 3.420, p = 0.040) with signal intensity lower in SZ subjects across all subregions versus CTRs (Fig. 1j–l ; BA32’; p = 0.036, BA24c’; p = 0.042, BA24ab’; p = 0.044). SST gene expression did not reveal an effect of diagnosis ( F (2,51.632) = 1.329, p = 0.274) or significant post-hoc findings for any subregion (data not shown). For all genes, mixed model ANOVAs showed no significant diagnosis x subregion interactions (data not shown). Diagnosis-specific dPCC/RSC/ESC gene expression Analysis of PV gene expression in dPCC/RSC/ESC showed both a significant effect of diagnosis ( F (2,59.349) = 4.222, p = 0.019) and diagnosis x subregion interaction ( F (10,222.602) = 3.875, p < 0.0001). Subsequent post-hoc analysis indicated that PV gene expression was lower in each of the SZ and BP groups vs. CTR and was confined to the RSC/ESC in both cases (Fig. 2f–h ). In BA30 of the SZ group, PV expression was decreased versus both CTR ( p = 0.037) and BP ( p = 0.020) groups (Fig. 2f ). In contrast, compared to CTR group, PV signal intensity values in BA29 (Fig. 2g ) and BA26 (Fig. 2h ) were each lower in the SZ ( p < 0.0001) and BP groups (BA29, p = 0.021 and BA26, p = 0.008). SST expression was not associated with a significant effect of diagnosis ( F (2,57.725) = 2.122, p = 0.129), but there was an indication of an appreciable trend for reduced gene expression in the dPCC and RSC of BP subjects (Fig. 2i–k ). Compared to the CTR group, this tendency in BP was just above significance (BAd23c; p = 0.091, BA30; p = 0.079, BA29; p = 0.082). There was no effect of diagnosis for VIP ( F (2,55.654) = 0.942, p = 0.396) nor were there diagnosis x subregion interactions (data not shown). Similarly, post-hoc analyses did not reveal any significant gene expression findings for GAD67 and VIP (data not shown). See Fig. 3 for schematic summarizing results. Fig. 3 Summary of interneuron-related gene expression changes. Decreases in the expression of interneuron-related genes (left, GAD67: purple, top to bottom, VIP: yellow, SST: red; PV: green) are summarized for both aMCC (left) and dPCC/RSC/ESC (right) regions. In aMCC, GAD67 expression was reduced in BA24c’ of BP subject vs. CTR. In contrast, aMCC VIP expression was decreased in SZ (32’, 24c’, and 24ab’) compared to CTR, presumably reflecting reduced inhibition of cortical interneurons (e.g., SST and/or PV) as well as decreased inhibition at the apical dendrite of pyramidal neurons. Compared to CTRs, there was a statistical trend for decreased SST expression in BP subjects within the dPCC (regions d23c) and RSC (regions 30 and 29). These potential decreases in SST may occur in Martinotti cells, known to synapse onto the apical tufts of excitatory pyramidal neurons. Decreased PV expression (vs. CTRs) was found in SZ subjects in the aMCC (24c’), RSC (regions 30 and 29) and ESC (region 26), while decreased PV expression in BP subjects (vs. CTRs) was found in the RSC region 29 and ESC (region 26). PV expression is localized to both basket cells and chandelier cells of the aMCC and RSC/ESC, where these neurons contact pyramidal neurons at the soma and initial axon segment, respectively.

[discussion] Discussion The current study was conducted to enhance the understanding of potential GABA-ergic circuit dysfunction in the cingulate gyrus of two disorders, SZ and BP that feature shared neurocognitive impairments of varied magnitude 3 , 4 . Our findings reveal distinct and overlapping transcriptional signatures of schizophrenia (SZ) and bipolar disorder (BP) within specific subregions of Brodmann’s areas in the cingulate gyrus. In this section, we speculate how diagnosis-related dysregulation of specific aMCC GABA-ergic cell-types may impact attention allocation toward action-outcomes and/or affect motor responses associated with the SAN as well as how similar RSC/ESC mechanisms may undermine visuospatial memory and attention distribution in relation to the DMN. Our results highlight both unique and common gene expression patterns that may relate to core symptoms of SZ and BP. Decreased GAD67 and VIP gene expression were exclusive to aMCC, but differentially displayed by BP and SZ subjects, respectively. In contrast, while diminished PV mRNA signal was specific to SZ subjects in aMCC, similar PV deficits were shared by SZ and BP subjects in the RSC/ESC. SST gene expression was not significantly altered in any subregion along the cingulate gyrus extent. However, an appreciable trend for reduced SST mRNA signal was observed in the dPCC/RSC of BP subjects. Here, we discuss the potential neuropsychiatric implications using as a framework previously proposed alterations in dynamic shifting between large-scale neurocognitive networks (DMN, SAN, and CEN) in accordance with the triple network of psychopathology. GAD67 expression Our results showing a non-significant decrease in GAD67 expression within aMCC of SZ subjects are similar to the analysis by Hashimoto et al. 32 . This study also did not find a diagnosis x subregion interaction for GAD67 mRNA levels, which were unchanged intrinsically, but rather comparably decreased when statistically considered along with expression within visuomotor network nodes (DLPFC, primary motor, and visual cortices). In contrast, some aMCC studies evaluating potential changes in SZ have reported a general non-subregional reduction in GAD67 mRNA 33 , 34 as well as diminished GABA 34 . Taken together with the current analysis, it remains debatable how well local GAD67 mRNA expression aligns with the reduced basal aMCC GABA levels described by SZ meta-analyses 23 , 24 . Our results also put into question the potential and somewhat controversial association of reported cortical interneuron loss with GAD67 mRNA deficits in SZ. For example, in the adjacent pACC, decreased GAD67 mRNA expressing neurons 35 as well as fewer non-pyramidal neurons, presumably interneurons, particularly in layers II and V 28 – 30 , have been shown. Conversely, DLPFC analyses suggest that interneurons may be viable but express undetectable mRNA levels for GAD67 36 , SST (layer II) 37 , and PV (layer IV) 37 , without cell loss. In the case of aMCC, SZ subjects have been shown to exhibit a trend for blunted density of BA24c’ layer II calbindin-positive interneurons 38 , no layer change in PV density 38 , and increased layer V/VI non-subtype specific neurons 39 . Future research in aMCC layer II could further explore whether the loss of GABAergic cells is linked to SZ. Our aMCC findings in BP subjects reveal reduced BA24c’ GAD67 mRNA. Previous neuroimaging studies examining BP patients collectively suggest no difference in GABA within that general region 40 . In addition, Ramaker et al. 34 , despite sampling aMCC in an overlapping subject cohort with the current investigation, did not observe reduced GAD67 mRNA in BP. However, the same study did uncover reduced GABA which parallels our GAD67 results. While the current findings oppose a report of an overall increase in aMCC BA24c’ neuronal density within layer VI of BP subjects 39 , they are more consistent with pACC data in BP showing a consistent reduction of layer II non-pyramidal neuron density 28 – 30 , decreased GAD67 mRNA-positive neurons 35 , and fewer GAD65-positive terminals 41 . From a functional standpoint, altered aMCC GABA-ergic systems in BP would be expected to impact encoding evaluative feedback from conflict-driven action-outcomes for future decision-making 42 , 43 . Normal aMCC activity elicited by incorrect choices during this process generates a GABA-mediated 44 error-related negativity (ERN) measure 45 , 46 that is attenuated across patients with SZ 47 – 49 , psychotic-bipolar 47 , 49 , and euthymic-bipolar when controlling for depressive symptoms 50 . A diminished ERN is also connected to poor real-world functioning, negative symptom severity, and compromised executive function in psychotic disorders 49 . Such manifestations may be linked to aberrant large-scale neurocognitive network interactions, particularly concerning the basal ganglia. Meta-analysis demonstrates a strikingly common reduction in aMCC-caudate FC across first-episode psychosis (schizophrenia and schizoaffective disorder), BP, and psychotic-bipolar patients 51 . Considering the caudate also functions as a SAN node, aMCC GAD67 mRNA deficits in BP subjects may contribute to psychomotor deficits through aberrant corticostriatal circuits and blunted error processing/action monitoring, thereby supporting the triple network hypothesis of psychopathology. Parvalbumin expression The neurocognitive implications of decreased PV gene expression in aMCC (BA24c’) and RSC/ESC (BA30/29/26) of SZ as well as BP (BA29/26) subjects can be posited from the neurophysiological properties of PV cells along with neuroanatomical and functional connectivity. Most PV interneurons are fast-spiking basket cells which harness perisomal synapses for feedforward and feedback inhibition and the generation of gamma band oscillations for synchronizing activity of pyramidal cell assemblies 31 , 52 . Convincing arguments have been made for connecting reduced DLPFC PV gene expression and GABA-ergic inhibition of local pyramidal cells to altered gamma oscillatory activity and cognitive deficits in SZ 52 . The current results combined with other work in SZ revealing a trend toward decreased aMCC PV mRNA 32 alludes to potentially similar deleterious effects of dysregulated BA24c’ gamma oscillatory activity on cognition. Several SZ studies examining PV interneurons in the BA24c region of pACC indicate reduced somal size and dendritic tree extent as well as increased axonal cartridges and layer V PV+ cell count 53 – 55 . Although the latter concurs with heightened aMCC layer V/VI neuronal density in SZ 39 , our results imply an overall hypofunction of BA24c’ PV interneurons, potentially affecting local gamma band oscillatory activity. Functional imaging studies often show that tasks associated with cognitive motor control coincides with BA32’/24c’ co-activation, thus acquiring the collective term dorsal aMCC (daMCC) (see review by Vogt et al.) 8 . This collaboration is of particular importance considering our results demonstrate that SZ subjects also displayed a trend for reduced PV expression in BA32’. The functional capacity of daMCC is expansive with its activity implicated in divided attention, movement anticipation, and action initiation while also serving as the cingulate region engaged in the monitoring of ongoing action-outcomes 8 and producing ERN responses 56 . Upper primate studies indicate BA24c’ function is mediated by reciprocal connections with DLPFC and premotor/supplementary motor cortex in concert with cingulospinal projections 57 – 60 . Given that DLPFC exhibits particularly strong FC with daMCC 45 , 61 and classifies as both a SAN node and CEN hub 20 , it is tempting to hypothesize that decreased daMCC PV expression may point to desynchronization of aMCC-DLPFC gamma oscillatory activity during cognitive motor control behaviors in SZ patients. In support, FC between BA24c’ and DLPFC is reduced in SZ subjects during verbal fluency tasks 62 . Within the caudal cingulate gyrus, RSC/ESC displayed the highest PV gene expression in controls while exhibiting reductions in SZ (BA30/29/26) and BP (29/26). Importantly, interpretating these results within context of previous RSC analyses is made challenging due to potential confounds regarding past neuroanatomical atlases/schematics that often incorrectly place its location caudal to the corpus callosum splenium on the surface of the caudomedial lobule, which is actually ventral PCC as discussed by Vogt et al. 63 . Our in situ hybridization assessment was conducted dorsal to the splenium and adjacent to the posterior MCC within what we refer to as anterior RSC. Careful attention to brain coordinates reveals anterior RSC likely shares functional similarities with its caudal counterpart where lesions induce impairment of visuospatial memory, impaired navigation, and episodic memory 11 . Indeed, anterior RSC activation couples with location/action recollection 10 , 64 , anticipatory allocation of spatial attention 65 , autobiographical memory 18 , and memory encoding 66 . In contrast, while caudal-ventral RSC is an integrated node of the self-referential DMN known to be dysregulated in SZ and BP, the role of anterior RSC is less clear. Our data demonstrates trends toward lower PV mRNA values in RSC (BA30/29))/ESC (BA26) in SZ versus BP, reaching significance in BA30. These findings are conceptually in-line with overlapping neurocognitive deficits in SZ and BP where SZ patients display greater impairment in attention/speed of processing and visual memory 3 , 4 . Compared to controls, SZ patients demonstrate increased anterior RSC activity during self-reflection 67 , which is influenced by visuospatial attention 68 , as well as enhanced FC with the lingual gyrus 69 , indicating heightened interaction with visual systems. SZ patients experiencing auditory verbal hallucinations exhibit increased anterior RSC-DMN FC and decreased anterior RSC-hippocampal formation FC 70 – 72 . In addition, less anterior RSC deactivation is observed during verbal fluency tasks in both SZ and BP patients 73 . Collectively, lower anterior RSC PV gene expression points to the possibility of altered gamma oscillations contributing to both positive and negative symptoms, particularly in SZ. To our knowledge, the current study is among the initial quantitative analyses of gene expression performed in ESC (BA26). Positioned lateral to anterior RSC BA29 and dorsal to the hippocampal rudiment, also referred to as indusium griseum 63 , downregulation of BA26 PV gene expression in SZ and BP is more difficult to interpret. Precise ESC function is unknown due to, in part, its diminutive size being less than ideal for functional imaging assessments 63 . Adjacent to the uniformly reduced PV gene expression in BA30/29, we hypothesize ESC in SZ and BP subjects may exhibit disorganized filtering of unrefined visuospatial memory-related information. The latter is likely to be communicated to a compromised anterior RSC in SZ and BP for further processing via ESC-BA29 interconnections. Somatostatin expression Previous cortical studies focused on SZ and BP have shown SST mRNA to be one of the more downregulated of the major interneuron markers with respect to magnitude of change 32 , 74 , 75 . Interestingly, the current cingulate analyses did not uncover significant findings amongst any cingulate subregion. Instead, we report non-significant decreases in subregional aMCC SST gene expression in SZ. This aligns more closely with findings of decreased SST mRNA demonstrated by Hashimoto et al. 32 , which did not show a diagnosis x region interaction but rather demonstrated an overall reduction in SST when analyzed in conjunction with other nodes in the long-range visuomotor network. On the other hand, there were remarkably stronger trends, just missing significance, for decreased SST expression in dPCC (BAd23c) and RSC (BA30/29) of BP subjects that mirror the PV reductions seen ventrally in both SZ and BP. Furthermore, our findings agree with prior investigations of orbital frontal cortex and DLPFC showing comparatively lower mean SST mRNA levels in BP versus SZ 74 . Consequently, the inherently different synaptic patterns of SST+ interneurons upon pyramidal cells versus PV+ cells dictates that dPCC/RSC/ESC information processing may to differ between SZ and BP thereby indicating one potential link to unique yet overlapping clinical features. SST+ cells are largely Martinotti type and synapse on distal dendrites of pyramidal cells 31 which receive afferent input from thalamic, dopaminergic, and long-range cortical afferents 76 . Thus, a tendency for decreased dPCC/RSC SST expression in BP subjects may reflect an enhanced liability for dysregulated sharpening of input from long-range cortical and subcortical afferents. Indeed, SST+ neurons are important for synchronizing the activities of distant brain regions to achieve optimal long-range cortico-cortical transmissions 77 , 78 . More specifically, while SST interneurons have been linked to generation of theta oscillations 79 , animal models demonstrate that disruption of SST+ cells may also minimize gamma oscillatory power 80 , 81 . Thus, potential alterations in gamma and/or theta band oscillatory activity, reflected by a trend for reduced SST mRNA, may be associated with disrupted local circuits as well as large-scale cognitive networks that contribute to compromised attention set shifting and visuospatial memory in BP. Vasoactive intestinal peptide expression Significant decreases in VIP gene expression were confined to aMCC of SZ subjects and present across all subregions (BA32’/24c’/24ab’). It is noteworthy to mention that decreased VIP expression in BA24ab’ of SZ subjects constitutes the only altered transcript in this region irrespective of diagnosis. Few cortical studies have investigated cortical VIP gene expression in psychiatric illness. Of these, reduced transcription has been reported in DLPFC and orbital frontal cortex of both SZ and BP 74 as well as the primary visual cortex in SZ subjects 75 . VIP + GABA-ergic interneurons receive long-range cortical afferent input and specialize in disinhibitory processes by primarily innervating pyramidal dendrite-targeting SST+ cells thereby increasing the gain of excitatory pyramidal neurons 31 . Altered gain control mediated by BA32’/24c’ (daMCC) VIP+ interneurons in SZ may contribute to impaired action monitoring, especially that related to processing of visuomotor incongruence 82 . Alternatively, similar dysregulation in BA24ab’ of SZ subjects may be of particular importance regarding avoidance/escape behaviors driven by daMCC 7 , 8 considering BA24ab’ receives greater input from the amygdala 57 and is uniquely activated by fear 8 . Rodent studies show that deactivation of VIP+ interneurons in ACC or mPFC is associated with more time spent in open arms during elevated maze testing 83 , 84 . In Humans, neuroimaging assessments reveal that BA24ab’ exhibits decreased activity in SZ versus control subjects at rest 85 as well as hypodeactivation during a facial emotion discrimination requiring matching/labeling of fear and anger depictions 86 . Similar studies show patients diagnosed as having high negative schizotypy with anhedonia demonstrate reduced FC between BA24ab’ and amygdala under both fear and happy conditions 69 . Furthermore, decreased FC between BA24ab’ and putamen is positively and negatively associated with delusion score and antipsychotic treatment, respectively 87 . Therefore, decreased BA24ab’ VIP expression in SZ subjects revealed here may point to the dysregulation of pyramidal cell gain control required for current and future avoidance behaviors related to fear in SZ patients. Limitations A limitation of the current study involves analyzing cryosections, without corresponding Nissl staining, from archived cingulate blocks extracted from frozen coronal brain slabs. Variable dissection outcomes due to multiple dissectors led to some subjects not having available, fully or partially, various targeted BAs. However, location of retained BA subregions could be approximated (see Figs. 1 a and 2a ) by comparing the positions of cell-based boundaries, as defined by previously published cingulate maps 5 , 6 , to visual interrelationships between anatomical landmarks and observed dorsoventral PV mRNA gradients which are in excellent agreement with immunohistochemical patterns 88 . Given the total area (mm 2 ) variability of BAs available for quantification, e.g., due to artifacts and/or or gross dissection differences, we acquired a single optical density value representing the average signal intensity above background for each BA. This measurement, corresponding to the entire available area within an outlined BA, differs from previous cortical studies that often sample smaller but consistent representative areas for acquiring cell counts to measure neuronal density. Consequently, an important question as to whether reduced aMCC gene expression may relate to lower interneuron density/loss, as indicated by some SZ and BP studies in pACC 28 – 30 , cannot be gleaned from the current study. In addition, without neurohistological identification of precise cortical layer borders, quantified BA subregions may have included minor contributions from superficial interstitial white matter just below cortical layer VI. Quantitative implications of the latter, if any, are unknown. However, it is notable that SZ subjects have been shown to display increased interneuron density in DLPFC superficial white matter as measured by GAD65/67 protein 89 and SST mRNA 90 . Similarly, both SZ and BP subjects demonstrate a 2-fold increase in density of white matter-localized NeuN+ neurons in aMCC 91 . Nevertheless, the present study still highlights diagnosis-related gene expression differences within functionally diverse cingulate subregions that should be considered BA-enriched.

[conclusion] Conclusions In summary, the present study uncovered divergent and overlapping reductions in interneuron subtype-specific gene expression within the rostral and caudal cingulate gyrus of SZ and BP subjects. In aMCC, altered PV and VIP mRNA signals were exclusive to SZ subjects and raise the possibility more prominent interneuron-related impairment versus BP that may lend to altered error/action monitoring and psychomotor behavior. Caudally, decreased PV gene expression was exhibited by both SZ and BP subjects in the RSC/ESC, which may reflect shared circuits affecting visuospatial memory processing and recollection. These results show unique diagnosis-related gene expression patterns in the cingulate gyrus of SZ and BP subjects and highlight neuroanatomical targets for conducting future psychiatric studies.

retrieved_papers.title: Association of air pollutants with psychiatric disorders: a two-sample Mendelian randomization.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2024]
[Abstract] The link between air pollution and increased risk of psychiatric disorders has been growing in evidence. However, the causal relationship between air pollution and psychiatric disorders remains poorly understood.
Single-nucleotide polymorphisms associated with air pollutants (including NOx, NO2, PM2.5, PM2.5-10, and PM10) from the UK Biobank were used as instrumental variables. Summary-level data for psychiatric disorders (major depressive disorder, anxiety, bipolar disorder, schizophrenia, post-traumatic stress disorder, attention deficit hyperactivity disorder, autism spectrum disorder, anorexia nervosa, and obsessive-compulsive disorder) were procured from the Psychiatric Genomics Consortium and FinnGen consortium. Two-sample Mendelian randomization (MR) analysis was conducted to analyze the causal associations.
The MR analysis revealed significant associations between certain air pollutants and specific types of psychiatric disorders. The inverse-variance weighted model of preliminary analysis indicated that genetically predicted NO2 was associated with increased risks of major depressive disorder (odds ratio [OR]: 1.13, 95 % confidence intervals [CI]: 1.00-1.28, P = 0.041), bipolar disorder (OR: 1.26, 95 % CI: 1.00-1.58, P = 0.0497), schizophrenia (OR: 1.57, 95 % CI: 1.23-2.00, P < 0.001), attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (OR: 1.61, 95 % CI: 1.25-2.09, P < 0.001) and autism spectrum disorder (OR: 1.39, 95 % CI: 1.01-1.91, P = 0.044). Genetically predicted PM2.5 showed a positive association with the risk of major depressive disorder (OR: 1.21, 95 % CI: 1.06-1.39, P = 0.006), bipolar disorder (OR: 1.32, 95 % CI: 1.03-1.69, P = 0.030) and attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (OR: 1.57, 95 % CI: 1.16-2.12, P = 0.004). In addition, our results also indicated that NOx (OR: 1.64, 95 % CI: 1.21-2.21, P = 0.0012) and PM10 (OR: 1.70, 95 % CI: 1.23-2.36, P = 0.0014) could increase the risk of attention deficit hyperactivity disorder.
The MR analysis provides evidence for the causality of different air pollutants on specific psychiatric disorders, underscoring the importance of mitigating air pollution to reduce the risk of psychiatric disorders.

retrieved_papers.title: Peripheral Immune-Inflammatory Pathways in Major Depressive Disorder, Bipolar Disorder, and Schizophrenia: Exploring Their Potential as Treatment Targets.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2025]
[Abstract] Major depressive disorder (MDD), bipolar disorder (BD), and schizophrenia (SCZ) are major mental disorders linked to substantial morbidity. Traditional monoamine-based pharmacotherapies frequently produce inadequate outcomes for many patients. The elevated levels of treatment resistance require the exploration of new pharmacological targets. Evidence indicates that peripheral immune-inflammatory dysregulation, characterized by an imbalance between immunological responses and compensatory immune-regulatory systems (IRS/CIRS), together with increased oxidative and nitrosative stress (O&NS), significantly contributes to the pathogenesis of these disorders. This review examines IRS/CIRS/O&NS pathways as new drug targets and highlights novel pharmacological trials. Antiinflammatory drugs have been repurposed as augmentation strategies for the treatment of MDD/BD and SCZ, including nonsteroidal antiinflammatory medications, such as cyclooxygenase-2 (COX-2) inhibitors; cytokine-targeting biologics, such as tumor necrosis factor-α monoclonal antibodies; and minocycline, an antibiotic that attenuates neuroinflammation. N-acetylcysteine, curcumin, and omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids demonstrate some efficacy as augmentation therapies in MDD, likely by diminishing IRS activation and O&NS. Strategies aimed at the gut-brain axis and gut dysbiosis, including fecal microbiota transplantation, are under investigation for their capacity to restore immunological homeostasis by improving gut barrier integrity and microbiome composition. This review examines new potential therapeutic targets arising from recent discoveries in neuro-immune interactions and oxidative stress, including particular lymphocyte surface markers, the CIRS, and intracellular network molecules in both affective and psychotic disorders. The evidence underscores the clinical importance of immune-targeted augmentation treatments in psychiatric disorders and supports the ongoing development of these novel pharmacotherapies within a precision medicine paradigm.

[Introduction] 1 Introduction Major depressive disorder (MDD) is a severe mental health condition marked by consistently low mood and lack of interest in activities. MDD affects 4.4 % of the global population, substantially impairing somatic and mental health, while generating considerable socioeconomic burdens for caregivers and healthcare infrastructure ( World Health Organization, 2017 ). Although monoaminergic agents retain their first-line status in MDD management ( Almulla and Maes, 2025 ), their clinical utility is frequently compromised by side effects and withdrawal syndromes, ultimately diminishing real-world effectiveness ( Carreno and Frazer, 2017 ), particularly in treatment-resistant cases. In recent years, increasing research has highlighted the critical role of peripheral systems, especially the microbiota-gut-brain (MGB) axis, in the pathophysiology of mood disorders. This axis interconnects the gut microecological environment with central neural activity through neural, immune, and endocrine signaling networks. As the main bidirectional communication pathway between the central nervous system and the gastrointestinal tract, the vagus nerve (VN) plays a pivotal role in the MGB axis. Approximately 80 % of its fibers are afferent, which can transmit chemical and inflammatory signals from the intestine to the nucleus tractus solitarius and regulate emotion and cognition ( Bonaz et al., 2018 ). In contrast, postganglionic cholinergic projections of the VN regulate peripheral immune responses through inflammatory reflexes, affecting intestinal and systemic inflammatory states ( Goggins et al., 2025 ; Jiang et al., 2024 ). Studies have shown that transection of the VN abolishes the anxiolytic effects of specific probiotics in a model of intestinal inflammation, providing strong evidence that the influence of gut microbes on central function depends on intact vagal pathways ( Bercik et al., 2011 ). Given the central role of the VN within the MGB axis, invasive vagus nerve stimulation (VNS) has emerged as a promising therapeutic approach for patients with treatment-resistant depression ( Aaronson et al., 2017 ). Clinical research has revealed that VNS improves the quality of life and functionality in patients with depression unresponsive to treatment ( Rush et al., 2025 ). However, the invasive nature of the procedure carries risks, including hemorrhage and infection, which restrict its clinical utility ( Revesz et al., 2016 ). These safety concerns have prompted the development of noninvasive alternatives, particularly approaches that target the VN transcutaneously. The auricular concha is a promising region, as neuroanatomical studies have confirmed it as the only area of the body surface innervated by VN fibers ( Peuker and Filler, 2002 ), providing a direct anatomical rationale for transcutaneous auricular vagus nerve stimulation (taVNS). Emerging clinical evidence suggests that taVNS produces therapeutic antidepressant benefits, potentially comparable to that of VNS in specific populations, while offering superior safety profiles, clinical feasibility, broader accessibility, and cost-effectiveness ( Li et al., 2021 ). Crucially, randomized neuroimaging trials have validated taVNS-mediated normalization of aberrant default mode network connectivity in MDD, emphasizing its mechanistic plausibility and translational applicability ( Sun et al., 2023 ). In addition, taVNS has been reported to influence gut microbiota composition and enhance the abundance of beneficial bacterial taxa, thereby promoting gut homeostasis ( Liu et al., 2024 ). Thus, the antidepressant effect of taVNS may be partially rely on its modulation of gut microbiota. Alterations in the gut microbiota have been shown to impact molecular signaling cascades underlying neuroplasticity ( Tang et al., 2025 ). Disruptions in microbial composition can modify the expression of glutamatergic receptor subunits, especially the key structural unit of the N-methyl-D-aspartate receptor (NMDAR), the glutamatergic ionotropic receptor subunit 1 (GluN1). Such changes may affect synaptic plasticity and excitatory neural transmission in circuits governing emotional processing ( Takahashi et al., 2024 ). As an essential NMDAR subunit, GluN1 activity can be directly regulated by gut microbes and their metabolites. Certain intestinal bacteria can induce enteric neurons to synthesize D-serine, which selectively activates non-classical NMDAR complexes containing GluN1 (GluN1-GluN3 subtypes), thereby regulating neuronal excitability and synaptic signaling independently of the classical NMDAR pathway ( Osorio et al., 2023 ). Activation of NMDARs subsequently triggers the mitogen-activated protein kinase (MAPK) signaling cascade, serving as a key conduit for the transmission of peripheral microbial signals to the central nervous system ( Franco et al., 2018 ). The MAPK family, including c-Jun N-terminal kinase (JNK), extracellular signal-regulated kinase (ERK), and P38 isoforms, is pivotal in this process, and its phosphorylation state is modulated by gut microbial composition and the functional integrity of the intestinal barrier ( Ikegami et al., 2024 ). Dysbiosis or chronic inflammation can lead to MAPK pathway dysfunction and downregulate the synthesis of brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF) ( Genheden et al., 2015 ; Park et al., 2024 ), while NMDAR-GluN1 activation can reverse the MAPK phosphorylation, forming a dynamic bidirectional regulation ( Ragnarsson et al., 2023 ). Moreover, gut-derived metabolites such as short-chain fatty acids and tryptophan derivatives can activate the MAPK/ERK signaling cascade ( Luo et al., 2024 ), thereby promoting the expression of BDNF and its receptor tropomyosin receptor kinase B (TrkB), ultimately enhancing neuronal survival and plasticity ( Ge et al., 2023 ). The BDNF-TrkB signaling pathway is considered a central molecular link in the antidepressant response, and its activation promotes ERK phosphorylation and induces cAMP response element binding protein (CREB) transcription, forming a positive feedback loop within the GluN1/MAPK/BDNF axis ( Chen et al., 2023 ; Kim et al., 2025 ). Collectively, gut microbiota may modulate neuroplasticity via the GluN1/MAPK/BDNF cascade, thus participating in the occurrence and remission of depression at the gut-brain axis level. In this context, we hypothesize that taVNS regulates gut microbiota composition through the VN, thereby affecting the GluN1/MAPK/BDNF signaling pathway to promote central nervous plasticity and antidepressant effects. To address this, we integrated proteomic and metagenomic analyses, as well as molecular and histopathological techniques, to investigate the MGB axis of the chronic unpredictable mild stress (CUMS) depression rat model and reveal the mechanism of taVNS-mediated antidepressant from a multi-omics perspective. These findings establish the mechanisms and translational evidence to support the clinical application of taVNS, while providing a framework for future research.

[methods] 2 Materials and methods 2.1 Animals and experimental procedure Thirty-two male Sprague-Dawley rats (SPF, 160–200 g) were utilized. These rats were obtained with institutional approval (No. SCXK(Su) 2024-0001) and kept in a standard environment. At the same time, water and food were freely available. The Animal Ethics Committee of Nanjing University of Chinese Medicine approved the study protocol (Approval No. 202411A068). The experimental timeline is shown in Fig. 1 A. Following a 7-day acclimation period, the rats were randomly assigned to four groups (Control, CUMS, CUMS + fluoxetine (Flu), and CUMS + taVNS; n = 8/group) and housed four per cage under optimal rearing conditions. During days 21–35 of modeling, all groups received synchronized daily interventions. Specifically, Control and CUMS rats received 0.9 % saline by gavage; CUMS + Flu rats were administered 10 mg/kg fluoxetine (Shanxi C&Y Pharmaceutical Group, Shanxi, China; Lot#230816) dissolved in 0.9 % saline by gavage; and CUMS + taVNS rats were stimulated using an electroacupuncture apparatus (Hwato brand, China). Body weight was measured every seven days from the start of modeling, and a battery of behavioral tests was conducted on days 21 and 35, respectively. Fig. 1 Treatment with transcutaneous auricular vagus nerve stimulation (taVNS) improves chronic unpredictable mild stress (CUMS)-induced depression-related behaviors. (A) Flowchart of the experimental timeline. (B) Schematic of taVNS application. (C) Weekly body mass changes in each group (*** p < 0.001 compared with CUMS group). (D) Total distance traveled during the open field test (OFT). (E) Center movement distance during the OFT. (F) Immobility time in the forced swim test (FST). (G) Sucrose consumption during the testing phase of the sucrose preference test (SPT). (H) Open arm travel distance in the elevated plus maze (EPM) test. (I) Trajectory diagrams for the OFT and EPM test. ( n = 8 per group; * p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01, *** p < 0.001). Fig. 1 2.2 The CUMS model The CUMS model is the most commonly utilized, reliable, and validated model for mimicking MDD ( Antoniuk et al., 2019 ). During the 35-day modeling phase, the non-control groups were exposed to daily randomized stressors, including 24-h exposure to wet bedding, 5-min tail clipping, ice-water immersion (5 min, 4 °C), 2 h of restraint, 24 h deprivation of water or food, 24-h day/night cycle reversal, and 24-h exposure to inclined bedding (at 45°), with no stressor repeated within a 3-day period. This model was selected because of its ability to emulate human psychopathological responses to unpredictable life stressors through exposure to diverse stimuli ( Willner, 1997 ). Critically, chronic stress disrupts hippocampal glutamatergic neurotransmission and impairs neuroplasticity, thus providing a neurobiologically relevant framework for investigating the pathogenesis of depression. 2.3 Protocol for taVNS During days 21–35 of modeling, the CUMS + taVNS rats received daily taVNS stimulation. Prior to stimulation, the animals were gently restrained, and 2 × 1 cm electrode patches were positioned on both auricular conchae. Stimulation (20 Hz, 2 mA) was administered for 30 min daily between 10:00 a.m. and 12:00 p.m. to control for circadian influences ( Fig. 1 B) ( Guo et al., 2020 ). 2.4 Behavioral tests 2.4.1 Open field test (OFT) The OFT evaluates risk-taking and anxiety-like behaviors ( Knight et al., 2021 ). The rats were familiarized with the environmental settings prior to the test. Each rat was then individually positioned at the center of an open arena (100 cm × 100 cm × 50 cm), oriented away from the experimenter. Animal movement paths were tracked for 5 min using Smart 3.0. Total distance and central zone distance were measured. Testing was performed under low-light and low-noise conditions. 2.4.2 Forced swim test (FST) The FST takes advantage of rodents’ natural aversion to swimming. Rats typically progress from vigorous activity to immobility, reflecting behavioral despair ( Armario, 2021 ). Each rat was placed alone in a cylindrical container that was 60 cm tall and 25 cm wide, with water filled to a depth of 30 cm at 25 °C. Smart 3.0 was used to automatically record and analyze the immobility time during the 5-min test. 2.4.3 Sucrose preference test (SPT) The SPT assesses anhedonia, a core symptom of depression ( Verharen et al., 2023 ). During training, individually housed rats acclimated to two 1 % sucrose bottles for 24 h. One bottle was substituted with water for 24 h, followed by 24 h of food and water deprivation. For testing, rats received one sucrose and one water bottle. Bottle positions were switched at 12 h. After 24 h, the bottles were weighed. Sucrose preference (%) = [( W 1 − W 3 ) / ( W 1 − W 3 + W 2 − W 4 )] × 100 % where W 1 and W 2 refer to the initial weights of the sucrose and water bottles, respectively, and W 3 and W 4 to the final weights of the sucrose and water bottles, respectively, at 24 h. 2.4.4 Elevated plus maze (EPM) test The EPM test quantifies rodent anxiety by exploiting the conflict between the exploratory drive and height aversion ( Knight et al., 2021 ). The setup included two open arms (30 cm × 7 cm) and two enclosed arms (30 cm × 7 cm × 15 cm height) interconnected by a central platform (7 cm × 7 cm), with the rats placed facing the open arms. Sessions of 5 min were video-recorded using Smart 3.0, followed immediately by cleaning using 75 % ethanol for 30 s. 2.5 Data-independent acquisition (DIA) quantitative proteomics Hippocampal tissue (150 mg) was pulverized in liquid nitrogen, lysed in SDT buffer, and centrifuged after sonication. Protein levels were determined using a BCA assay. For analysis, 15 μg aliquots were separated on SDS-PAGE gels and stained, while 150 μg samples underwent FASP digestion. Peptides were dried, reconstituted, quantified, and analyzed using Vanquish Neo UHPLC with DIA-MS, with data processed in DIA-NN for quantification. 2.6 Western blotting (WB) Hippocampal tissue (20 mg) was homogenized in 300 μL RIPA lysis buffer with inhibitors and beads. After 15 min on ice, centrifugation produced a supernatant. Protein concentration was determined via a BCA assay. Samples were denatured, run on SDS-PAGE gels, and transferred to PVDF membranes. Membranes were blocked, incubated with primary antibodies overnight at 4 °C, then with secondary antibodies for 1 h at 25 °C, and visualized using Image J. Antibody details are in Supplementary Table 1 . 2.7 Quantitative real-time PCR (qRT-PCR) analysis Hippocampal RNA was extracted from 20 mg tissue samples with TRIzol reagent and purified via chloroform-isopropanol purification. RNA concentration was assessed with a NanoDrop 2000. Subsequently, 1 μg RNA underwent reverse transcription (Yeason, Shenzhen, China) to generate cDNA. Then, qRT-PCR amplification was utilized with SYBR Green Master Mix (Yeason) under standard conditions. Primer sequences are provided in Supplementary Table 2 . 2.8 Hematoxylin-eosin (HE) staining Rat colonic tissues were fixed in 4 % paraformaldehyde (4 °C, 48 h). The tissues were subjected to graded ethanol dehydration, paraffin embedding, microtome sectioning, and HE staining for light microscopic analysis. Three independent investigators, blinded to group allocation, scored the sections based on epithelial integrity, inflammatory cell infiltration, and mucosal architecture using a four-point scale (0 = normal, 1 = mild, 2 = moderate, 3 = severe). 2.9 Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) Colonic tissue samples from rats were initially immersion-fixed in ice-cold 2.5 % glutaraldehyde solution (4 °C, 2 h). Subsequently, the samples were post-fixed with 1 % osmium tetroxide (1 h). Specimens were then processed through graded acetone dehydration, epoxy resin infiltration, and embedding. Ultrathin tissue sections (70 nm) were stained sequentially with uranyl acetate (15 min, 25 °C) followed by Reynolds’ lead citrate (2 min, 25 °C). Three independent investigators, blinded to group allocation, evaluated ultrastructural changes, including microvillus disruption and tight junction integrity, using the same four-point scale. 2.10 Metagenomics Genomic DNA was extracted using kits (Magigene, Shenzhen, China), and its integrity was assessed by agarose gel electrophoresis. The concentration and purity were measured spectrophotometrically and fluorometrically, respectively. Libraries were prepared by fragmentation, end repair, adaptor ligation, size selection, qRT-PCR amplification. Sequencing was performed on the Illumina NovaSeq platform using PE150 mode (San Diego, CA, USA). 2.11 Graphs and statistics Normality of data distribution was assessed using the Kolmogorov-Smirnov test. Statistical analyses were performed using GraphPad Prism 9.0.0. Depending on the experimental design, either two-way ANOVA or one-way ANOVA was applied, followed by Tukey's post hoc multiple comparisons test when appropriate. P < 0.05 was considered statistically significant.

[results] 3 Results 3.1 TaVNS improves CUMS-induced depression-like behavior Body weight was measured weekly. During the first 21 days of modeling, the Control group exhibited rapid weight gain, whereas the other groups displayed more gradual increases with no significant pairwise differences among them ( p > 0.05). Post-intervention, the CUMS group displayed a significantly lower body mass than the Control group ( p < 0.001), whereas taVNS treatment markedly restored body weight compared with the CUMS group ( p < 0.001) ( Fig. 1 C). The OFT ( Fig. 1 D and E) revealed deficiencies in spatial exploration in the CUMS group, which exhibited lower total distance traveled ( p < 0.001) and less center movement ( p < 0.001) than the Control group. These deficiencies were notably ameliorated following taVNS treatment ( p < 0.001). In the FST, immobility time is proportional to the degree of despair in depressed rats ( Zhang et al., 2022 ). Rats subjected to taVNS intervention had shorter immobility times than the CUMS group ( p < 0.001) ( Fig. 1 F). The SPT results ( Fig. 1 G) demonstrated that both taVNS ( p < 0.001) and fluoxetine ( p < 0.01) significantly alleviated CUMS-induced anhedonia. Finally, in the EPM test ( Fig. 1 H), the CUMS group exhibited fewer open-arm entries than the other three groups. TaVNS effectively reversed this ( p < 0.001), indicating mitigation of anxiety-like behavior. The representative movement trajectories from the OFT and EPM test are presented in Fig. 1 I. Notably, across all behavioral assessments, the CUMS + taVNS group exhibited a more pronounced improvement in depressive-like behaviors compared with the CUMS + Flu group. 3.2 TaVNS alters hippocampal protein expression Proteomic analysis identified 95,643 peptides and 10,971 proteins ( Supplementary Fig. 1 ). Principal component analysis (PCA) revealed significant segregation between the Control and CUMS groups, whereas the CUMS + taVNS group exhibited convergence toward the control cluster ( Fig. 2 A). Differentially expressed proteins (DEPs) were identified using thresholds of fold change (FC) ≥ 1.2 for upregulation or FC ≤ 0.83 for downregulation, with statistical significance defined at p < 0.05. Relative to the Control group, the CUMS group exhibited 931 DEPs, with 595 upregulated and 336 downregulated ( Fig. 2 B and C). In contrast, the CUMS + taVNS group exhibited 633 DEPs relative to the CUMS group, with 276 upregulated and 357 downregulated. An intersection of these two sets of DEPs was obtained, and the top 50 proteins that exhibited consistent trends in the Control and CUMS + taVNS groups but opposite trends in the CUMS group were selected for cluster analysis ( Fig. 2 D), suggesting taVNS's antidepressant effects primarily via these reversibly regulated proteins. Functional annotation utilized the Gene Ontology (GO) and Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG) databases. GO analysis outcomes ( Fig. 2 E) revealed DEPs enrichment in: vesicular transport, neuronal differentiation, and synaptic signaling (Biological Process); synaptic and neuronal projection localization (Cellular Component); gated channel activity, ionotropic glutamate receptor activity, and cation channels (Molecular Function). KEGG analysis indicated that the DEPs were primarily implicated in pathways related to endothelial cell proliferation, glutamatergic synapses, neurodegeneration, and GABAergic synapses ( Fig. 2 F). Fig. 2 Effects of transcutaneous auricular vagus nerve stimulation (taVNS) on hippocampal proteins in chronic unpredictable mild stress (CUMS) depression model rats. (A) Principal component analysis (PCA). (B) Volcano plot (Control vs. CUMS). (C) Volcano plot (CUMS + taVNS vs. CUMS). (D) Heatmap showing the top 50 differentially expressed proteins (DEPs) among the three groups. (E) Gene Ontology (GO) analysis of DEPs. (F) Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG) analysis of DEPs. ( n = 4 per group). Fig. 2 3.3 TaVNS may exert antidepressant effects through the GluN1/MAPK/BDNF pathway DIA-based quantitative proteomics revealed that taVNS exerts antidepressant effects primarily via signaling cascades, notably the MAPK pathway and glutamatergic synapses. In MDD, augmented levels of pro-inflammatory cytokines, including interleukin-1 beta (IL-1β) and tumor necrosis factor-alpha (TNF-α), directly impair GluN1 expression and selectively activate stress-responsive MAPK branches, specifically those involving phosphorylated JNK (P-JNK), phosphorylated ERK (P-ERK), and phosphorylated P38 (P-P38). This pathological skewing of MAPK signaling disrupts the CREB-BDNF-ERK axis. Therefore, we initially quantified the expression of the pertinent proteins via WB. Relative to the Control group, the CUMS group exhibited significant downregulation of GluN1, BDNF, and P-ERK proteins in the hippocampus ( p < 0.05), accompanied by a notable upregulation of P-JNK ( p < 0.05), IL-1β ( p < 0.05), and P-P38 ( p < 0.001) ( Fig. 3 A–F). Intriguingly, taVNS treatment markedly ameliorated these alterations, consistent with the proteomic insights derived from DIA analysis. Based on the WB results, the CUMS + Flu and CUMS + taVNS groups exhibited a clear reversal trend across all indicators except for P-JNK, with the CUMS + taVNS group demonstrating significantly greater BDNF activation than the CUMS + Flu group ( p < 0.05). Furthermore, qRT-PCR analysis confirmed that taVNS intervention reversed the CUMS-induced decrease in GRIN1 ( p < 0.01) and BDNF ( p < 0.05) mRNA levels ( Fig. 3 G and H), as well as in Creb mRNA ( p < 0.01), which integrates glutamatergic inputs with the MAPK pathway ( Fig. 3 I). In addition, taVNS significantly diminished the mRNA expression of TNF-α ( p < 0.01), IL-1β ( p < 0.05), and P38 ( p < 0.05) ( Fig. 3 J–L). These findings underscore the modulatory role of taVNS in normalizing the dysregulated molecular cascades associated with depressive phenotypes. Fig. 3 Transcutaneous auricular vagus nerve stimulation (taVNS) treatment ameliorates neurological disorders via the GluN1/MAPK/BDNF pathway in chronic unpredictable mild stress (CUMS) depression model rats. (A) Protein expression of GluN1. (B) Protein expression of BDNF. (C) Protein expression of P-ERK. (D) Protein expression of P-JNK. (E) Protein expression of P-P38. (F) Protein expression of IL-1β. (G) qPCR analysis of GRIN1. (H) qPCR analysis of BDNF. (I) qPCR analysis of Creb. (J) qPCR analysis of TNF-α. (K) qPCR analysis of P38. (L) qPCR analysis of IL-1β. ( n = 4 per group for Western blot and n = 3 per group for qPCR; * p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01, *** p < 0.001). Fig. 3 3.4 TaVNS alleviates CUMS-induced gut microbiota dysbiosis Research on the MGB axis in MDD therapy has gained prominence. Therefore, to understand the changes in intestinal homeostasis in MDD, we performed HE staining and TEM. In comparison with the Control group, the CUMS group showed notable pathological changes, such as inflammation, abnormal proliferation, and disordered arrangement, broken mucosal epithelium, blurred tight junctions, and sparse microvilli ( Fig. 4 A). The taVNS intervention reversed the negative effects of CUMS modeling on the intestinal tract. Fig. 4 Alterations in intestinal microorganisms in chronic unpredictable mild stress (CUMS) depression model rats after transcutaneous auricular vagus nerve stimulation (taVNS) intervention. (A) Hematoxylin and Eosin staining and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) of the colonic tissue from each group. (B) Alpha diversity (Chao1 index). (C) Principal coordinate analysis (PCoA). (D) Venn diagram, showing common and unique microbial species between groups. (E) Ternary phase diagram. (F, G) Linear discriminant analysis effect size (LEfSe) analysis (LDA score >2.0 and p < 0.05) (Control vs. CUMS vs. CUMS + taVNS). (H) Compositional analysis of the top 10 species in each group at the phylum and species levels. (I) The top 15 microorganisms in each group that were notably different at the species level. ( n = 3 per group; * p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01, *** p < 0.001). Fig. 4 To further examine the specific gut microbial changes after taVNS intervention, we performed metagenomic analysis of intracolonic fecal samples. In the analysis of alpha diversity, the Chao1 index is often used to study the unobserved species richness of communities. No notable differences were observed among the groups ( p > 0.05) ( Fig. 4 B). UniFrac-based principal coordinates analysis (PCoA), to assess differences between samples and clustering patterns, revealed significant differences in gut microbial composition between the Control and CUMS groups. The gut microbiota of taVNS-treated rats showed a clear trend of regression ( Fig. 4 C). Venn diagrams revealed greater bacterial abundance in CUMS + taVNS versus CUMS groups ( Fig. 4 D). The relative abundances of several key microorganisms in each group are presented in a ternary plot ( Fig. 4 E). The results of the evolutionary tree generated from LEfSe analysis identified eight and three differentially abundant clades (family level) in the CUMS + taVNS and CUMS groups respectively ( P < 0.05, LDA >2.0) ( Fig. 4 F and G). Species composition histograms, used to analyze the specific dissimilarity of the microbial communities between the samples, revealed that at the phylum level, taVNS intervention increased the abundance of the Firmicutes phylum; at the species level, it markedly increased the relative abundances of Ligilactobacillus murinus and Akkermansia muciniphila , while markedly reducing those of Bacteroides ndongoniae and Limosilactobacillus reuteri ( Fig. 4 H). The specific differences among groups are illustrated in Fig. 4 I. 3.5 Correlation analysis of key differentially expressed hippocampal proteins and key differential intestinal microorganisms To explore the potential mechanisms of action of the MGB axis, we conducted correlation analyses. In Mantel tests, we selected the four significantly differential bacterial genera for preliminary analysis with the key differential hippocampal proteins: Ligilactobacillus was notably correlated with Pfn2 levels, Bacteroides with Syt1, ATP8, and Glud1 levels, Prevotella with Cox5b levels, and Akkermansia with Syngr1 and Prdx1 levels. Protein-protein correlation analysis revealed significant associations among several hippocampal proteins, suggesting their potential involvement in interconnected biological processes. Specifically, Glud1 showed a strong positive correlation with Syt1, implying that Glud1 may participate in the coordinated regulation of glutamate metabolism and synaptic plasticity ( Fig. 5 A). To further elucidate the underlying relationship patterns, we performed canonical correlation analysis (CCA). The results showed that Syngrl displayed the most prominent correlation with bacterial abundance, being negatively correlated with species diversity in the CUMS group and positively correlated with it in the Control group; notably, taVNS reversed the negative correlation between Syngr1 expression and bacterial abundance ( Fig. 5 B). The correlation heatmap further illustrated the relationship between the key DEPs and the differential flora. Consistent with the CCA results, Syngr1 expression, which had a significant positive effect on bacterial abundance in the Control group, was also significantly positively correlated with Ligilactobacillus abundance ( Fig. 5 C). Fig. 5 Correlation analysis of hippocampal differentially expressed proteins (DEPs) and differential intestinal microorganisms. (A) Mantel test analysis of hippocampal DEPs and gut differential microorganisms at the genus level. (B) Redundancy Analysis (RDA) and Canonical Correspondence Analysis (CCA) to confirm the correlation between hippocampal DEPs and differential intestinal microorganisms ( n = 3 per group); acute angle: positive correlation; obtuse angle: negative correlation; right angle: no correlation. (C) Heatmap of Spearman correlations between key differential proteins and key differential microorganisms at the genus level. Fig. 5

[discussion] 4 Discussion Substantial evidence indicates that the pathophysiological mechanisms of MDD involve immune-inflammatory activation, disturbances in monoaminergic neurotransmitter homeostasis, neuroplastic impairments within limbic circuits, and structural abnormalities in the prefrontal cortex-hippocampal axis ( Page et al., 2024 ; Wang et al., 2025 ). These mechanisms are modulated by VNS, which exerts anti-inflammatory effects via neuroimmune modulation and enhances blood-brain barrier integrity through cholinergic pathways, demonstrating favorable clinical efficacy in treatment-resistant depression ( Wang et al., 2025 ). Despite its therapeutic potential, traditional VNS is invasive and may lead to complications such as surgical hemorrhage, infection, nerve injury, and cardiac arrhythmias, thus limiting its clinical application ( Alexander et al., 2017 ). TaVNS overcomes these limitations by stimulating the auricular concha, sending impulses to the nucleus tractus solitarius with ascending projections to key brain regions implicated in depression. This approach achieves VNS-comparable therapeutic effects while avoiding surgical morbidity ( Lange et al., 2011 ). In addition to elucidating the relevant neuroanatomical pathways, studies have confirmed the VN modulation of glutamatergic systems and MAPK-mediated anti-inflammatory effects ( Long et al., 2022 ; Pal, 2021 ), suggesting a probable mechanistic link whereby taVNS exerts antidepressant effects by modulating the GluN1/MAPK/BDNF cascade. Given the role of the VN in gut-brain signaling, we concurrently assessed changes in the gut microbiota. To directly test this molecular hypothesis, we employed the CUMS model to recapitulate the core pathophysiological features of human depression, including chronic stress-induced pathogenesis. This multidimensional integration of behavioral, proteomic, and molecular evidence provides insight into the therapeutic mechanisms of taVNS. The CUMS-induced rats developed depression- and anxiety-like phenotypes. Behavioral profiling via body mass monitoring, FST, OFT, EPM, and SPT confirmed the successful establishment of the model. TaVNS intervention considerably increased sucrose preference, reduced FST immobility time, increased central zone distance in the OFT, and enhanced open-arm exploration in the EPM. These findings demonstrated that taVNS can significantly ameliorate depression-related behaviors in CUMS rats. To elucidate the dynamic proteomic networks underlying the antidepressant mechanisms of taVNS, we performed DIA-based quantitative proteomic profiling. Functional enrichment analyses (GO/KEGG) indicated that taVNS primarily modulates glutamatergic synaptic function and promotes neuronal viability. Given that glutamatergic signaling plays a central role in neurotrophic factor release and neuroplasticity, it has emerged as a promising therapeutic target for depression ( Hasin et al., 2018 ). Dysregulation of glutamatergic transmission contributes to neuropsychiatric disorders, including MDD, Parkinson's disease, and Alzheimer's disease ( Vanle et al., 2018 ). Cross-validation of KEGG-based DEPs was performed through integrated WB and qRT-PCR analyses. CUMS markedly downregulated GluN1 expression in the hippocampus, and taVNS treatment reversed this effect. GRIN1 encodes the obligatory GluN1 subunit of the NMDAR complex ( Wu et al., 2018 ), and conditional GRIN1 deficiency impairs GluN1-mediated Ca 2+ influx and downstream signaling ( Venkatesan et al., 2023 ). Functional NMDAR channels are essential for higher-order cognitive processes, including learning and memory ( Wu et al., 2011 ). Neuropsychiatric disorders induce NMDAR overactivation, triggering excitotoxic neuronal death and is frequently accompanied by reduced GRIN1 expression ( Ding et al., 2017 ). Our findings are consistent with previous findings indicating that taVNS counteracts NMDAR dysfunction through both anti-excitotoxic and anti-inflammatory mechanisms. Specifically, α7nAChR activation on microglia suppresses NF-κB translocation ( Terrando et al., 2014 ), reducing the levels of proinflammatory cytokines that exacerbate NMDAR excitotoxicity. In parallel, taVNS-induced noradrenergic potentiation within the locus coeruleus can diminish prefrontal glutamatergic hyperactivity ( Kong et al., 2018 ). The restoration of physiological Ca 2+ influx through functional NMDARs subsequently activates CaMKII, promoting Ras-GTP formation and initiating MAPK/ERK signaling ( Thomas and Huganir, 2004 ). Converging evidence implicates the MAPK signaling cascade in the bidirectional modulation of neuroplasticity and apoptotic processes ( Carrier and Kabbaj, 2012 ). As a stress-responsive pathway, MAPK integrates inflammatory cues and cellular stress signals, shaping neuronal differentiation, survival, and synaptic remodeling; these processes are fundamental to affective regulation and cognitive function ( Cargnello and Roux, 2011 ). Building upon this mechanistic foundation, we revealed pathway-specific MAPK modulation in taVNS-treated rats, characterized by downregulation of P-P38 and P-JNK phosphorylation and upregulation of P-ERK in the hippocampus. These results indicate that taVNS efficacy involves re-equilibration of pro-survival (ERK) versus pro-apoptotic (P38/JNK) signaling rather than activation of an isolated pathway. In further comparative analysis, we found that both taVNS and fluoxetine ameliorated CUMS-induced depressive-like alterations, as confirmed by behavioral evaluations. However, taVNS showed a stronger effect on upregulating BDNF, suggesting that taVNS had a greater efficacy in activating signals related to neuroplasticity. Although similar restorative trends were observed for P-ERK, GluN1, P-JNK, P-P38, and IL-1β, the differences between taVNS and fluoxetine did not reach statistical significance. Notably, taVNS could rapidly increase hippocampal BDNF levels within 14 days. This rapid antidepressant effect may be due to the phosphorylation of CREB induced by MAPK pathway activation, which in turn enhances BDNF transcriptional expression and promotes neuronal survival, dendritic branch formation, and synaptic remodeling ( Ahuja et al., 2021 ). A study utilizing two-photon fluorescence imaging revealed the role of glutamate receptors as potential upstream regulators of BDNF-TrkB signaling, thereby contributing to BDNF release ( Harward et al., 2016 ). Collectively, these findings suggest that taVNS directly targets neurotrophic pathways independent of the monoaminergic system. TaVNS-induced hippocampal GluN1 upregulation activates MAPK signaling, downregulates P-P38/P-JNK phosphorylation, and upregulates P-ERK. The concurrent upregulation of BDNF further supports this antidepressant mechanism. Accumulating evidence implicates the MGB axis in the pathogenesis of MDD. The VN employs afferent fibers to relay gut microbiota-derived signals through enteroendocrine cells, thereby regulating intestinal homeostasis. Extensive visceral innervation underlies this bidirectional gut-brain communication ( Pan et al., 2024 ). A recent clinical research on MDD indicated that multi-strain probiotics exert antidepressant effects primarily via VNS mediation ( Moerkl et al., 2025 ). Current evidence focuses primarily on the application of taVNS in gastrointestinal pathologies ( Atalar et al., 2025 ). However, there is limited scientific evidence regarding taVNS-mediated gut modulation in MDD. Here, using HE staining and TEM, we observed that taVNS positively affects intestinal homeostasis. To specifically address the mechanisms of taVNS in MDD, we utilized metagenomic sequencing to investigate alterations in the gut microbiota induced by CUMS and taVNS. In terms of alpha diversity, the CUMS group exhibited an elevated Chao1 index, reduced Simpson's index, and higher microbial richness than the Control group, an apparent paradox potentially linked to the significant depletion of protective bacteria, while opportunistic bacteria expanded ( Montgomery et al., 2024 ). Analysis of species differences among the three groups revealed a marked elevation in Firmicutes and a substantial reduction in Bacteroidetes members in the CUMS + taVNS group relative to the CUMS group, with Ligilactobacillus murinus , Bacteroides ndongoniae , Limosilactobacillus reuteri , Lactobacillus johnsonii , and Akkermansia muciniphila displaying the most significant declines. Critically, Limosilactobacillus reuteri colonization reduces the abundance of SCFA-producing microbiota and associated metabolites ( Montgomery et al., 2024 ). These results elucidate the nature of microbiotic dysbiosis in depression, particularly within the MGB axis. Ligilactobacillus murinus potently alleviates intestinal inflammation via immunomodulation of the TLR2/IL-10 pathway, enhancing intestinal barrier integrity and restoring gut homeostasis ( Hu et al., 2022 ). Notably, Limosilactobacillus reuteri displays bifunctional characteristics; its elevation induces ceramide accumulation, causing hippocampal mitochondrial impairment ( Wang et al., 2024 , Wang et al., 2024 ); conversely, it interacts synergistically with galactooligosaccharides to promote intestinal health ( Wu et al., 2024 ). Here, Limosilactobacillus reuteri was less abundant in the CUMS + taVNS group than in the CUMS group. Bacteroides ndongoniae was more abundant in the gut microbiota of patients with MDD, contributing to cognitive deficits via microglial activation ( Xia et al., 2023 ). Importantly, both Lactobacillus johnsonii and Akkermansia muciniphila have shown therapeutic promise for depression in recent years ( Zheng et al., 2025 ). Here, after taVNS intervention, Lactobacillus johnsonii abundance was markedly reduced, but Akkermansia muciniphila abundance was elevated relative to the CUMS group, potentially owing to the mucin-derived propionic acid production by Akkermansia muciniphila , which induces intestinal acidification below that tolerated by Lactobacillus johnsonii , resulting in compromised colonization capacity. The viability of Lactobacillus johnsonii declines with increasing acidity ( Ioannou et al., 2025 ; K. Wang et al., 2024 ). Simultaneously, owing to interspecies competition within microbial communities, the excessive proliferation of Akkermansia muciniphila may deprive Lactobacillus johnsonii of nutrients via competition for mucin ( Montgomery et al., 2024 ). Such microbial restructuring may perturb synaptic protein expression by vagal afferent fibers, necessitating neuroproteomic profiling. In this integrated multi-omics study, we identified significant associations between the gut microbiota and neural proteins, suggesting that these interactions may be related to the antidepressant effects of taVNS. Compared with the CUMS group, the correlations between Syngr1 and microbial taxa were strongest in the Control and CUMS + taVNS groups, implying that Syngr1 may serve as a key associative node within the MGB axis and be closely linked to the antidepressant action of taVNS. Further analysis revealed a strong positive correlation between Syngr1 expression and the abundance of Ligilactobacillus, indicating that this genus may participate in processes associated with synaptic homeostasis and antidepressant effects. Nevertheless, the potential mechanisms underlying these associations remain to be explored. The results offer integrated evidence for the multi-tiered mechanisms underlying taVNS-mediated amelioration of MDD. Behaviorally, taVNS alleviated the CUMS-induced depressive-like phenotype. Neuroplastically, it effectively maintained MAPK signaling homeostasis and promotes BDNF release by restoring glutamatergic synapse functionality. Regarding intestinal ecology, taVNS promotes the abundance of beneficial taxa ( Ligilactobacillus murinus and Akkermansia muciniphila ) to rebalance the microbiota. Microbial restructuring attenuates intestinal inflammation and enhances barrier integrity. Notably, correlation analysis revealed a strong link between Bacteroides abundance and Sty1/Glud1 levels, suggesting a potential gut-synapse signaling connection implicated in the pathogenesis of depression. Previous studies have demonstrated that Bacteroides species produce neuroactive metabolites that directly regulate Syt1-mediated vesicular exocytosis of neurotransmitters such as BDNF to establish a microbial metabolic pathway that coordinates presynaptic function and glutamate homeostasis ( Zhao et al., 2024 ). Consistent with emerging evidence, Ligilactobacillus reuteri modulates hippocampal synaptic gene expression via vagal afferent signaling ( Tofani et al., 2025 ). Within this regulatory network, Syngr1 critically regulates synaptic vesicle endocytosis. Correlation analysis further supports this concept by showing a significant positive association between Syngr1 abundance and Ligilactobacillus levels. Collectively, these findings suggest that microbial metabolites may be associated with the activity of hippocampal GluN1/MAPK/BDNF signaling, providing new insights into the potential mechanisms underlying the antidepressant effects of VNS. Despite these insights, several limitations should be acknowledged. First, only male rats were used to avoid additional variation due to hormonal cycles in female rats; however, sex may influence susceptibility to mood disorders and treatment responses. Second, neurobiological analyses were limited to the hippocampus, without examining other emotion-related regions such as the prefrontal cortex. Finally, although we integrated metagenomic and proteomic data, microbial gene abundance does not necessarily correlate linearly with the corresponding protein levels. Therefore, the association between changes in gut microbiota and hippocampal GluN1/MAPK/BDNF signaling is only correlational, and it is not possible to determine whether there is a causal relationship. Overall, future studies should include female rats to assess sex differences and extend neurobiological analyses to brain regions beyond the hippocampus to more comprehensively characterize the effects of taVNS on neural circuits. In addition, further mechanistic studies are needed to determine whether alterations in gut microbiota directly influence GluN1/MAPK/BDNF signaling, and clinical research will be essential to validate these mechanisms and elucidate the antidepressant effects of taVNS.

[conclusion] 5 Conclusions We integrated behavioral assessments, metagenomics, proteomics, and molecular pathology techniques to evaluate the therapeutic effects of taVNS in a CUMS rat model. Results demonstrated that taVNS markedly improved depression- and anxiety-like behaviors in CUMS-exposed rats. Specifically, taVNS upregulated hippocampal GluN1, activated the MAPK/ERK signaling pathway, and downregulated the phosphorylation of P38 and JNK, thereby promoting BDNF release and restoring glutamatergic synaptic function and neural plasticity. Meanwhile, gut microbiota analysis revealed that taVNS increased the abundance of Akkermansia muciniphila and Ligilactobacillus murinus while reducing Limosilactobacillus reuteri and Lactobacillus johnsonii .
