Future studies using single nucleus RNAseq or high-resolution microscopy mRNA and protein analysis will provide more insight into the cell-type specific alterations in the transcriptomic pathways identified in our study. The information available for our cohort did not allow for analysis of potential relationships of mania, depression, and euthymia at death on our outcome measures. Future studies using larger cohorts with more detailed patient histories may shed light on the specific effects that mood state may have on metabolic pathways and ATPase signaling. In summary, our results identify diagnosis-specific alterations in metabolic pathways, immune pathways, and calcium ion transport in the amygdala of subjects with SCZ, BPD, and MDD. These results suggest that the amygdala is a region where alterations in these pathways occur in a disease-specific manner, potentially associated with broader metabolic and immune system dysfunction that is often comorbid with these disorders. Identifying disease-specific alterations may guide the development and application of metabolic-based therapeutic strategies for SCZ, BPD, and MDD.

[conclusion] Conclusion. Our findings suggest metabolic pathways are differentially altered in the amygdala in these disorders, which may impact approaches for therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_papers.title: Low glial numbers in the amygdala in major depressive disorder.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2002]
[Abstract] Functional imaging studies implicate the prefrontal cortex and amygdala in major depressive disorder and bipolar disorder, and glial decreases have been reported in the prefrontal cortex. Here, glia and neurons were counted in the amygdala and entorhinal cortex in major depressive disorder, bipolar disorder, and control cases.
Tissue blocks from major depressive disorder (7), bipolar disorder (10), and control (12) cases, equally divided between right and left, were cut into 50 microm sections and stained with the Nissl method. One major depressive disorder and all but two bipolar disorder cases had been treated with lithium or valproate. Neurons and glia were counted using stereological methods.
Glial density and the glia/neuron ratio were substantially reduced in the amygdala in major depressive disorder cases. The reduction was mainly accounted for by counts in the left hemisphere. No change was found in neurons. Average glia measures were not reduced in bipolar disorder cases; however, bipolar disorder cases not treated with lithium or valproate had significant glial reduction. Similar but smaller changes were found in the entorhinal cortex.
Glia are reduced in the amygdala in major depressive disorder, especially on the left side. The results suggest that lithium and valproate may moderate the glial reduction.

[Introduction] 1 INTRODUCTION Major depressive disorder (MDD) is a debilitating psychiatric condition characterized by persistent low mood, anhedonia, and cognitive impairments such as difficulty concentrating, indecisiveness, and memory deficits. According to the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders Fifth Edition (DSM‐5), MDD is diagnosed when symptoms persist for at least 2 weeks and significantly impair social and occupational areas of functioning. 1 , 2 MDD is one of the leading causes of disability worldwide, affecting over 280 million people across all age groups and regions. 3 The prevalence of MDD is higher in women than in men, with hormonal, psychosocial, and genetic factors contributing to this disparity. 4 The economic burden of MDD is substantial, with annual costs in the United States alone exceeding $326 billion, driven by healthcare expenditures, workplace costs and suicide‐related costs. 5 Similar trends have been observed globally. MDD has also been shown to frequently comorbid with systemic diseases such as diabetes and cardiovascular disorders (CVD), creating a bidirectional relationship where each condition exacerbates the other. For instance, individuals with diabetes have a higher prevalence of comorbid MDD, estimated at 11%, while those with CVD have a prevalence ranging from 15% to 20%. 6 Likewise, research has shown that individuals with MDD are at a higher risk of developing type 2 diabetes, while those with diabetes are twice as likely to experience depressive symptoms. 7 This comorbidity makes the management of comorbid conditions difficult and contributes to the high societal burden of MDD. The role of neuroinflammation in the development of MDD, particularly in the context of old age, stress, brain injury, and the pathophysiology of MDD comorbid with systemic diseases, has garnered significant attention over the last decade. Research has shown that the etiology of neuroinflammation is multifactorial, with conditions such as chronic stress, medical illnesses (e.g., heart failure and stroke), and metabolic disorders like diabetes and obesity being strongly correlated with chronic neuroinflammation and the subsequent onset of depression. 8 This has led to the understanding that neuroinflammation serves as a common pathway linking MDD and systemic diseases, which, in turn, contributes to the challenges of managing MDD in individuals with comorbid conditions. Several immune factors, such as brain glial cells, 9 , 10 , 11 , 12 , 13 peripheral T‐cells, 14 , 15 , 16 , 17 , 18 cytokines, 15 , 19 , 20 chemokines, 21 , 22 complement system, 23 , 24 and mitogen‐activated protein kinase, 25 , 26 have been implicated in the pathophysiology of neuroinflammation. The activation and overexpression of astrocytes and microglia are primary contributors to neuroinflammation in the central nervous system (CNS), leading to impaired neurogenesis and subsequent loss of brain functions. In response to inflammatory stimuli, such as pathogen‐associated molecular patterns (PAMPs) or damage‐associated molecular patterns (DAMPs), astrocytes and microglia undergo morphological and functional changes, including the upregulation of pro‐inflammatory cytokines (e.g., interleukin [IL]‐1β, tumor necrocsis factor [TNF]‐α, IL‐6) and chemokines (e.g., C‐C motif ligand [CCL]2, CCL5). 27 These inflammatory mediators disrupt neuronal homeostasis, impair synaptic plasticity, and contribute to the degeneration of neural circuits, ultimately leading to cognitive and behavioral deficits observed in neuropsychiatric disorders such as MDD. In a systematic review published a few years ago, one of the authors on this review evaluated the role of microglia in neuroinflammation and its subsequent link to depression. 9 Microglia, the resident immune cells of the CNS, play a crucial role in initiating inflammatory responses by releasing pro‐inflammatory cytokines and chemokines. 28 In contrast, astrocytes are essential for modulating neuroinflammatory responses and maintaining neuronal homeostasis. They regulate synaptic transmission and play a pivotal role in the formation of new memories, long‐term potentiation (LTP), and functional synaptic plasticity. 29 Additionally, astrocytes provide trophic and metabolic support to neurons, contributing to ionic homeostasis and overall brain function. 30 The interplay between microglia and astrocytes is complex and significant in the context of neuroinflammation and neurodegeneration. Historically, astrocytes were believed to primarily provide mechanical and trophic support to neurons. 31 However, over time, it became evident that astrocytes play more significant roles in CNS activities, including neural growth and development, 30 , 32 , 33 neural function, 34 , 35 neural protection, 36 , 37 and neurotransmission and synaptic plasticity. 38 , 39 , 40 , 41 Subsequent research revealed that astrocytes synthesize and release trophic factors such as brain‐derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF), fibroblast growth factor 2 (FGF‐2), insulin‐like growth factor 1 (IGF‐1), and glial cell line‐derived neurotrophic factor (GDNF), which support neuronal survival, neurite outgrowth, and synaptogenesis. 42 Under pathological conditions, astrocytes shift from their neuroprotective roles to promoting neuroinflammation by upregulating the expression and secretion of pro‐inflammatory cytokines (e.g., IL‐1β, IL‐6, and TNF‐α and chemokines (e.g., CCL2 and CCL5). 43 , 44 , 45 , 46 These pro‐inflammatory molecules attract leukocytes to the site of injury or attack 47 and amplify microglial inflammatory responses, leading to the production of neurotoxic mediators, 48 and creating a pro‐inflammatory environment in the CNS. This inflammatory response can compromise the integrity of tight junctions in the blood‐brain barrier (BBB), further exacerbating neuronal degeneration and the inflammatory milieu in the brain. This shift in understanding marked a turning point in astrocyte research, as it became clear that astrocytes respond to both endogenous and exogenous signals, such as DAMPs and PAMPs. This dual responsiveness implicates astrocytes in the pathophysiology of neuroinflammation‐induced depression. 49 Moreover, the development of depressive symptoms was found to be brain‐region specific, 50 suggesting that changes in localized astrocytic activity, rather than total astrocytic responses, may be critical in the progression of depression. The versatility of astrocyte functions and their involvement in immune signaling pathways during neuroinflammation has fueled interest in developing safe and effective astrocyte‐targeted treatments for depression and other psychiatric disorders. 51 This review aims to elucidate the emerging role of astrocytes in neuroinflammation and their involvement in the pathophysiology of MDD. We discuss the dual role of astrocytes in neuroprotection and neurotoxicity, their interactions with microglia, and their involvement in BBB regulation. Methodological challenges, including discrepancies between animal and human studies and sample variations, are addressed, highlighting the need for standardized research approaches. We further explore therapeutic strategies targeting astrocyte‐mediated inflammation, including anti‐inflammatory agents, metabolic modulators, and noninvasive brain stimulation techniques, along with ongoing clinical trials on TNF‐α inhibitors and NOD‐like receptor thermal protein domain associated protein (NLRP3) inflammasome inhibitors. Understanding astrocyte‐driven neuroinflammation may provide novel avenues for more effective depression treatments, particularly in patients with inflammatory comorbidities.

[methods] 2 METHODS The review is written following the guidelines prescribed by Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta‐analyses (PRISMA). 52 , 53 , 54 , 55 2.1 PRISMA criteria PRISMA's checklist items applicable to this analysis were included, such as those related to search and writing strategies, while items not relevant, such as those related to meta‐analyses, were omitted. 2.2 Search and selection process A comprehensive analysis of PubMed and Google Scholar online repositories up until February 2025 using various keywords in different combinations and permutations was performed. Some of the key words included, but not limited to, in our search were astrocytes, glia, neuroinflammation, depression, anxiety, cytokines, IL‐1, TNF, cellular, humoral, immune, aging, cognition, behavior, psychosocial stress, brain injury, PAMPs, and DAMPs. Titles and abstracts were reviewed at each stage of the search, and the most relevant articles were organized into separate folders using EndNote 20 software (Clarivate, Philadelphia, PA, USA). Additionally, the reference lists of other relevant publications are evaluated to retrieve articles pertinent to our discussion on each subtopic. This process yielded a total of 273 papers for initial review. After applying our inclusion and exclusion criteria (as depicted in Figure 1 ), 226 articles closely related to the aims set forth for this review were selected and utilized. Figure 1 Study inclusion flowchart. Methodology for search and collection of relevant articles for this review, following PRISMA guidelines. PRISMA, the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic reviews and Meta‐Analyses. 2.3 Inclusion and exclusion criteria Articles detailing the role of astrocytes in the brain and their mechanisms in neuroinflammation are selected for a comprehensive analysis. Specifically, the impact of aging, psychosocial stress, and brain injury on astrocytic behavior is examined. Additionally, supporting non‐English publications dating back to 1836 were included to provide a complete historical timeline of progress in understanding the morphology, heterogeneity, and functions of astrocytes. Articles lacking accessible full texts and those with anecdotal evidence were excluded at the time of writing this review. 2.4 Depression and mental health Depression is a psychiatric syndrome characterized by significant psychophysiological changes, manifesting as behaviors such as low mood, a pervasive sense of lack of control, irritability, and a loss of interest in day‐to‐day activities. 1 , 2 MDD is the clinical form of this condition, and its diagnosis is guided by the criteria outlined in the DSM‐5 (see Table 1 ). 2 , 56 The economic burden of MDD on public health systems has increased dramatically over the past two decades. In the United States alone, the economic burden among adults rose from $236 billion in 2010 to $326 billion in 2018. 5 Table 1 Diagnostic criteria for major depressive disorder as per the fifth edition of the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders. 1 , 2 Diagnostic criteria (1 + 2) Items 1. Specific symptoms Loss of interest or pleasure in day‐to‐day activities, tense and agitated, unpleasant thoughts, impaired social, occupational, educational functions for more than 2 weeks 2. At least 5 of the following 9 must be positive for the diagnosis of major depressive disorder Feeling sad/empty/tearful most of the day, every day Loss of interest in most daily activities most of each day >5% loss of weight Insomnia/hypersomnia Psychomotor agitation/retardation Feeling tired most of the time Feeling of inappropriate guilt/worthlessness Unable to concentrate/indecisiveness Thoughts/plan of committing suicide John Wiley & Sons, Ltd. Depression also contributes to a high incidence of suicide, 57 , 58 and increases the likelihood of life‐threatening metabolic diseases, such as type II diabetes and CVD. 59 , 60 , 61 The clinical course of depression is complex, often involving extended periods of remission interspersed with depressive episodes. In some cases, these episodes become increasingly frequent, leading to severe and persistent deterioration. Repeated observations suggest that the complexity of depression may be linked to underlying inflammatory processes. However, it is important to note that with appropriate treatment, 70%–80% of individuals with MDD experience significant improvement in their condition. 57 2.5 Pathophysiology of depression Depression is influenced by a complex interplay of risk factors that include social and behavioral components such as substance addiction, lack of family support, marital problems, low socioeconomic status, unemployment, and stressful life experiences. In addition to these external factors, individual triggers like developmental disorders and inherited structural defects also contribute to the onset and progression of depressive symptoms. These factors not only increase the likelihood of developing depression but also shape the nature and severity of its manifestations. For example, the first episode of depression is typically severe, often precipitated by significant external psychosocial stressors, whereas subsequent episodes may emerge more gradually, driven by moderate, endogenous internal stressors. Magnetic resonance imaging studies have provided insights into the neurobiological changes underlying depression, revealing a close link between these mechanisms and cellular‐level anatomical changes. 62 , 63 , 64 Notable among these structural changes are decreased volumes in critical brain regions, including the frontal and temporal lobes and the hippocampus, alongside increased ventricle volumes relative to overall brain size. 65 , 66 These anatomical changes are closely linked to the cellular‐level mechanisms of depression, including neuroinflammation and neurodegeneration. Furthermore, there is growing evidence of a correlation between these observable pathological changes and biochemical alterations in the brains of depressed patients. 67 Neurotransmitter imbalances, particularly elevated levels of serotonin, norepinephrine, dopamine, and gamma‐aminobutyric acid (GABA), have long been associated with the pathophysiology of depression, forming the basis for established treatment recommendations aimed at correcting these imbalances, such as selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors and serotonin‐norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors. 62 , 68 , 69 , 70 However, emerging evidence suggests that dopamine pathways, particularly the mesolimbic and mesocortical circuits, play a crucial role in symptoms such as anhedonia and cognitive deficits, which are often resistant to conventional therapies. 71 In addition to neurotransmitters, the altered expression of other key factors, such as BDNF, glutamate, cannabinoid receptors, acetylcholine, and substance P, has been identified as playing a crucial role in the development and persistence of depressive symptoms. 72 , 73 , 74 , 75 The hypothalamic‐pituitary‐adrenal (HPA) axis also plays a significant role in the pathophysiology of MDD, with both overactivity and underactivity of this system, leading to abnormal cortisol levels and impaired feedback mechanisms, being implicated in the condition. 76 , 77 , 78 Chronic stress exacerbates HPA axis dysregulation, resulting in prolonged exposure to glucocorticoids, which can damage hippocampal neurons and further contribute to depressive symptoms. Beyond neurotransmitters and the HPA axis, glial cells, particularly astrocytes and microglia, are increasingly recognized for their dynamic role in mood disorders. Alterations in the number, density, and size of these glial cells in patients with MDD can have profound effects on neurobiological and anatomical structures, further contributing to the complexity of the disorder. 79 Recent studies have highlighted the role of neuroinflammation in MDD, with elevated levels of pro‐inflammatory cytokines such as IL‐6, TNF‐α, and IL‐1β observed in patients. A meta‐analysis confirmed a robust link between these cytokines and major depression. 80 Research has also found that individuals with MDD exhibit higher plasma concentrations of IL‐1α and IL‐6 compared to healthy controls, which correlates with depressive severity, anxiety, and stress. 81 Similarly, reduced densities of glial fibrillary acidic protein (GFAP)‐positive astrocytes in the prefrontal cortex and hippocampus have been linked to impaired neuroplasticity and synaptic dysfunction in depression. Postmortem studies have reported fewer glial cells in several brain areas of patients with depressive disorders. 69 , 70 , 82 , 83 Likewise, glial ablation in the prefrontal cortex has been shown to induce depressive‐like behaviors, supporting the hypothesis that loss of glia contributes to the symptoms of depression. 84 Astrocytes, key regulators of neuroinflammation and neuroplasticity, play a dual role in depression. Under normal conditions, astrocytes maintain brain homeostasis by regulating synaptic function, neurotransmitter metabolism, and ion balance. However, in MDD, astrocytes often shift to a reactive phenotype, releasing pro‐inflammatory cytokines and contributing to neuronal dysfunction. This astrocyte‐mediated neuroinflammation disrupts synaptic plasticity and impairs neurogenesis, further exacerbating depressive symptoms. Targeting astrocyte‐mediated inflammatory pathways, such as the NLRP3 inflammasome, has shown promise in preclinical studies. 85 Other noninvasive brain stimulation techniques like repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation (rTMS) have been effective in modulating deep brain regions such as the subgenual anterior cingulate cortex, offering rapid relief from depressive symptoms. 86 These approaches highlight the potential for personalized treatments that address the specific biological underpinnings of depression in individual patients. 2.6 Astrocytes: Multitasking cells of the brain Astrocytes are the most abundant and vital type of macroglia in the brain, performing a wide array of functions essential for maintaining homeostasis. They guide neuronal growth by releasing neurotrophic factors, contribute to neurotransmitter metabolism, and regulate extracellular pH, and sodium (Na+) and potassium (K+) levels. 87 The increased expression of growth proteins by astrocytes also promotes synaptic health by enhancing synapse number and plasticity. 88 Moreover, astrocytes play a critical role in monitoring and maintaining the structural and functional integrity of the BBB. 89 Any alterations in the number or activity of astrocytes can significantly impact BBB functions and their ability to repair. 90 It has been demonstrated that the removal of reactive astrocytes within the CNS leads to considerable neuronal degeneration. 91 , 92 Astrocyte foot processes are closely associated with the abluminal surface of the BBB's microvascular endothelium, contributing both to the structural and functional integrity of the BBB. 93 Research has shown that ablation of astrocytes results in failed BBB repair and vasogenic edema. 94 Soluble factors secreted by astrocytes are believed to maintain the BBB, likely by inducing tight junctions between endothelial cells, although this has not been conclusively demonstrated. 95 Interestingly, endothelial cells have been shown to induce the differentiation of astrocyte precursor cells into mature astrocytes in vitro, a process mediated by leukemia‐inhibitory factor. 96 Therefore, soluble factors from both endothelial cells and astrocytes may contribute to the formation and maintenance of the BBB. Astrocytes undergo a process called astrogliosis, characterized by proliferation, morphological changes, and increased expression of GFAP, which is a common hallmark of many neurodegenerative diseases. 97 , 98 Depending on the disease context, astrogliosis can be either beneficial, promoting neuronal survival by producing growth factors and neurotrophins that support neuronal growth, or detrimental, leading to the formation of glial scars that impair neuronal function. 2.7 History, origin, structure, and the heterogeneity of astrocytes Robert Remak first identified myelinated and non‐myelinated nerve fibers in 1836, depicting what are now recognized as the earliest figures of glial cells. 99 In 1858, Rudolf Virchow coined the term “nervenkitt” (meaning nerve‐glue), which was later translated to “neuroglia”. Virchow and Somjen described neuroglia as cellular elements that fill the spaces around neurons and hold them together. 100 , 101 This pivotal characterization sets the stage for in‐depth research into glial cells. In 1865, Deiters published the first images of astrocytes in the brain, 102 challenging Virchow's notion by proposing that astrocytes were of ectodermal origin, rather than merely connective tissue. The term “astrocytes” referring to stellate‐shaped cells in the brain, was coined by Lenhossék in 1893. 103 In the same year, Andriezen further subdivided astrocytes into ectodermal fibrous astrocytes found in white matter and mesoblastic protoplasmic astrocytes located in gray matter. 104 Later, García‐Marín et al. and Cajal, whose drawings were the first to reveal the remarkable pleomorphism of astrocytes, 105 , 106 supported Andriezen's classification but proposed an ectodermal origin for both fibrous and protoplasmic astrocytes. 101 , 107 Notably, much of the early literature on astrocytes was published in German, but in 1897, Robertson provided what was then the first known systematic literature on glial cells in English. 101 , 108 Other glial cells such as microglia and oligodendrocytes were not distinguished from astrocytes until the early 20th century. 109 This initial difficulty in distinguishing individual glial cells stemmed from their common origin and the absence of cell‐specific surface markers. In vitro experiments confirmed the pluripotency of glial progenitor cells. 110 The precise origin of astrocytes remains a subject of debate. One hypothesis suggests that astrocytes derive from the radial glia, which guide neuroblasts and other multipotent progenitor cells during development (type I astrocytes). 111 Another proposes that multipotent stem cells in the ventricular and subventricular zones form astrocytic progenitors without an intermediate radial glial stage (type II astrocytes). 112 Given the lack of definitive evidence, it is possible that astrocytes exhibit heterogeneity in their origins. This heterogeneity in origin contributes to the functional diversity of astrocytes, which vary in mitotic regulation, functional gap junctional coupling, expression of transmitter receptors, membrane currents, glutamate transporters, interspecies characteristics, and electrophysiological and calcium signaling properties. 113 , 114 , 115 , 116 Golgi staining techniques confirmed the distinct morphological variations between fibrous and protoplasmic astrocytes. 113 , 117 Fibrous astrocytes are characterized by a star‐like morphology with fewer but longer branching processes that intersect with blood capillaries, forming end feet structures and aligning longitudinally along white matter tracts. In contrast, protoplasmic astrocytes have numerous fine processes that form highly branched, sheet‐like projections enveloping neuronal processes and are distributed uniformly within the cortical gray matter. A key marker of astrocyte reactivity is GFAP, an intermediate filament protein. While GFAP is widely used to identify astrocytes, not all astrocytes express GFAP, and not all GFAP‐positive cells are astrocytes. 118 , 119 In fact, a single astrocyte may not be entirely GFAP‐positive, as its numerous fine processes may lack GFAP expression. 113 Studies have shown a reduction in the number and density of GFAP‐positive astrocytes in the amygdala and hippocampus of MDD patients. 120 , 121 , 122 Additionally, GFAP immunostaining has confirmed the distinct antigenic phenotype, developmental history, morphology, and location of the two astrocyte types within the CNS. 123 Astrocytes also exhibit significant functional heterogeneity. For example, they regulate synaptic function through the release of neurotrophic factors like BDNF and GDNF, maintain ion homeostasis, and modulate BBB integrity. 42 However, under pathological conditions, astrocytes can adopt a reactive phenotype, releasing pro‐inflammatory cytokines such as IL‐6 and TNF‐α, which contribute to neuroinflammation and neuronal dysfunction. 43 , 44 , 45 , 46 2.8 Role of astrocytes in the regulation of neuroplasticity Astrocytes play a central role in regulating neuroplasticity, the brain's ability to adapt and reorganize synaptic connections in response to experience, learning, and injury. Astrocytes secrete neurotrophic factors such as BDNF, fibroblast growth factor‐2 (FGF‐2), and GDNF. These factors together promote neuronal survival, neurite outgrowth, and synaptogenesis, which are essential for maintaining and enhancing synaptic plasticity. 124 , 125 , 126 , 127 Similarly, astrocytes maintain ion and neurotransmitter homeostasis, which is critical for synaptic function. They uptake excess glutamate from the synaptic cleft via glutamate transporters (e.g., glutamate transporter‐1 [GLT‐1] and glutamate aspartate transporter [GLAST]) and convert it into glutamine, which is then transported back to neurons for the synthesis of glutamate and GABA, preventing excitotoxicity and ensuring efficient synaptic transmission. 128 , 129 Astrocytes also regulate extracellular potassium levels, maintaining the ionic environment necessary for neuronal excitability and synaptic plasticity. 129 Notably, astrocytes directly modulate synaptic function through the release of gliotransmitters such as ATP, d ‐serine, and glutamate. 34 , 130 These molecules influence synaptic strength and plasticity by activating neuronal receptors. For example, ATP modulates synaptic transmission and plasticity through purinergic receptors and d ‐serine acts as a co‐agonist at N‐methyl‐D‐aspartate (NMDA) receptors, facilitating LTP and synaptic plasticity. Additionally, astrocytes protect synapses by forming the tripartite synapse (neuron‐astrocyte‐neuron). 131 , 132 This structural support stabilizes synaptic connections and facilitates communication between neurons. Astrocytic dysfunction has been implicated in impaired neuroplasticity, a hallmark of MDD. Alterations in astrocyte morphology and function can lead to disrupted synaptic connectivity and neuronal atrophy, contributing to depressive symptoms. 49 Under neuroinflammatory conditions, astrocytes shift from a neuroprotective to a reactive phenotype, releasing pro‐inflammatory cytokines (e.g., TNF‐α, IL‐1β) that disrupt synaptic plasticity, as discussed in the next section. 2.9 Role of astrocytes in the pathophysiology of neuroinflammation Neuroinflammation has been extensively studied for its role in contributing to neurodegeneration and subsequent functional deficits in the brain. The inflammatory response is an innate mechanism designed to fight and clear infection, remove damaging chemicals, and repair damaged tissue. 133 Therefore, neuroinflammation can be either neuroprotective or neurotoxic, depending on interactions between environmental factors and genetically encoded components of the inflammatory response. 134 Acute inflammation poses an immediate life‐threatening risk, whereas chronic inflammation can result in the impairment of brain functions over extended periods, potentially lasting months, years, or a lifetime. Researchers have increasingly focused on targeting neuroinflammation using anti‐inflammatory drugs and novel nonpharmacological interventions to prevent or treat depression. For instance, Shi et al 135 demonstrated that omega‐3 fatty acids from fish oil inhibit neuroinflammation and prevent lipopolysaccharide‐induced depressive‐like behavior by reducing local cytokine production. In a case report, Najjar et al. 136 showed that intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) pulse therapy, when used as an adjunct with minocycline, a tetracycline derivative that inhibits microglial activation and cytokine production, markedly reduced depression and anxiety symptoms in a 39‐year‐old man, along with normalization of his frontal hypoperfusion. While such case studies represent lower‐level evidence, they have sparked interest in finding novel treatments for MDD through anti‐inflammatory therapies in the near future. Neuroinflammation is primarily mediated by microglia and astrocytes, but it can also be reinforced by invading peripheral leukocytes. 137 Astrocytes, the most abundant cell type in the CNS, perform diverse physiological functions, including providing structural and trophic support to neurons, facilitating neurogenesis and synaptic regulation, 138 maintaining ion balance, 139 releasing growth factors, 140 , 141 forming the BBB, and secreting apolipoprotein E, 142 thus maintaining neurophysiological and neuroimmune homeostasis in a healthy brain. Research has unveiled critical factors and intracellular signaling pathways associated with astrocyte behavior during neuroinflammation. In the presence of pathogen‐ or damage‐associated molecular patterns, resting astrocytes change to a reactive state. In this state, they upregulate the expression and secretion of pro‐inflammatory cytokines, including TNF‐α, IL‐1β, and IL‐6, as well as chemokines like CCL2 and CXL5 in the brain. 43 , 44 , 45 While the overexpression of pro‐inflammatory cytokines and chemokines, and the influx of immune phagocytic cells are crucial for controlling brain damage and promoting faster healing, they have also been shown to contribute to neurodegeneration, playing a significant role in the pathophysiology of brain diseases such as depression, dementia, and Alzheimer's disease (AD) as evidenced by clinical trials. 19 , 79 , 143 Similarly, numerous other pathologies, including viral, bacterial, and fungal infections of the peripheral nervous system, as well as stroke, trauma, neurodegenerative diseases like AD, and neuropsychiatric disorders like depression within the CNS, have been associated with the overexpression of pro‐inflammatory cytokines. 144 Notably, glial cells are not the only source of cytokines in the brain. Cytokine signals can reach the brain parenchyma through humoral, neural, and cellular pathways, involving various immune cells such as monocytes, macrophages, Th17 cells, and other T cells. 145 Reactive astrocytes also enhance the microglial inflammatory response, leading to the release of neurotoxic reactive oxygen and nitrogen species. 48 Additionally, pro‐inflammatory cytokines and chemokines attract leukocytes from the peripheral circulation through the BBB to the site of injury. 47 While this creates an inflammatory environment in the CNS that helps eliminate inflammatory stimuli, a persistent stimulus can continuously activate astrocytes, microglia, and leukocytes, leading to the destruction of BBB tight junctions, chronic neuroinflammation, and subsequent neuronal degeneration. Depending on the brain region affected, this process can result in various psychiatric illnesses. For example, elevated expression of TNF‐α has been shown to reduce hippocampal volumes via the neurodegenerative tumor necrosis factor receptor 1 (TNFR1) pathway, leading to the development of depressive‐like behavior. 50 Despite their enormous heterogeneity, astrocytes actively communicate through electrically linked syncytium at gap junctions. An increase in intracellular Ca2+ levels, induced by the neurotransmitter glutamate, forms calcium waves that propagate through astrocyte syncytium non decrementally from one astrocyte to another over relatively long distances. 146 , 147 , 148 , 149 Any change in astrocyte function can trigger a self‐propagating Ca2+ wave, expanding astrocytic activity over time. Research has shown that Ca2+ signals spread either through propagation via gap junctions or the release of adenosine triphosphate (ATP) from astrocytes and subsequent activation of purinergic receptors. 150 , 151 These Ca2+ waves activate distant astrocytes, which then react by expressing and releasing pro‐inflammatory mediators into the extracellular space, thereby spreading neuroinflammation. Recent studies have also highlighted the role of specific astrocytic receptors and ion channels in modulating these inflammatory pathways. For example, the deletion of Orai1, a calcium channel in astrocytes, has been shown to mitigate inflammation‐induced depression‐like behaviors, underscoring potential therapeutic targets within astrocyte‐mediated pathways. 152 Similarly, a study published recently showed that the NLRP3 inflammasome in astrocytes is a key mediator of neuroinflammation in MDD. 153 Activation of the NLRP3 inflammasome leads to the release of IL‐1β and IL‐18, which disrupt synaptic plasticity and promote neuronal apoptosis. 2.10 Bidirectional relationship between astrocytes and microglia in the pathophysiology of neuroinflammation The self‐sustaining interaction between activated microglia and astrocytes is central to neuroinflammation. Microglia release pro‐inflammatory cytokines (e.g., TNF‐α, IL‐1β) that induce astrocyte reactivity, while reactive astrocytes amplify inflammation by secreting chemokines (e.g., CCL2) and recruiting peripheral immune cells. 154 Bidirectional communication also occurs via ATP‐mediated calcium waves. The intracellular calcium increase in astrocytes induces the release of ATP, which, in turn, is responsible for triggering a delayed calcium response in microglial cells. Repeated stimulations of microglial cells by astrocyte‐released ATP activated P2X7 purinergic receptor on microglial cells and greatly increased membrane permeability eventually led to microglial apoptosis. 150 , 155 Similarly, aberrant astrocytic calcium signals can trigger dysfunction in microglial functions. 156 Metabolic coupling further exacerbates neuroinflammation, as glutamate released from microglial cells induces astrocyte excitotoxicity, 157 while astrocyte‐derived lactate induces microglial activation. 158 Together, these interactions disrupt synaptic plasticity, contributing to depressive phenotypes. Figure 2 shows the mechanistic crosstalk between microglia and astrocytes during MDD. Figure 2 Mechanistic crosstalk between microglia and astrocytes in major depressive disorder (MDD). The figure illustrates the mechanistic interactions between microglia and astrocytes under pathological conditions associated with MDD. Quiescent microglia become activated in response to pathogen‐associated molecular patterns (PAMPs), damage‐associated molecular patterns (DAMPs), and psychosocial stress via pattern recognition receptors (PRRs), Toll‐like receptors (TLRs), and the NOD‐like receptor thermal protein domain‐associated protein (NLRP3) inflammasome pathway. Upon activation, microglia adopt a pro‐inflammatory phenotype and release cytokines such as tumor necrosis factor‐alpha (TNF‐α), interleukin‐1 beta (IL‐1β), IL‐6, and IL‐18, as well as adenosine triphosphate (ATP) and complement proteins (C1q, C3). Astrocytes, initially exhibiting a neuroprotective A2 phenotype, respond to these inflammatory signals and convert to a neurotoxic A1 phenotype. A1 astrocytes are characterized by increased expression of glial fibrillary acidic protein (GFAP), excessive secretion of glutamate and d ‐serine, exacerbating excitotoxic stress in microglia, and the release of ATP and lactate. Lactate induces metabolic activation of quiescent microglia and contributes to histone lactylation, an epigenetic modification that sustains the A1 reactive state. Furthermore, A1 astrocytes secrete pro‐inflammatory cytokines (TNF‐α, IL‐1β, IL‐6, interferon‐gamma [IFN‐γ]), amplifying immune responses and facilitating recruitment of peripheral immune cells, such as monocytes and T cells. Astrocyte‐derived ATP also drive calcium signaling cascades that activates purinergic P2X7 receptors on microglia, further enhancing microglial inflammatory activation. This reciprocal signaling establishes a positive feedback loop between microglia and astrocytes, perpetuating neuroinflammation and neuronal stress. In contrast, A2 astrocytes, which are associated with regenerative processes such as neurogenesis and tissue repair are functionally suppressed under MDD‐associated conditions. The cumulative outcome of this glial interplay includes chronic neuroinflammation, impaired neuroplasticity, synaptic loss, and the emergence of mood and cognitive symptoms characteristic of MDD. Astrocytes have also been reported to enhance microglial inflammatory responses through an NF‐κB‐dependent mechanism, leading to the formation of neurotoxic reactive oxygen and nitrogen species. 43 , 48 Conversely, another study showed that NF‐κB signaling in microglia during manganese toxicity enhanced the expression of mRNA for TNF‐α, IL‐1β, IL‐6, CCL2, and CCL5 in astrocytes. 44 Similarly, functional crosstalk between astrocytes and microglia has also been reported during lead toxicity. 45 These findings suggest that astrocytes, despite their heterogeneity, are dependent on other cells within the larger neuroimmune network, particularly microglia, for their activity and function. For example, developing astrocytes release signals that induce the expression of complement components in the CNS. Synapses tagged with complement proteins are eliminated by microglial cells expressing complement receptors. 159 This self‐sustaining interaction between activated microglia and astrocytes plays a crucial role in neuroinflammation, BBB dysfunction, and associated depression. Hence, targeting microglia‐astrocyte crosstalk could be a promising strategy to treat MDD. 160 , 161 2.11 Metabolic and epigenetic regulation of astrocyte‐mediated neuroinflammation Astrocytes play a critical role in brain metabolism, particularly through the production and release of lactate, which serves as an energy substrate for neurons. Emerging evidence suggests that alterations in astrocytic metabolism and epigenetic modifications significantly influence neuroinflammatory responses, thereby contributing to the pathophysiology of MDD. A relative recent study has demonstrated that astrocytic lactate dehydrogenase A (LDHA) enzyme, which is responsible for lactate production, plays a critical role in maintaining neuronal excitability. Conditional knockout of LDHA in astrocytes led to reduced neuronal activity and the emergence of depressive‐like behaviors in murine models. 162 Additionally, recent studies have identified epigenetic modifications, such as histone lactylation, as key regulators of astrocyte responses during neuroinflammation. This process involves the addition of lactate moieties to histone proteins, thereby altering gene expression. For instance, under conditions such as subarachnoid hemorrhage, increased glycolytic activity in astrocytes elevates lactate production, leading to enhanced histone lactylation. This modification, mediated by bromodomain‐containing protein 4, promotes a neurotoxic A1 astrocyte phenotype, exacerbating neuroinflammation and impairing neuronal recovery. 163 Understanding these metabolic and epigenetic mechanisms may provide new targets for therapeutic intervention. 2.12 Aging: A risk factor for neuroinflammation‐induced psychiatric conditions Aging is frequently associated with deficits in brain functions, such as dementia, diminished cognition, impaired memory, anorexia, hypersomnia, lethargy, decreased social interaction, and compromised motor function, which depend on extrinsic factors (e.g., environmental influences) and intrinsic factors (e.g., genotype). 9 , 164 , 165 , 166 Cellular senescence, accompanied by anatomical changes, is a crucial underlying mechanism for the physiological changes associated with the aging brain. For example, gradual molecular changes linked with aging have been shown to result in reduced neuronal synapse number and function, 79 as well as other cellular changes. Research in rodents has demonstrated an increase in the expression of pro‐inflammatory cytokines in various brain regions with age, particularly in the hippocampus, hypothalamus, and cerebral cortex. 167 Similarly, there is evidence suggesting an increase in the levels of pro‐inflammatory cytokines in the brains of patients with MDD. The functional deficits in the brain and the onset of MDD and neurodegenerative diseases with age are, therefore, the result of molecular alterations, particularly an increase in pro‐inflammatory mediators. This leads to chronic neuroinflammation and subsequent neurodegeneration, as reported previously. 165 , 166 The reduced immune response, combined with a rise in circulating autoantibodies and lymphoproliferative disorders, further enhances the susceptibility of the aging brain to developing neuroinflammatory disorders, including MDD. 168 2.13 Astrocytes: The mediators of neuroinflammation‐induced psychiatric conditions during aging Low‐grade neuroinflammation in the aging brain involves interactions between glial cells and neurons. As aging progresses, microglia and astrocytes become increasingly dysfunctional and lose their neuroprotective properties. This has been reported to create a self‐sustaining chronic interaction between activated microglia, astrocytes, neurons, and amyloid‐beta (Aβ) plaques in the brains of mice, which resembles alterations found in patients with AD. 169 , 170 , 171 Additionally, evidence suggests that neuroinflammation exacerbates following activation of the peripheral innate immune system, leading to self‐sustaining inflammation in aged mice, which results in behavioral deficits, sickness, and depressive‐like behavior. 172 Depression in such contexts is associated with psychiatric morbidity, disability, increased healthcare utilization, and mortality in neurodegenerative diseases. The role of microglia in age‐related low‐grade neuroinflammation was highlighted in a review by Singhal and Baune. 9 The role of astrocytes in the inflammatory response, synapse health, and neurodegeneration has become increasingly clearer. Changes in astrocytic morphology and function contribute to a pro‐inflammatory and neurotoxic environment in the brain, potentially resulting in neuroinflammatory disorders such as AD, Parkinson's disease (PD), and MDD. 173 , 174 , 175 Astrocytes are key regulators of both the innate and adaptive immune responses in the brain, and their number decreases with age, transitioning to a reactive phenotype characteristic of neuroinflammatory reactive astrocytes. 176 This transformation may be due to altered gene expression in brain areas such as the hippocampus, prefrontal cortex, and striatum. 176 Excessive secretion of pro‐inflammatory cytokines (e.g., TNF‐α, IL‐1β, and IL‐6) by dysfunctional microglia in the aged brain further activates reactive astrocytes. 177 Reactive astrocytes, in turn, express pro‐inflammatory cytokines (e.g., TNF‐α, IL‐1β, IL‐6, IL‐10, interferons α, β, and γ) and chemokines (e.g., CCL2 and CCL5), mediating inflammation and immune reactivity in association with microglia. Recent clinical and experimental data have shown a strong correlation between blood pro‐inflammatory cytokine levels, especially IL‐6, quality of life, and neuropsychiatric symptoms in elderly subjects 178 and aged laboratory mice. 179 The interaction between microglia and astrocytes results in the impairment of several vital neuroprotective functions, including synapse formation and maturation, 79 neurotransmission, and the development of age‐related psychiatric disorders such as AD and MDD. Both under‐expression and overexpression of astrocytes can disrupt brain homeostasis, leading to neurodegenerative diseases and depression. 175 In addition to creating an inflammatory state, released pro‐inflammatory cytokines, such as TNF‐α and IFN‐γ, impair serotoninergic and glutamatergic neurotransmission systems by activating the tryptophan‐ and serotonin‐degrading enzyme indoleamine 2, 3‐dioxygenase (IDO). Increased consumption of serotonin and tryptophan via IDO activation is associated with depressive states during inflammatory somatic disorders. 38 , 180 , 181 Furthermore, astrocytes play a crucial role in the re‐uptake and metabolic conversion of glutamate. 38 Pro‐inflammatory cytokines in the brain may also alter hippocampal synaptic plasticity in both adult and aged rodents. 182 Reduced synaptic plasticity, dysregulated neurotransmission, and impaired neurogenesis during neuronal aging have been established as etiologies for cognitive and motor performance impairments, as well as MDD. 39 , 40 , 41 2.14 Psychosocial stress: A risk factor for neuroinflammation‐induced neurobiological impairment The complex psychosocial interactions of 21st century society introduce stressors that are unprecedented compared to those experienced in ancestral times. A substantial body of epidemiological, genetic, and clinical research supports the association between stress, impairment in neural functions, and mood disorders. Notably, patients with psychiatric diseases such as AD and PD are at an increased risk of developing depression during chronic mild psychosocial stress. 183 , 184 The immunological reactions to perceived stressors can contribute to behavioral dysfunctions and mood disorders. Glial cells, particularly microglia and astrocytes, are increasingly recognized as being primed during chronic psychosocial stress, leading to an inflammatory response in the brain that results in cognitive and behavioral deficits. 184 , 185 , 186 Activation of inflammatory and neurodegenerative pathways is reported to be an intermediate step in psychosocial stress‐induced depression. 187 , 188 During psychosocial stress, the neuroimmune system responds through various pro‐inflammatory mechanisms, including enhanced signaling of pro‐inflammatory cytokines, 189 increased activity of microglia and astrocytes, 184 , 186 , 188 activation of the complement system 190 and mitogen‐activated protein kinase pathways. 188 For the purposes of this review, the focus will be on the role of astrocytes in psychosocial stress‐induced neural impairment and subsequent depression. 2.15 Astrocytes: Modulators of neuroinflammation‐induced psychiatric conditions during psychosocial stress? Astrocytes play a crucial role in regulating several neurobiological processes, including synaptic transmission and plasticity, 191 as well as neurogenesis in the adult dentate gyrus through the production of neurotrophins. 192 Psychosocial stress negatively impacts the production of neurotrophins by astrocytes, impairing their ability to regulate these neurobiological processes and neuronal function. 186 Moreover, psychosocial stress has been shown to significantly decrease the number of astrocytes in the hippocampus of male tree shrews, which impairs the clearance of extracellular glutamate. 186 Elevated glutamate levels in the synaptic cleft can lead to excitotoxic damage to neurons. Similarly, chronic social defeat has been observed to lower GFAP levels in the rat hippocampus, 193 , 194 indicating reduced astrocytic activity and compromised maintenance of neural homeostasis. This reduction in astrocyte function may contribute to the development of depression. In fact, a postmortem study of human brains has found that a decreased density of GFAP‐immunoreactive astrocytes in the hippocampus is associated with MDD. 122 However, the decrease in GFAP‐immunoreactive astrocytes during psychosocial stress may be attributed to atrophy in astrocyte process length, branching, and volume, rather than a reduction in the total number of astrocytes. 195 Additionally, reactive astrocytes during chronic psychosocial stress produce free radicals and metalloproteinases, which can lead to increased neuronal cell death. 188 This creates a neural environment conducive to the development of inflammatory disorders, supporting the idea that neuroinflammation may act as an intermediary between psychosocial stress and depression, as suggested by other studies. 187 Thus, astrocytic dysregulation of neurobiological activity could contribute to neuroinflammation‐induced depression during psychosocial stress. While the role of microglia in psychosocial stress‐induced depression has been extensively studied, 9 further research into how psychosocial stress affects astroglia morphology and function, particularly in key areas such as the hippocampus and prefrontal cortex, is needed to fully understand the contribution of astrocytes to psychosocial stress‐induced depression. 2.16 Role of blood‐brain barrier in cross immune‐regulation and astrocytes activation The BBB plays a critical role in maintaining CNS homeostasis by regulating the movement of cells, molecules, and immune mediators between the peripheral circulation and the brain. 95 This selective permeability is essential for protecting the brain from potential toxins and pathogens. However, the BBB also facilitates crosstalk between peripheral immune signals and the CNS, which can significantly impact astrocyte function and contribute to neuroinflammatory processes. 94 Conversely, astrocytes help in maintaining the structural and functional integrity of the BBB, 89 , 93 , 95 and any change in the number or activity of astrocytes can significantly impact BBB functions. 90 , 94 Elevated levels of peripheral pro‐inflammatory cytokines, such as TNF‐α, IL‐1β, and IL‐6, are well‐documented in systemic inflammation and have been implicated in the pathogenesis of neuropsychiatric disorders, including depression and AD. 19 , 171 , 196 , 197 Peripheral cytokines and chemokines enter the CNS through various mechanisms, one of which is the disruption of the BBB. Systemic inflammation can impair BBB integrity, leading to increased permeability and allowing cytokines to cross into the brain. 197 , 198 This disruption is mediated by alterations in tight junction proteins and endothelial cell function. It allows pro‐inflammatory cytokines, along with peripheral immune cells, including monocytes and macrophages, to infiltrate the CNS and interact with brain‐resident glial cells, particularly astrocytes. 199 The astrocytes then respond by undergoing a process known as reactive astrogliosis characterized by changes in morphology and functions, increased expression of GFAP, release of reactive oxygen species (ROS) and further upregulation of inflammatory mediators. 98 For example, astrocytes may release additional pro‐inflammatory cytokines, chemokines, and reactive oxygen species, further amplifying neuroinflammation. These mediators can further disrupt the BBB, creating a feedback loop that maintains neuroinflammation. Chronic inflammation and reactive astrogliosis are associated with disruptions in synaptic plasticity, neuronal connectivity, and overall brain function. 200 These changes can result in impaired brain ability to adapt and respond to environmental and emotional stimuli, thus contributing to the development of depressive symptoms. The inflammatory environment induced by elevated peripheral cytokines can also disrupt regulating neurotransmitter levels, synaptic function, and neurogenesis that astrocytes regulate, leading to alterations in mood and cognitive function. For instance, increased levels of TNF‐α and IFN‐γ have been shown to affect serotonin and glutamate metabolism and synaptic transmission, which are critical for maintaining normal cognitive and emotional states. 38 , 180 , 181

[discussion] 3 DISCUSSION Psychiatric disorders impose a substantial global disease burden, and conventional pharmacological treatments often prove ineffective. This highlights the need to explore novel targets, such as inflammatory pathways. Key pro‐inflammatory cytokines, including IL‐1β, IL‐6, and TNF‐α, produced peripherally, have central immunological implications. 201 Neuroinflammation is a prominent feature in common psychiatric disorders, particularly MDD, bipolar disorder (BD), and schizophrenia. Evidence suggests that the progression of MDD is significantly influenced by inflammatory events. 202 , 203 , 204 While the role of microglia in MDD has long been acknowledged, 9 it is now becoming evident that astrocyte‐mediated inflammatory responses also play a critical role in pathology development, synapse health, and neurodegeneration during depression. 12 , 13 , 17 , 51 Table 2 and Figure 3 summarize the role of astrocytes in neuroinflammation and MDD. Table 2 Summary of astrocyte involvement in neuroinflammation and depression. Aspect Key roles and mechanisms Implications for depression References Neuroplasticity regulation Secretion of BDNF, FGF‐2, and GDNF, ion and neurotransmitter balance, synaptogenesis Impaired astrocytic function leads to reduced synaptic plasticity and cognitive function [ 42 , 49 , 124 , 125 , 126 , 127 ] Neurotransmission Glutamate uptake, d ‐serine and ATP release at synapses Dysregulation contributes to excitotoxicity, impaired LTP, and mood symptoms [ 34 , 38 , 128 , 129 , 130 , 131 , 132 ] Neuroinflammation mediators Reactive astrocytes release IL‐1β, IL‐6, TNF‐α, and chemokines like CCL2, CCL5 Impaired functions create a pro‐inflammatory environment, disrupting neuronal integrity [ 19 , 43 , 44 , 45 , 46 , 48 , 49 , 79 , 80 , 143 , 144 ] Microglia crosstalk Bidirectional cytokine signaling and ATP‐mediated Ca²⁺ waves, metabolic coupling Exacerbates neurotoxicity and sustains inflammatory signaling loops [ 44 , 45 , 150 , 154 , 155 , 156 , 157 ] BBB regulation Astrocyte end‐feet maintain tight junctions, secrete soluble factors to support endothelial cells Disruption permits cytokine and leukocyte infiltration, worsening inflammation [ 89 , 90 , 91 , 92 , 93 , 94 , 95 , 96 , 199 ] Aging effects Decline in astrocyte number and function, enhanced cytokine secretion (IL‐1β, TNF‐α, IL‐6) Increases chronic neuroinflammation and risk of MDD in the elderly [ 79 , 169 , 170 , 171 , 176 , 177 , 178 , 179 ] Psychosocial stress Reduced GFAP+ astrocytes, impaired neurotrophin production and glutamate clearance Excitotoxicity, reduced synaptic support, and stress‐induced depressive behavior [ 186 , 188 , 189 , 190 , 193 , 195 ] Epigenetic and metabolic shifts Lactate‐driven histone lactylation, LDHA deletion effects, Orai1 calcium channel signaling Promote pro‐inflammatory phenotypes and behavioral dysfunction [ 152 , 162 , 163 ] Note : ATP, adenosine triphosphate; BBB, the blood‐brain barrier; BDNF, brain‐derived neurotrophic factor; CCL, the C–C motif chemokine ligand; FGF‐2, fibroblast growth factor 2; GDNF, glial cell line‐derived neurotrophic factor; GFAP, glial fibrillary acidic protein; IL, interleukin; LDHA, lactate dehydrogenase A; LTP, long‐term potentiation; MDD, major depressive disorder; TNF‐α, tumor necrosis factor‐alpha. John Wiley & Sons, Ltd. Figure 3 Multifaceted role of astrocytes in the pathophysiology of depression. The figure illustrates the central role of astrocytes in regulating multiple neurobiological processes that are disrupted in major depressive disorder. Astrocytes contribute to neuroplasticity by releasing neurotrophic factors such as brain‐derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF), fibroblast growth factor‐2 (FGF‐2), and glial cell line‐derived neurotrophic factor (GDNF), and by maintaining ion homeostasis and supporting synaptogenesis. They are also critical mediators of neurotransmission, involved in the uptake of glutamate, the release of d ‐serine, and adenosine triphosphate (ATP)‐dependent signaling, all of which are essential for proper synaptic function. Astrocytes also play a role in neuroinflammation through the release of pro‐inflammatory cytokines, the adoption of reactive phenotypes, and cross‐communication with microglia. They are key regulators of the blood‐brain barrier (BBB), modulating its integrity via their end‐feet interactions with endothelial cells. Dysfunction in any of these astrocytic roles contributes to neuronal stress, resulting in the emergence of depressive symptoms, such as cognitive deficits, mood disturbances, and emotional dysregulation. Astrocytes are crucial regulators of synapse formation and function throughout development and adulthood. Changes in astrocytic function due to aging or psychosocial stress can significantly impact synaptic plasticity and neurotransmission. Dysregulated neurotransmission associated with neuronal aging and psychosocial stress has been shown to result in reduced synaptic plasticity and neurogenesis, impairing cognitive and motor functions. 39 , 40 , 41 , 186 Aging is also linked to impairments in growth hormone 39 , 205 and glucose metabolism. 40 Specific growth factors, such as hydrocortisone, putrescine, prostaglandin F‐2α, and pituitary fibroblast growth factor, regulate GFAP expression in astrocytes and influence their maturation. 206 Additionally, astrocytes play a role in glucose uptake, metabolizing it glycolytically to lactate, which is then released extracellularly to support neuronal activity. 207 , 208 Together, alterations in astrocytes due to aging and psychosocial stress have significant implications for gliosis, neurogenesis, and the subsequent development of neuroinflammation‐induced psychiatric conditions. The BBB is crucial in regulating the movement of immune signals into the CNS and mediating the interaction between peripheral inflammation and central immune responses. Any disruption in the BBB results in pro‐inflammatory cytokines and immune cells entering the brain, leading to astrocyte activation and subsequent neuroinflammation. 47 , 89 , 90 , 93 , 94 , 96 Understanding this process is essential for unraveling the mechanisms underlying neuroinflammatory diseases and developing targeted therapeutic interventions. By bridging neuronal activity and immune responses, astrocytes emerge as promising targets for novel therapeutic interventions aimed at modulating neuroinflammation in depression. Several anti‐inflammatory therapies targeting astrocytes have demonstrated positive outcomes in animal models of disease. However, the effectiveness of these therapies is often complex, as modulating inflammatory pathways depends on the specific phenotype and activities of astrocytes within various cellular environments. 209 Further research is necessary to elucidate the interactions between astrocytes, neurons, and other immune components, particularly microglia, under different conditions. Several clinical trials have also investigated the role of astroglia‐mediated pathways and related inflammatory mechanisms in MDD. A study evaluated the efficacy of infliximab, a TNF‐α antagonist, in patients with treatment‐resistant depression. 210 The study found that TNF‐α antagonism improves depressive symptoms in patients with high baseline inflammatory biomarkers. Similarly, a randomized clinical trial that assessed the effect of minocycline on patients with treatment‐resistant depression found that adjunctive minocycline led to improvement in depressive symptoms. 211 Another randomized controlled trial indicated that high‐dose omega‐3 polyunsaturated fatty acids (PUFAs) improved motivational symptoms and cognitive function in depressed individuals with elevated inflammation. 212 While the NLRP3 inflammasome has been implicated in the pathophysiology of depression, 213 there is a scarcity of clinical trials investigating NLRP3 inhibitors for depression treatment. These studies underscore the potential of targeting astroglia‐mediated pathways and inflammatory mechanisms in MDD treatment. However, further research is needed to validate these therapeutic approaches as potential novel antidepressant treatments. 3.1 Limitations, unresolved challenges, and inconsistencies in astrocyte research Despite significant progress, several challenges remain in understanding the role of astrocytes in MDD. Astrocytes exhibit considerable diversity across different brain regions and functional states. This heterogeneity complicates the identification of specific astrocyte subtypes that contribute to MDD pathophysiology. Current research often relies on generalized models, which may overlook region‐specific astrocytic functions and their unique contributions to neuroinflammation and mood regulation. The lack of selective tools to explore astrocyte function in vivo limits our ability to establish causal relationships between astrocyte activity and MDD symptoms. Additionally, the interplay between astrocytes and other immune components, such as peripheral T‐cells and monocytes, remains poorly understood. Astrocytes can transition between neuroprotective and neurotoxic states in response to various stimuli. The mechanisms governing these state changes, and their implications for MDD, are not well characterized. Focusing solely on astrocytes may not provide a complete picture of the interactions among various cell types and signaling pathways during neuroinflammation. Moreover, the influence of comorbidities and environmental factors on neuroinflammation is not fully addressed, which could confound the interpretations. Translating findings from animal models to human patients also remains a significant challenge, as many preclinical studies rely on acute inflammatory rodent models that may not fully replicate the chronic neuroinflammatory processes observed in human MDD. For instance, rodent studies have demonstrated that astrocyte dysfunction can lead to brain‐wide connectivity abnormalities associated with depressive‐like behaviors. 214 However, translating these findings into human pathology is challenging due to species‐specific differences in astrocyte biology and the complexity of human depressive disorders. Addressing these knowledge gaps may require innovative approaches, including single‐cell RNA sequencing and advanced imaging techniques, to unravel the complex role of astrocytes in MDD. The role of astrocytes in neuroinflammation and depression has also been subjected to significant controversy and variability. For example, while some studies suggest that astrocytes primarily exert neuroprotective effects by releasing anti‐inflammatory cytokines (e.g., IL‐10), others report that astrocytes contribute to neurotoxicity by producing pro‐inflammatory mediators (e.g., TNF‐α). These discrepancies may arise from differences in experimental models, such as the use of acute versus chronic inflammatory stimuli, or variations in sample characteristics, such as age and sex. Additionally, the choice of measurement criteria, such as the use of GFAP as a marker of astrocyte activation, may not fully capture the functional heterogeneity of astrocytes. Addressing these inconsistencies will require a more careful approach that considers the context‐dependent nature of astrocyte responses. 3.2 Future research directions and treatment strategies Future research should focus on identifying specific astrocyte subtypes and signaling pathways involved in MDD pathophysiology. For example, targeting astrocyte‐derived cytokines (e.g., IL‐6, TNF‐α) or metabolic regulators (e.g., lactate transporters) could offer novel therapeutic avenues. 49 , 215 The NLRP3 inflammasome in astrocytes has emerged as a key mediator of neuroinflammation, with inhibitors such as MCC950 showing promise in preclinical models. 85 Modulating specific astrocytic receptors and ion channels, such as the Orai1 calcium channel, has shown potential in preclinical models for reducing inflammation‐induced depressive behaviors. 152 Similarly, by tailoring rTMS protocols to modulate specific neural circuits implicated in a patient's depressive symptoms, clinicians can enhance therapeutic outcomes. 216 Developing drugs that target these channels could provide new treatment options. However, several challenges remain, especially the need for selective astrocyte‐targeting drugs. Clinical trials exploring anti‐inflammatory therapies, such as minocycline and omega‐3 fatty acids, have shown promise in reducing depressive symptoms, but their specific effects on astrocytes require further investigation. Additionally, investigating how peripheral immune modulation influences astrocyte function in MDD could uncover new therapeutic avenues. Furthermore, the role of astrocytes in neuroinflammation and depression varies significantly across experimental models and pathological conditions. For instance, animal studies often utilize acute inflammatory models, which may not fully replicate the chronic neuroinflammatory processes observed in MDD. 217 Rodent models, particularly those subjected to chronic stress paradigms, are extensively used to mimic depressive‐like behaviors and study astrocytic alterations. Evidence indicates that chronic stress in rodents leads to a reduction in GFAP‐immunoreactive astrocytes within the cortex, accompanied by changes in astrocyte morphology. 218 While these models provide insights into astrocyte function within an intact neural network, species‐specific differences in astrocyte physiology and responses may limit the direct applicability of results to human conditions. Discrepancies between animal and human studies may arise due to inherent physiological differences, environmental factors, and the challenges of replicating complex human psychiatric conditions in animal models. Similarly, discrepancies between in vitro and in vivo studies further underscore the importance of considering the complex interactions among astrocytes, microglia, and neurons within the CNS. The human in vivo studies offer valuable information on astrocytic changes associated with depression but are constrained by factors like postmortem delay, potential confounding variables (e.g., medication history, comorbidities), and the inability to observe dynamic processes. Individual factors such as sex and age critically influence astrocyte responses. Studies have shown that chronic stress induces astrocyte activation with sex‐ and region‐specific effects, particularly in females. 219 , 220 Similarly, research indicates that astrocytes in males may exhibit a more pronounced inflammatory response compared to astrocytes in females. 221 Additionally, aged female brains have been shown to mount a higher pro‐inflammatory response to inflammatory challenges than in males. 222 A study has also shown that GFAP expression is significantly correlated with age, with more pronounced changes observed in younger mood disorder patients. 223 To address these challenges, we propose a novel framework that integrates astrocyte heterogeneity, methodological considerations, and individual variability. This framework emphasizes the necessity for standardized measurement criteria, such as single‐cell RNA sequencing and advanced imaging techniques, to elucidate the diverse roles of astrocytes in neuroinflammation and depression. Implementing longitudinal designs will allow for the observation of temporal changes in astrocyte activity and their correlation with depressive behaviors, facilitating the identification of causal relationships. Establishing standardized experimental protocols, including consistent stress paradigms, animal handling procedures, and outcome measures, is essential to reduce variability and improve reproducibility across studies. This approach aims to enhance the reproducibility and translational potential of astrocyte research in the context of neuroinflammatory diseases. 3.3 Concluding remarks In conclusion, the astroglial response plays a critical role in neuroinflammation and the subsequent development of psychiatric conditions. The crosstalk between microglia and astrocytes, characterized by mutual activation of inflammatory cytokines and receptors, can lead to enhanced inflammatory and neurotoxic responses. This interplay may result in BBB dysfunction, neuronal degeneration, and the emergence of anxiety or depressive‐like behavior. Neuroinflammation‐induced psychiatric conditions are prevalent during neuronal aging and psychosocial stress, and targeting astrocytes presents a potential strategy for prevention, treatment, and possibly cure. Nevertheless, further research is essential to elucidate the complex signaling pathways involved in microglial and astrocytic interactions during neuroinflammation and to identify novel astrocytic targets for the prevention and treatment of psychiatric conditions.

[conclusion] 3.3 Concluding remarks In conclusion, the astroglial response plays a critical role in neuroinflammation and the subsequent development of psychiatric conditions. The crosstalk between microglia and astrocytes, characterized by mutual activation of inflammatory cytokines and receptors, can lead to enhanced inflammatory and neurotoxic responses. This interplay may result in BBB dysfunction, neuronal degeneration, and the emergence of anxiety or depressive‐like behavior. Neuroinflammation‐induced psychiatric conditions are prevalent during neuronal aging and psychosocial stress, and targeting astrocytes presents a potential strategy for prevention, treatment, and possibly cure. Nevertheless, further research is essential to elucidate the complex signaling pathways involved in microglial and astrocytic interactions during neuroinflammation and to identify novel astrocytic targets for the prevention and treatment of psychiatric conditions.

retrieved_papers.title: Peripheral cytokine levels across psychiatric disorders: A systematic review and network meta-analysis.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2023]
[Abstract] Immune dysregulated cytokine production is involved in mental diseases. However, the results are inconsistent and the pattern of cytokine alterations has not been compared across disorders. We performed a network impact analysis of cytokine levels for different psychiatric disorders including schizophrenia, major depressive disorder, bipolar disorder, panic disorder, post-traumatic stress disorder and obsessive compressive disorder to evaluate their clinical impact across conditions. Studies were identified by searching the electronic databases up to 31/05/2022. A total of eight cytokines, together with (high-sensitivity) C-reactive proteins (hsCRP/CRP) were included in the network meta-analysis. The levels of proinflammatory cytokines, hsCRP/CRP and interleukin 6 (IL-6) were significantly increased in patients with psychiatric disorders when compared to controls. IL-6 showed no significant difference among comparisons between disorders according to the network meta-analysis. Interleukin 10 (IL-10) is significantly increased in patients with bipolar disorder compared to major depressive disorder. Further, the level of interleukin-1 beta (IL-1β) was significantly increased in major depressive disorder as compared to bipolar disorder. The level of interleukin 8 (IL-8) varied among these psychiatric disorders based on the network meta-analysis result. Overall, abnormal cytokine levels were found in psychiatric disorders, and some of the cytokines displayed differential characteristics in these disorders, especially IL-8, pointing to a role as potential biomarkers for general and differential diagnosis.

[Introduction] Background This study was a randomized controlled trial investigating the effects of a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI) and improvement in depressive symptoms on innate immunity and inflammation in people with HIV (PWH).

[introduction] Background This study was a randomized controlled trial investigating the effects of a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI) and improvement in depressive symptoms on innate immunity and inflammation in people with HIV (PWH).

[methods] Methods and Materials Participants Participants were recruited from multiple settings in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, including HIV and mental health clinics, community-based organizations, and outreach to groups including PWH. Participants were included if they met the following criteria: 1) ages 18 to 70; 2) seropositive for HIV (perinatal acquisition excluded); 3) on a regimen of antiretroviral therapy (ART) for at least 3 months; 4) HIV RNA <200 copies/mL; 5) 17-item Hamilton Depression Rating Scale (HAMD-17) score ≥13; 6) diagnosis of major depressive disorder (MDD), persistent depressive disorder, unspecified depressive disorder, or other specified depressive disorder; 7) willing and able to participate with antidepressant medication regimen and scheduled follow-up visits; and 8) able to understand and provide informed consent. Participants were excluded if they met any of the following criteria: 1) acute suicidal ideation, gestures, or attempts; 2) significant cognitive impairment; 3) current bipolar disorder (I or II), schizophrenia, presence of psychotic symptoms, or known diagnosis of a primary psychotic disorder; 4) currently taking antipsychotic medication; 5) current pharmacotherapy for depression; 6) immunization with trial HIV vaccine; 7) currently pregnant, within 9 months postdelivery, and/or breastfeeding; 8) history of seizure disorder or taking carbamazepine; 9) renal failure; 10) history of acute narrow/closed-angle glaucoma; 11) a current or chronic medical condition that would likely preclude adherence to protocol or completion of trial; 12) use of immune-altering medication within 4 weeks prior to enrollment (except acyclovir and related antiviral medications, corticosteroids, or statin medications); 13) history of intolerance or nonresponse to an adequate trial of SSRI; 14) taken monoamine oxidase inhibitors within 14 days; 15) taking linezolid or intravenous methylene blue; 16) on a regular regimen of anticoagulant medication; 17) currently taking central nervous system drugs (except gabapentin, pregabalin, varenicline, antihistamines, and hypnotics); 18) on any triptan medications; or 19) undergoing electroconvulsive therapy. Individuals not eligible were referred for care outside of the research setting as clinically indicated. The protocol was approved by the University of Pennsylvania Institutional Review Board. All participants provided written informed consent after thorough review of the informed consent form and discussion of study procedures with research staff. Participants were reimbursed for their time and travel (maximum $380 across 10 weeks). Procedures Study activities occurred in Philadelphia from February 2017 to January 2022 (see Figure 1 ). Prior to enrollment, study staff screened each participant, who received a comprehensive medical and psychiatric assessment, including plasma HIV RNA measurement, urine pregnancy test in premenopausal women with no history of tubal ligation, the International HIV Dementia Scale, and the Structured Clinical Interview for DSM-5 diagnoses ( 35 ). Depression symptom severity was evaluated with the HAMD-17. Figure 1 CONSORT (Consolidated Standards of Reporting Trials) flow diagram for study screening, enrollment, randomization, and completion. PI, principal investigator. To minimize potential circadian and activity/exertional effects on immune measures, blood samples were collected in almost all cases in the morning after participants had fasted since midnight and rested for at least 30 minutes. The research pharmacy randomized participants according to a list generated by the study statistician (using PROC PLAN in SAS/STAT software; version 8) to 10 weeks of double-blinded therapy with either the SSRI escitalopram or placebo. Stratification was based on gender and baseline depression symptom severity (i.e., those with HAMD-17 scores ≤19 or ≥20) and randomizations within each stratum were balanced in blocks of size 8. No study staff with participant contact knew of the treatment assignment prior to unblinding at each participant’s final study visit. Only the research pharmacy remained unblinded to treatment condition. Escitalopram was selected for its efficacy and pharmacological properties. A meta-analysis concluded that escitalopram was one of only 2 (along with sertraline) newer-generation antidepressants that were superior in terms of both efficacy and tolerability ( 36 ). Furthermore, it was considered well suited to a study of medically complex patients because of relatively simple dosing and few drug-drug interactions. Eligible participants were enrolled in the RCT by study psychiatrists, who prescribed 10 mg of escitalopram or placebo equivalent daily and evaluated participants weekly from baseline to week 6 and at weeks 8 and 10. Upward titration was permitted after 1 week based on tolerability and clinical response, and downward titration was permitted if side effects were severe. Because of ethical concerns regarding the use of placebo in a potentially vulnerable group, all participants concurrently received computer-assisted cognitive behavior therapy (CCBT) in combination with pharmacotherapy. CCBT was conducted using the Good Days Ahead program ( 37 ). This model paired baseline and biweekly sessions with a cognitive therapist and weekly web-based modules that teach CBT skills through interactive exercises. This form of CCBT has been shown to be noninferior to, and significantly more cost-effective than, conventional CBT in one RCT ( 38 , 39 ) and was found to be significantly more effective than treatment as usual in one study of depression in primary care ( 40 , 41 , 42 ).
