In addition, the presence of ROS/reactive nitrogen species in mitochondria leads to oxidation of mitochondrial DNA (mtDNA), proteins, and lipids [ 10 , 11 ]. Inflammation and immune dysfunction may be involved in BD pathophysiology [ 12 , 13 , 14 , 15 , 16 , 17 ]. This review focuses on the roles of ROS and ROS-induced oxidative damage, mitochondrial dysfunction, DNA damage, and DA system dysregulation, and immune dysfunction in the pathophysiology of BD. It also presents an overview of potential biomarkers, including lipid peroxidation, thiobarbituric acid reactive substances (TBARSs), and brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF), in patients with BD. Diet influences several processes that are dysregulated in BD, including monoaminergic activity, inflammation and immune processes, OS, mitochondrial function, and neuroprogression. Therefore, in this review, we also discuss the possible use of nutrients, including vitamin D, copper, folic acid, and polyunsaturated fatty acids (PUFAs), in the treatment of patients with BD. In addition to nutraceutical approaches, we further discuss the use of anti-inflammatory agents and novel antipsychotic agents, such as clozapine, olanzapine, risperidone, cariprazine, and quetiapine, as alternatives to lithium or conventional antipsychotic agents. Furthermore, we assess the use of therapies, such as cognitive–behavioral therapy (CBT), bright light therapy (BLT), imagery-focused cognitive therapy (ImCT), and electroconvulsive therapy (ECT), to treat BD.

[conclusion] 15. Conclusions BD is becoming increasingly understood as a condition of aberrant neuroplasticity. Multiple factors, such as OS, imbalance of neurotransmitters, and genetics, are associated with the pathophysiology of BD. OS, caused by an imbalance between oxidant and antioxidant enzymes, may lead to cell damage. Decreased antioxidants and greater production of oxidizing agents lead to OS, causing alterations in proteins, carbohydrates, lipids, and DNA. In addition, reactive species act on mitochondria to eventually lead to increased concentrations of ROS/reactive nitrogen species, further oxidizing mitochondrial lipids, proteins, and DNA. In this regard, BD is associated with lipid peroxidation and DNA damage. Consistent with this observation, TBARS levels, which act as a lipid peroxidation marker, tend to be increased in patients with BD. Moreover, patients with BD have reduced BDNF levels, along with altered Ca 2+ homeostasis and increased peripheral Ca 2+ levels. Studies of both CNS-related and systemic cytokine changes in patients with BD have indicated that the neuroimmune system plays an important role in BD pathophysiology. Therefore, nutraceuticals may have a role in the adjuvant treatment of BD. An accumulating body of evidence suggests that the therapeutic use of antioxidants in BD is beneficial in the treatment of depression associated with BD. Supplements that have been proposed to have therapeutic value in BD include vitamin D, omega-3 PUFAs, and folic acid. In addition to omega-3 fatty acids and vitamin D, other nutrients such as vitamin A, vitamin C, polyphenols, carotenoids, and other anti-inflammatory compounds might contribute to the homeostatic regulation of OS and inflammation. To date, lithium is the leading mood stabilizer used to ameliorate the pathophysiology of BD because of its effects on neuroplasticity. Oral antipsychotic agents, such as clozapine, olanzapine, risperidone, cariprazine, and quetiapine, are regarded as the first-line drug treatments for psychotic depression, and they are especially recommended for maintenance therapy. In addition, CBT, BLT, ImCT, and ECT have all been proven as effective adjuvant therapies for the treatment of BD.

retrieved_papers.title: Duration of Adjunctive Antidepressant Maintenance in Bipolar I Depression.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2023]
[Abstract] Antidepressants are used to treat acute depression in patients with bipolar I disorder, but their effect as maintenance treatment after the remission of depression has not been well studied.
We conducted a multisite, double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled trial of maintenance of treatment with adjunctive escitalopram or bupropion XL as compared with discontinuation of antidepressant therapy in patients with bipolar I disorder who had recently had remission of a depressive episode. Patients were randomly assigned in a 1:1 ratio to continue treatment with antidepressants for 52 weeks after remission or to switch to placebo at 8 weeks. The primary outcome, assessed in a time-to-event analysis, was any mood episode, as defined by scores on scales measuring symptoms of hypomania or mania, depression, suicidality, and mood-episode severity; additional treatment or hospitalization for mood symptoms; or attempted or completed suicide. Key secondary outcomes included the time to an episode of mania or hypomania or depression.
Of 209 patients with bipolar I disorder who participated in an open-label treatment phase, 150 who had remission of depression were enrolled in the double-blind phase in addition to 27 patients who were enrolled directly. A total of 90 patients were assigned to continue treatment with the prescribed antidepressant for 52 weeks (52-week group) and 87 were assigned to switch to placebo at 8 weeks (8-week group). The trial was stopped before full recruitment was reached owing to slow recruitment and funding limitations. At 52 weeks, 28 of the patients in the 52-week group (31%) and 40 in the 8-week group (46%) had a primary-outcome event. The hazard ratio for time to any mood episode in the 52-week group relative to the 8-week group was 0.68 (95% confidence interval [CI], 0.43 to 1.10; P = 0.12 by log-rank test). A total of 11 patients in the 52-week group (12%) as compared with 5 patients in the 8-week group (6%) had mania or hypomania (hazard ratio, 2.28; 95% CI, 0.86 to 6.08), and 15 patients (17%) as compared with 35 patients (40%) had recurrence of depression (hazard ratio, 0.43; 95% CI, 0.25 to 0.75). The incidence of adverse events was similar in the two groups.
In a trial involving patients with bipolar I disorder and a recently remitted depressive episode, adjunctive treatment with escitalopram or bupropion XL that continued for 52 weeks did not show a significant benefit as compared with treatment for 8 weeks in preventing relapse of any mood episode. The trial was stopped early owing to slow recruitment and funding limitations. (Funded by the Canadian Institutes of Health Research; ClinicalTrials.gov number, NCT00958633.).

[Introduction] 1 Introduction The suicide rate in people with bipolar disorder (BD) is estimated to be up to 24 times the rate in the general population [ 1 , 2 , 3 ], and therefore individuals suffering from BD account for a significant proportion (4%–14%) of all suicide deaths [ 1 ]. One model for suicide in BD [ 4 ] that shares features with others [ 5 , 6 ] incorporates neurobiological elements alongside clinical and environmental factors [ 4 ]. Collectively, these factors predispose people with BD to suicidal thinking that may culminate in a lethal attempt. While suicide in BD is thought to be preventable, current predictive models, both general [ 7 ] and those specifically developed for BD populations [ 8 , 9 ], have failed to achieve sufficient accuracy to provide confidence in the prediction of suicide in individuals in clinical settings. This has compelled a shift towards broad‐based suicide prevention efforts that ensure all people at risk for suicide receive care to diminish their risk of suicide [ 10 , 11 ]. A central aspect of any suicide prevention strategy is ensuring patients receive evidence‐based care for managing their condition, and this is particularly important in BD, which, as highlighted, carries a disproportionately greater risk [ 12 ]. Prospective data have demonstrated the anti‐suicide benefits of receiving appropriate evidence‐based treatment for BD [ 13 , 14 ] and this includes algorithm‐guided pharmacotherapy and psychotherapy for BD [ 15 ]. Specifically, first‐line BD treatments such as mood stabilizers and atypical antipsychotics reduce suicidal behaviors and deaths [ 16 ]. Of these, lithium in particular has been extensively studied in this regard, with substantive evidence supporting a likely direct anti‐suicide effect in addition to benefits achieved through more general mood stabilization [ 17 , 18 ]. There are now several well‐established and easily accessible clinical practice guidelines that recommend specific pharmacotherapeutic strategies for acute and maintenance phases of BD [ 19 , 20 , 21 ], and large clinical datasets confirm frequent use of efficacious medications [ 22 ] which serve as a key component of effective suicide prevention efforts [ 23 , 24 ]. The nature of pharmacotherapy received by people with BD around the time of their suicide death has been examined in a small number of previous studies [ 25 , 26 ], including our own [ 27 ]. In a small Australian sample, the majority of people with BD who died by suicide did not receive benchmark standards of care [ 25 ]. In a much larger study of suicides in people with BD in England from 1996 to 2009, among those receiving mental health care, 60% had been prescribed an antidepressant, 58% were prescribed lithium or another mood stabilizer, and 38.9% were prescribed an atypical antipsychotic [ 26 ]. The findings did not differentiate between monotherapy and combinations, and also did not report data from the subpopulation not receiving any mental health care. A past analysis of our group's Toronto dataset of suicides in people with BD up to 2012 included all suicide deaths irrespective of recent mental health contact, and found benzodiazepines and antidepressants to be the most common medication classes present in toxicology samples among those with BD who died via self‐poisoning [ 27 ]. The available studies to date reported on suicide data up to 2012, with concerning findings about the extent of evidence‐based care at the time of death. Over the past decade there has been substantial progress, with several new treatment options becoming available and evidence‐based clinical practice guidelines increasingly shaping clinical care for patients with BD [ 19 ], with resultant shifts in prescribing patterns [ 28 ]. We therefore sought to conduct a contemporary examination of potential changes over the years in the medications and/or substances found at the time of death among BD suicide decedents using a large dataset. In this study, we have also further expanded our approach to include toxicology results from a subsample of those who died by suicide methods other than self‐poisoning—data that was not considered in our previous research. Characterizing which compounds were detected has the potential to shed light on aspects of the suicide method as well as the presence of medications at any level that could serve as a proxy for understanding the medications patients had been prescribed and were taking at the time of death. Therefore, our objective for this study was to characterize all BD suicide decedents in Toronto over a 23‐year period (1998–2020) and gain insights into key factors surrounding suicide in this highly vulnerable group by examining which medications and substances were present and/or lethal at the time of death from their toxicology reports and how this changed over time.

[introduction] 1 Introduction The suicide rate in people with bipolar disorder (BD) is estimated to be up to 24 times the rate in the general population [ 1 , 2 , 3 ], and therefore individuals suffering from BD account for a significant proportion (4%–14%) of all suicide deaths [ 1 ]. One model for suicide in BD [ 4 ] that shares features with others [ 5 , 6 ] incorporates neurobiological elements alongside clinical and environmental factors [ 4 ]. Collectively, these factors predispose people with BD to suicidal thinking that may culminate in a lethal attempt. While suicide in BD is thought to be preventable, current predictive models, both general [ 7 ] and those specifically developed for BD populations [ 8 , 9 ], have failed to achieve sufficient accuracy to provide confidence in the prediction of suicide in individuals in clinical settings. This has compelled a shift towards broad‐based suicide prevention efforts that ensure all people at risk for suicide receive care to diminish their risk of suicide [ 10 , 11 ]. A central aspect of any suicide prevention strategy is ensuring patients receive evidence‐based care for managing their condition, and this is particularly important in BD, which, as highlighted, carries a disproportionately greater risk [ 12 ]. Prospective data have demonstrated the anti‐suicide benefits of receiving appropriate evidence‐based treatment for BD [ 13 , 14 ] and this includes algorithm‐guided pharmacotherapy and psychotherapy for BD [ 15 ]. Specifically, first‐line BD treatments such as mood stabilizers and atypical antipsychotics reduce suicidal behaviors and deaths [ 16 ]. Of these, lithium in particular has been extensively studied in this regard, with substantive evidence supporting a likely direct anti‐suicide effect in addition to benefits achieved through more general mood stabilization [ 17 , 18 ]. There are now several well‐established and easily accessible clinical practice guidelines that recommend specific pharmacotherapeutic strategies for acute and maintenance phases of BD [ 19 , 20 , 21 ], and large clinical datasets confirm frequent use of efficacious medications [ 22 ] which serve as a key component of effective suicide prevention efforts [ 23 , 24 ]. The nature of pharmacotherapy received by people with BD around the time of their suicide death has been examined in a small number of previous studies [ 25 , 26 ], including our own [ 27 ]. In a small Australian sample, the majority of people with BD who died by suicide did not receive benchmark standards of care [ 25 ]. In a much larger study of suicides in people with BD in England from 1996 to 2009, among those receiving mental health care, 60% had been prescribed an antidepressant, 58% were prescribed lithium or another mood stabilizer, and 38.9% were prescribed an atypical antipsychotic [ 26 ]. The findings did not differentiate between monotherapy and combinations, and also did not report data from the subpopulation not receiving any mental health care. A past analysis of our group's Toronto dataset of suicides in people with BD up to 2012 included all suicide deaths irrespective of recent mental health contact, and found benzodiazepines and antidepressants to be the most common medication classes present in toxicology samples among those with BD who died via self‐poisoning [ 27 ]. The available studies to date reported on suicide data up to 2012, with concerning findings about the extent of evidence‐based care at the time of death. Over the past decade there has been substantial progress, with several new treatment options becoming available and evidence‐based clinical practice guidelines increasingly shaping clinical care for patients with BD [ 19 ], with resultant shifts in prescribing patterns [ 28 ]. We therefore sought to conduct a contemporary examination of potential changes over the years in the medications and/or substances found at the time of death among BD suicide decedents using a large dataset. In this study, we have also further expanded our approach to include toxicology results from a subsample of those who died by suicide methods other than self‐poisoning—data that was not considered in our previous research. Characterizing which compounds were detected has the potential to shed light on aspects of the suicide method as well as the presence of medications at any level that could serve as a proxy for understanding the medications patients had been prescribed and were taking at the time of death. Therefore, our objective for this study was to characterize all BD suicide decedents in Toronto over a 23‐year period (1998–2020) and gain insights into key factors surrounding suicide in this highly vulnerable group by examining which medications and substances were present and/or lethal at the time of death from their toxicology reports and how this changed over time.

[methods] 2 Methods 2.1 Data Source All verified suicide deaths in the City of Toronto, Canada between January 1, 1998 and December 31, 2020 were identified through systematic chart reviews and abstraction at the Office of the Chief Coroner (OCC) of Ontario. These were identified by the internal data capture system at the coroner's office and provided to our research team for review. Verified deaths had to have been ruled a suicide by the investigating coroner, and the deceased had to reside and die in the City of Toronto. It can take up to 2 years for coroner investigations to be completed. The study was approved by the Research Ethics Board at Sunnybrook Health Sciences Centre, Toronto, Canada. Strict privacy procedures utilized by the OCC were fully adhered to, with all extracted data maintained in an encrypted and de‐identified format. 2.2 Data Retrieval Trained coders reviewed all documentation within the coroner investigation records, including the coroner's report, rough notes, post‐mortem report, toxicology report, and police reports. Psychiatric and medical history included data from either the deceased's medical records or interviews with relatives, friends, or acquaintances. Coders used a standardized template to extract the following information: (i) demographics (age, sex, relationship/marital status and living situation); (ii) relevant stressors (bereavement, employment/financial/academic, relationship problems, interpersonal conflict, recent medical/health, police/legal); (iii) mental health history (psychiatric diagnosis, alcohol/substance use problems, past suicide attempts, past week acute care mental health contact); (iv) history of physical problems (e.g., pain, cancer, diabetes, cardiac issues) and (v) details of the suicide (method of death, presence of a suicide note, place of death). All coders reached high inter‐rater reliability ( κ ≥ 0.8) on a training set of charts and then independently extracted information from charts. Coders would engage in frequent discussion throughout to resolve uncertainties, with the research team leads (M.S. and A.S.) resolving any disagreements. 2.3 Sample Consistent with prior reports from our research group using this data source [ 29 ], the presence of a diagnosis of BD was established based on information in the coroner's investigation records. Diagnostic information was obtained by the coroner from a variety of sources, including medical records, collateral information from family (e.g., a child reporting their parent having a longstanding history of BD), police reports of personal records or other evidence that was present at the scene of death or home of the deceased, and content of the suicide note that may have stated a diagnosis of BD. Specific symptom reviews or psychological autopsies were not part of the study protocol, and since diagnostic evaluation was not the primary focus of the coroner investigations, complete accuracy of the diagnosis could not be established. It is likely that a small number of decedents with BD were not recorded as having the diagnosis, and it is also possible that a small number of decedents identified as having BD were misclassified. All suicide deaths in people with a BD diagnosis documented in the coroner's investigation records were included in the sample of BD suicide deaths, and all other suicides were included in the non‐BD suicide sample. The OCC primarily conducts toxicology tests at the time of death in cases where self‐poisoning is suspected, but at times at their discretion will also obtain these for other modes of death. Toxicology reports include information on the level and distribution of all substances detected in the blood or urine, which is interpreted based on the approximate time since death. The pathologist uses this information to determine whether the substance was a cause of death (which we describe as “lethal”) or present at any level (lethal or not) at the time of the death (which we describe as “present”). Such determinations are complex, as they can depend on the deceased's medical history as well as substance levels that are influenced by the timing of bodily fluid retrieval. We relied exclusively on the coroner's determination of the presence and/or lethality of specific substances in each case rather than arriving at our own conclusions from the review of raw toxicology data, and prior studies using OCC toxicology data in other datasets have firmly established these procedures for collection and determination [ 30 , 31 ]. The broad categories of substances we utilized included specific classes of prescription psychotropic medications, prescription non‐psychotropic medications, over‐the‐counter medications, alcohol, opioids, recreational drugs, and poisons. 2.4 Data Analysis Descriptives of each of the variables of interest were calculated. Comparisons of BD and non‐BD groups were conducted by univariate analyses ( t ‐test or χ 2 ) of all demographic, clinical, stressor, and suicide‐specific variables. Differences in the proportion of different medication/substance classes or specific agents detected as being present or lethal at the time of death were compared between the BD and non‐BD groups using a series of χ 2 tests. In a subgroup analysis, χ 2 tests were also used to examine for differences in the proportion of medications/substances present between the first and second 11.5‐year time epochs. While this is an arbitrary dividing point between the two epochs, and changes are likely to occur more continuously, equally separating the time periods assisted with maintaining sufficient power to explore differences over time.

[results] 3 Results During the period from 1998 to 2020, suicide deaths among people in Toronto identified as having BD accounted for 340 out of 5288 recorded suicides (6.4%). Table 1 compares the sociodemographic, mental health history, and suicide‐related variables between those identified as having BD and those without BD. The BD suicide group was found to be significantly younger, less likely to be married, and to have a significantly lower male‐to‐female (M:F) ratio (M:F ratio = 1.30 in BD vs. 2.46 in non‐BD). Decedents with BD were significantly less likely to have experienced a recent employment, financial, and/or academic stressor, or a stressor related to their medical health. They were significantly more likely to have a history of prior suicide attempts, comorbid alcohol/substance use problems, or recent acute mental health care presentation. With regard to method of death, the decedents with BD were significantly more likely to die via self‐poisoning with a medication, and significantly less likely to die by hanging. self‐immolation, electrocution, and shooting. Table 2 lists the classes of medications or substances present at the time of suicide death, irrespective of level found (i.e., present but not necessarily at lethal levels). An average of 2.1 prescription psychotropic medications was present in the BD group as compared to 1.4 in the non‐BD group. There was an average of 3.5 different medications or substances present in the BD group compared to 2.7 in the non‐BD group. Antidepressants were the single most common specific medication group present among decedents with BD (48.9%) followed by benzodiazepines (46.7%). Antipsychotics and mood stabilizers were present in 37.2% and 22.6% of decedents with BD, respectively. When combining antipsychotics and/or mood stabilizers, that number increased to 51.1%. An unopposed antidepressant (i.e., without a mood stabilizer or antipsychotic) was present in 18.2% of BD decedents. There were no significant differences in the presence of opioids (27.7% vs. 28%), alcohol (42.3% vs. 43.9%), or recreational drugs (15.3% vs. 14.1%) between the BD and non‐BD groups. BD ( n = 137) N (%) Non‐BD ( n = 1366) N (%) χ 2 p Specific classes a Any antidepressant 67 (48.9) 510 (37.3) 7.047 0.008 Benzodiazepine 64 (46.7) 549 (40.2) 2.195 0.138 Non‐TCA antidepressant 58 (42.3) 382 (28.0) 12.420 < 0.001 Alcohol 58 (42.3) 599 (43.9) 0.116 0.733 Antipsychotic 51 (37.2) 199 (14.6) 46.099 < 0.001 Opioid 38 (27.7) 382 (28.0) 0.003 0.955 Over the counter medication 36 (26.3) 354 (25.9) 0.009 0.927 Mood stabilizer 31 (22.6) 33 (2.4) 124.768 < 0.001 Other prescription medication 28 (20.4) 217 (15.9) 1.891 0.169 Tricyclic antidepressant (TCA) 23 (16.8) 230 (16.8) 0.000 0.988 Recreational drug 21 (15.3) 193 (14.1) 0.147 0.702 Poison ꝉ 76 (5.6) 1.727 0.189 Combinations Antipsychotic and/or mood stabilizer (any AP/MS) 70 (51.1) 224 (16.4) 95.265 < 0.001 Antidepressant (without antipsychotic and/or mood stabilizer) 25 (18.2) 374 (27.4) 5.324 0.021 Note: ꝉ, Sample size < 5 suppressed. Bold is for p values of < 0.5. a Medications or substances could have been present on their own or in combination with others. Table 3 lists the specific medications/substances present among BD suicide decedents, irrespective of mode of death. The most common single agents that were present alone or in combination with other medications/substances were quetiapine (21.9%), clonazepam (17.5%), and lorazepam (13.1%). Among antidepressants, citalopram/escitalopram was the most common (9.5%) followed by trazodone (8.0%) and venlafaxine (7.3%). Within the mood stabilizer category, carbamazepine was most common (8.0%), followed by lithium (5.8%) and lamotrigine (5.1%). Any medication or substance not listed in Table 3 was by definition not found in any of the decedents with BD; for instance, there were several newer atypical antipsychotics not identified in any decedent. For any medication listed but without a numerical value, the number was suppressed due to the privacy requirement of any count between 1 and 4 cases. Table 5 shows the classes of medications or substances that were identified at lethal levels (i.e., had been determined to be the sole or combined cause of death) at the time of self‐poisoning death. Opioids were the most common substance category, and mood stabilizers and antipsychotics were significantly more common in the BD group, whereas tricyclic antidepressants were significantly less common. Table S1 displays which specific medications were found at lethal levels within the BD group, although much of this information is suppressed due to privacy restrictions associated with low frequency counts. The most common specific lethal medications identified, in decreasing order, were quetiapine, oxycodone, diphenhydramine, and codeine. BD ( n = 95) a N (%) Non‐BD ( n = 700) a N (%) χ 2 p Opioid 21 (22.1) 203 (29.0) 1.965 0.161 Antipsychotic 20 (21.1) 62 (8.9) 13.449 < 0.001 Benzodiazepine 15 (15.8) 98 (14.0) 0.220 0.639 Non‐TCA antidepressant 12 (12.6) 80 (11.4) 0.118 0.731 Over the counter medication 12 (12.6) 76 (10.9) 0.268 0.605 Mood stabilizer 10 (10.5) ꝉ 47.916 < 0.001 Tricyclic antidepressant 7 (7.4) 119 (17.0) 5.818 0.016 Alcohol ꝉ 39 (5.6) 0.303 0.582 Recreational drug ꝉ 16 (2.3) 0.273 0.488 Other prescription drug ꝉ 48 (6.9) 1.907 0.167 Poison ꝉ 3 (0.4) 0.409 1.000 Note: More than one class of drug could have been determined to be lethal; therefore, the cumulative percentage is above 100%. ꝉ, n < 5 suppressed. a Toxicology reports were available for 137 individuals with BD (95 of whom died by self‐poisoning) and for 1366 individuals without BD (700 of whom died by self‐poisoning).

[discussion] 4 Discussion In this large cohort of all suicide deaths in Toronto over a 23‐year span, those with BD accounted for 6.4% of all suicide deaths, which is within the expected range for the proportion of suicide deaths identified in the literature, thus indirectly supporting the diagnostic precision of this group. Demographic and clinical differences between the BD and non‐BD groups fell along expected patterns, including the BD group being younger, experiencing fewer precipitating psychosocial stressors, and having higher rates of prior suicide attempts and contact with acute mental health services. The significantly lower male‐to‐female ratio is also in keeping with prior studies and demonstrates the larger relative impact of the illness on suicides among females with BD [ 32 ]. The more notable findings stem from the available toxicology results for the BD group. Data on the presence of medications at the time of death (irrespective of lethality of the agent) can serve as a proxy measure for pharmacotherapy received at the time of death. Similar to prior studies conducted before 2012, antidepressants remain the most common medication class present (48.9%), followed closely by benzodiazepines (46.7%). Only when combining an antipsychotic and/or mood stabilizer do usual first‐line treatments for BD reach similar values (51%; see Table 2 ). Mood stabilizers, well established as the core pharmacotherapy class in BD, were less commonly present (22.6%) than opioids (27.7%). Among mood stabilizers, carbamazepine was the most common single agent, with lithium being present in only 5.8% of those with BD, followed by lamotrigine in 5.1%. The comparison of earlier and later epochs found a significant decline in the presence of mood stabilizers at the time of death. Since it is unlikely that the anti‐suicide effectiveness of mood stabilizers has changed, these findings suggest an increasing lack of concordance between the treatments used in people with BD who die by suicide and clinical guidelines that promote first‐line mood stabilizer use or even first‐use of lithium [ 19 , 20 , 33 ]. However, the prescription patterns of antidepressant use, especially without concomitant mood stabilizer and/or antipsychotic use, show a numerical reduction between the earlier and later epochs, which is consistent with guideline recommendations. The interpretation of these findings cannot veer into causality for several reasons. We did not have access to data that would have allowed a linked representative or matching living BD or non‐BD sample. Therefore, the denominator of how many people were prescribed or took these medications cannot be determined. Furthermore, at the time of death, a large proportion of BD suicide decedents were seemingly not receiving the pharmacotherapy with the strongest evidence, but there are several possible reasons for this. Firstly, it could be that this relates to prescribers failing to adhere to evidence‐based guidelines; secondly, a greater severity or refractoriness of illness in decedents with BD could have prompted the use of antidepressants or other medications, even if they are lower in the treatment algorithm (e.g., in the context of prior failure of treatments with more evidence); thirdly, there are specific clinical scenarios when medications such as antidepressants have a strong evidence base (e.g., BD‐II, or for acute or maintenance depression treatment when other agents have not been sufficient) [ 34 ]; or fourthly, a survivor bias wherein those who received evidence‐based care were less likely to die by suicide. This conundrum is most apparent when considering the very low percentage of BD suicide decedents who had lithium present at the time of death (< 6%). Lithium is known to protect against suicide over and above its mood stabilizing properties [ 33 ], and while one interpretation of our results could be to lament the extremely low numbers who were receiving lithium (which may have prevented their suicide), another interpretation is that, once a patient is taking lithium, they are at a much reduced likelihood of suicide and, therefore, this could be indirect evidence of the anti‐suicide effects of lithium. It is noteworthy that the two interpretations are not mutually exclusive and that both are likely. Indeed, a prior epidemiological survey conducted in 2002 of 852 people living with BD in Canada found that only 8.2% had been prescribed lithium at any point during the prior 12 months [ 35 ], which is in keeping with other recent data showing low frequency of lithium use during bipolar depressive and other phases [ 36 ], suggesting that, in fact, the large majority of people with BD are not provided lithium treatment, and the low numbers in our suicide cohort more likely highlight the under‐utilization of lithium and other first‐line treatments, with reduced use over time, and the tragic lethal consequences of this. While toxicology data alone cannot serve as a measure for the quality of pharmacotherapy received around the time of death, it does have some advantages over pharmacoepidemiological data which cannot confirm actual ingestion of the medications or substances. These data should therefore be interpreted within the broader literature on population‐wide prescribing patterns in BD and their impacts on illness stabilization and suicide‐related outcomes [ 37 , 38 , 39 ]. As a whole, this literature continues to suggest major gaps in the delivery of the first‐line treatments for BD, including low rates of lithium use. The most common lethal self‐poisoning ingestions within the BD group were quetiapine, oxycodone, diphenhydramine, and codeine. All other specific compounds fell below the privacy cut‐off of < 5, so could not be individually reported. Other than quetiapine, the most common substances ingested in lethal self‐poisoning are medications that do not fall within the category of prescription psychotropic medications, which highlights the need to broaden consideration of medication safety and means restriction beyond agents that are being prescribed for the purpose of BD management. Means restriction and close monitoring of potentially lethal agents such as opioids and diphenhydramine that are readily accessible to people with BD should be considered as part of a safety planning approach [ 40 , 41 ]. Interpreting the pattern of compounds present and/or lethal at the time of death also needs to incorporate the element of relative toxicity. We did not have data on compounds used in non‐lethal self‐poisonings, so we could not directly examine this. However, differences in toxicity are clearly present, with, for instance, evidence that the case fatality of carbamazepine is more than twice that of lithium [ 42 ], which likely impacts the ratio of medications in the lethal vs. present categories. There are a number of further limitations to consider when interpreting these results. First, there is no validated approach to ensuring BD diagnostic accuracy from coroner records, and even if the BD diagnosis is fairly accurate, which is supported by the proportion of suicide deaths among the full cohort, the specifics of the bipolar illness such as subtype, phase of illness, comorbidities, and past treatments are not systematically recorded and therefore could not be included in our analysis. This is especially relevant when trying to establish whether first‐line or even generally appropriate pharmacotherapy was being received, since the nature of evidence‐based care varies across these different illness characteristics. Clinical guideline recommendations have also changed from the 1990s to current times, and newer treatments were only available for a shorter duration of the study period. However, it is difficult to imagine a scenario where antidepressants and benzodiazepines would be consistently much more commonly recommended than atypical antipsychotics or mood stabilizers. Also, lithium only being present in 5.8% of suicide decedents with BD, alongside pharmacoepidemiological data of only 8.2% in Canada being prescribed lithium, is highly suggestive of under‐utilization in a group that ultimately died by suicide. Second, while toxicology results provide highly accurate information about what was present in the body at the time, they do not directly identify prescription or dispensing patterns, with non‐adherence certainly a common issue in BD. Similarly, we cannot determine the timing of any potential non‐adherence. For example, some decedents may have been taking other prescribed medications that were discontinued proximal to death but far enough in time from death/toxicology, based on their rate of clearance, to not have been detected. Common presence of alcohol (42.3%) and opioids (27.7%) also points to an interplay of acute states of intoxication and comorbidities that further complicate the picture. The BD group had a significantly higher proportion of comorbid alcohol/substance use problems, which underscores the importance of addressing substance use as a chronic and acute risk factor for suicidal behaviors in this patient population. Other differences between the BD and non‐BD groups such as likelihood of an identified psychosocial stressor should not be over‐interpreted given the possible differences in the degree to which coroners seek out additional contributing/motivating factors once diagnoses of major mental illness have been established. Finally, toxicology results were only available for a subsample of both groups, mostly among those who died by self‐poisoning. Coroner investigation methodology and toxicology panels have also evolved over time, which could complicate the interpretation of some of the findings across time and for newer medications, although it is unlikely to be a systematic bias. Therefore, while the results are informative, they may not fully generalize to all BD suicide deaths. In conclusion, the results from this large sample of BD suicide deaths reinforce known patterns of sociodemographic and clinical differences between BD and non‐BD suicide groups. The patterns of medications and substances present at the time of death also suggest some change over time in the pharmacotherapy and utilization pattern of compounds among those with BD who die by suicide, such as a reduced presence of mood stabilizers. While causality cannot be claimed based on this study alone, there is certainly reason for concern and need for further exploration of pharmacotherapy at the time of suicide death in people with BD, ideally also linked to treatment patterns in living controls with BD.

[conclusion] Conclusions The findings of this study suggest that a substantial proportion of people with BD who die by suicide are not receiving first‐line treatments at the time of death. While causality cannot be established, further exploration of pharmacotherapy at the time of death would ideally link to living controls to address issues of risk and lethality.

retrieved_papers.title: Gabapentin and lamotrigine in bipolar disorder.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 1999]
[Abstract] The utility of gabapentin and lamotrigine for the treatment of bipolar disorder is reviewed. Bipolar disorder is characterized by extreme mood fluctuations, including mania, hypomania, depression, and mixed episodes. Extrapolation of postulated mechanisms of anticonvulsant activity in bipolar disorder has led to the use of the newer anticonvulsants gabapentin and lamotrigine for therapy. Both agents appear promising on the basis of limited (often anecdotal) evidence. They may prove effective in patients with difficult cases of bipolar disorder, such as patients with rapid cycling, mixed episodes, and illness refractory to other treatments. Lamotrigine may offer a much-needed treatment alternative for bipolar depression and could be found effective for acute mania, but the need for slow dosage adjustment and the risk of rash may limit overall clinical utility. Gabapentin may offer significant advantages for acute mania: The dosage can be adjusted rapidly, adverse effects are generally minimal, the therapeutic index is high, there is no required laboratory monitoring, and there is minimal potential for interactions with other psychotropics. Until the results of randomized controlled trials are known, however, these two agents should be reserved for patients with bipolar disorder unresponsive to traditional therapies and for patients who cannot tolerate traditional agents. Preliminary evidence indicates that gabapentin and lamotrigine may be useful for the treatment of bipolar disorder.

[Introduction] Introduction Gabapentin was approved by the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) to treat partial seizures and postherpetic neuralgia [ 1 ]; however, gabapentin is now widely prescribed for numerous off-label conditions [ 2 ]. In fact, gabapentin was the 7th top prescribed medicine in 2019 [ 3 ], despite limited evidence supporting its off-label efficacy and safety. Off-label conditions include neuropathic pain, migraines, substance use disorder, psychiatric symptoms (including anxiety and depression), and refractory chronic cough [ 2 , 4 – 10 ]. In a commercially insured adult population, gabapentin prescriptions nearly doubled from 2009 to 2016 [ 11 ]. Similarly, a study using Danish healthcare registries found that the incidence of new gabapentin users quadrupled from 2010 to 2023 [ 12 ]. This increasing trend also was observed in American older adults, where participant-reported gabapentin use almost tripled from 2006 to 2019 among adults aged 65 years and older [ 13 ]. Despite its widespread off-label use, gabapentin’s efficacy and safety in older adults remain areas of concern. Placebo-controlled trials indicate that its efficacy in managing painful conditions is inconsistent or minimal [ 14 , 15 ]. Notably, these trials included younger adults, leaving an additional gap in evidence specific to older adults. Emerging evidence also highlights gabapentin’s potential for misuse and dependence [ 16 , 17 ], with motivations including misuse for euphoric effects, polydrug use, self-medication, and substitution for other drugs [ 18 , 19 ]. Furthermore, gabapentin use has been linked to an increased risk of falls and fractures, which is particularly concerning in older adult populations [ 20 – 22 ]. We recently reported that gabapentin initiation among older adults with normal cognition was associated with cognitive and functional status decline, while those with prevalent cognitive impairment experienced a higher risk of falls [ 22 , 23 ]. Concerns about the safety and the misuse potential of gabapentin resulted in reclassifying gabapentin as a Schedule V controlled substance medication in Kentucky (July 2017), followed by Alabama, Michigan, North Dakota, Tennessee, Utah, West Virginia, and Virginia, and its listing as a “drug of concern,” reportable to the state prescription drug monitoring program in 13 other states as of April 2023 [ 24 ]. In 2019, the FDA issued a warning about the potential risk of breathing difficulties associated with concurrent use of gabapentin and opioids or other central nervous system (CNS) depressants [ 25 ]. That same year, the American Geriatrics Society updated the Beers Criteria, adding gabapentin to the list of medications for which to avoid concurrent treatment with opioids [ 26 ]. Despite the potential risks, little is known about how gabapentin is used among older adults, including prescribing trends, patterns of use, and characteristics of gabapentin users. This study aimed to address these gaps by characterizing gabapentin initiators and examining usage patterns among older adults.

[methods] Methods Data Source We used a 5% random national sample of Medicare beneficiaries with Part D enrollment at any time from 2012 to 2021. For this study, data from annual Master Beneficiary Summary Files, Part D event files, chronic conditions data warehouse (CCW), outpatient claims, and Medicare Provider Analysis and Review (MedPAR) files were incorporated. Beneficiaries were included if they were enrolled in Medicare Parts A, B, and D owing to age. We excluded the months when beneficiaries had health maintenance organization (HMO) enrollment or were dually eligible for Medicare and Medicaid (Fig. 1 ). Each beneficiary’s continuous enrollment was determined, allowing for a gap of up to 2 months. Fig. 1 Sample selection flowchart. *Prescription with extended tablet, solution excluded ( N = 35 prescriptions; < 0.01%). Prescription with > 10,000 mg/day excluded ( N = 137 prescriptions; < 0.01%). FFS, fee-for service Cohort Selection Beneficiaries with gabapentin prescription were identified if they received at least one gabapentin prescription during their continuous enrollment period. Gabapentin prescriptions were identified using national drug codes (NDC) linked with Medi-span Generic Product Identifiers (GPI), excluding Gralise ® Starter. Since the maximum recommended therapeutic dose of gabapentin is 3600 mg/day [ 27 ], prescriptions with an average daily dose (dose × quantity/days of supply) exceeding 10,000 mg/day (137 prescriptions) were excluded as likely data entry errors. To mitigate bias from prevalent users, this study included only beneficiaries initiating gabapentin, defined as having at least 6 months of continuous eligibility prior to the first date of gabapentin prescription (i.e., washout period) (Fig. 1 ). The first date of gabapentin prescription was defined as the index date. Prevalence/Incidence of Gabapentin Use by Year The prevalence of gabapentin use was measured annually from 2013 to 2021. Prevalence was calculated by dividing the number of beneficiaries with at least one gabapentin prescription during the year by the total number of eligible beneficiaries during that specific year. To calculate the incidence rate, the number of new gabapentin users was divided by the total number of eligible beneficiaries in the given year. The year 2012 was excluded owing to the 6-month washout period required to determine initiation. Characterizing Gabapentin Use The average number of days of supply from the first gabapentin prescription were calculated; if multiple prescriptions were present at the index date, the prescription with the longer supply was used. Similarly, the average daily dose from the first prescription was calculated. For beneficiaries with more than one prescription at the index, the average daily doses were summed across all prescriptions. To examine maintained gabapentin dosing, the average daily dose from the second prescription was calculated for beneficiaries with additional prescriptions following the index date. Demographic information, including age, sex, and race, for gabapentin initiators was obtained from the Master Beneficiary Summary Files. To assess medical history during the 3 months prior to and including the index date—which may provide context for gabapentin prescription, given that data on prescribing reasons were not available—we identified diagnoses for the following conditions: acute pain, back pain, chronic pain, neuropathic pain, epilepsy, postherpetic neuralgia, migraine, depression, alcohol use disorder, and substance use disorder using MedPAR and outpatient files. These conditions were defined using the International Classification of Diseases, Ninth Revision (ICD-9) and ICD-10 codes (Supplementary Table S1 ) located in the first and second positions of the diagnosis fields. Similarly, emergency department (ED) utilization was identified if beneficiaries had any ED charge amount recorded in MedPAR files or Revenue Center Codes 0450–0459 or 0981 in outpatient files during 30 days prior to and including the index [ 28 , 29 ]. Hospitalizations were identified if a record existed in MedPAR during 30 days prior to and including the index, and provider indicator codes were used to classify stays as short-term, long-term, or skilled nursing facility (SNF) stays. Surgical procedures were identified as major therapeutic procedures using ICD-9-Clinical Modification (CM) or ICD-10-Procedure Coding System (PCS) codes from MedPAR files [ 30 , 31 ] or by applying the surgery flag algorithm using Healthcare Common Procedure Coding System/Current Procedural Terminology (HCPCS/CPT) codes from outpatient files [ 32 ] within the same time frame. Chronic conditions, such as Alzheimer’s disease, diabetes, depression, osteoporosis, hip fracture, osteoarthritis or rheumatoid arthritis, hypertension, atrial fibrillation, congestive heart failure, ischemic heart disease, stroke, and cancer, were examined using CCW files. For the current study, chronic conditions were considered present if their first recorded occurrence date was prior to the index date. Medication information on CNS depressant use during the 6-month washout period, including opioids, benzodiazepines (BDZ), antidepressants, and antipsychotics, was identified using Part D event files. Antidepressants were further specified by drug class (e.g., serotonin and norepinephrine reupdate inhibitors (SNRIs); selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs); and tricyclic antidepressants (TCA)). Owing to the established risk of concurrent use of opioids and gabapentin, opioid prescriptions at the index date were also examined. Detailed variable definitions, including specific ICD-9 and ICD-10 and GPI codes used for identification, are provided in Supplementary Table S1 . The study design is shown in Fig. 2 . Fig. 2 Depiction of study design and measurement time points Continuous Use of Gabapentin Gabapentin prescription patterns were evaluated using Part D event files. Continuous gabapentin prescriptions were defined as those with a gap of 30 days or fewer between fills. We calculated the number of prescriptions, the mean duration of continuous use (from the index date to the first gap exceeding 30 days), and the average daily dose for 90-day, 180-day, and 365-day follow-up periods. In addition, we determined the proportion of beneficiaries with 100% coverage of gabapentin (allowing for a 30-day gap) over 90 days, 180 days, and 365 days. Subgroup Analyses For subgroup analyses, gabapentin initiators were categorized on the basis of the duration of continuous gabapentin use: ≤ 90 days, > 90 days and ≤ 180 days, and > 180 days (allowing for a 30-day gap). For each subgroup, we measured demographics, recent medical history, chronic conditions, and healthcare utilization. Furthermore, since the FDA-recommended maximum daily dose of gabapentin is 3600 mg/day, we identified beneficiaries with an initial daily dose exceeding 3600 mg/day (up to 10,000 mg/day), to further describe their demographics, recent medical history, and chronic conditions.

[results] Results The study included 1,887,445 eligible Medicare fee-for-service (FFS) beneficiaries aged 65 years and older. Among the 1,887,445 eligible Medicare beneficiaries, 358,255 (19.0%) were identified as gabapentin users, and 247,612 (13.1%) were identified as gabapentin initiators (Fig. 1 ). The prevalence of gabapentin prescriptions increased from 6.7% of beneficiaries in 2013 to 10.2% in 2021. The incidence of gabapentin initiation remained similar between 2013 (2.4%) and 2021 (2.7%) (Fig. 3 ). Fig. 3 Time trends (prevalence and incidence) for gabapentin prescription among Medicare FFS beneficiaries (2013–2021) The mean (SD) age of gabapentin initiators at the time of initiation (index) was 76.1 (SD 7.1) years, and 61.5% were female. The median number of days of supply at the index was 30 days (interquartile range (IQR): 30–30), and the average daily dose was 482.2 mg/day (SD 389.0). Among beneficiaries who had a second gabapentin prescription after initiation, the average daily dose slightly increased (553.1 mg/day (SD 447.6)) (Table 1 ). Approximately 13% of gabapentin initiators had both opioid and gabapentin prescriptions starting on the same date. ED visits and hospitalizations during the baseline period occurred in 13.1% and 10.3% of beneficiaries, respectively, with the majority of hospitalizations being short stays (9.4%). In addition, 8% of gabapentin initiators underwent a surgical procedure within 30 days prior to and including the index date (Table 1 ). Since the growth in gabapentin prescriptions is hypothesized to be mostly for off-label use, we examined the recent medical history of beneficiaries to provide context for gabapentin prescribing. Epilepsy (0.2%) and postherpetic neuralgia (0.4%), the approved indications for gabapentin, had low prevalence among gabapentin initiators. However, 39.5% of beneficiaries had a diagnosis for at least one pain condition documented, and 32.6% had a diagnosis of chronic pain. Chronic conditions documented prior to initiation included hypertension (82.3%), osteoarthritis/rheumatoid arthritis (69.2%), ischemic heart disease (43.3%), diabetes (34.3%) and depression (31.6%) (Table 1 ). To assess continuous use, we analyzed gabapentin prescriptions during 90-day, 180-day, and 1-year follow-up. During the first 90 days, beneficiaries had an average of 1.9 (SD 1.2) gabapentin prescriptions with median daily dose of 330 mg (IQR 300–827.6). The average number of continuous days, allowing a 30-day gap, was 54.4 (SD 30.1), and about 46% had full coverage of gabapentin prescriptions during this period. During 180 days of follow-up, the average number of prescriptions was 2.6 (SD 2.0), with a median daily dose of 352.9 mg/day (IQR 300–855.0). About 25% of gabapentin initiators had full coverage during this period. During 1-year follow-up, the average number of prescriptions was 3.5 (SD 3.4), and the median daily dose was 395.6 mg (IQR 300–866), with 15% maintaining continuous coverage for the full 1-year (Table 2 ). The average daily dose for the second gabapentin prescription was slightly lower in group 3 (median [IQR]: 300 [300, 797.1]) compared with groups 1 and 2 (both: 400 [300, 900]). The prevalence of chronic pain at baseline was higher in group 1 (34.1%) compared with group 3 (30.2%), while neuropathic pain was more common in group 3 (6.1%) compared with group 1 (4.7%). Group 3 also had a higher prevalence of diabetes (37.8% versus 32.1%) and depression (34.9% versus 29.8%) compared with group 1. Opioid co-prescription at index was more common in group 1 (15.9%) than in group 3 (9.4%), and ED visits (14.3% versus 11.6%), hospitalizations (12.5% versus 7.7%), and surgical procedures (10.8% versus 4.1%) were higher in group 1 (Table 3 ). The prevalence of several chronic conditions prior to the index date was higher in group 3 compared with group 1 (Alzheimer’s disease: 3.6% versus 2.4%; diabetes: 37.8% versus 32.1%; depression: 34.9% versus 29.8%; hypertension: 84.6% versus 80.9%) (Table 3 ). 1029 (1.7) Hospitalization (short) 15,713 (11.6) 3452 (6.9) 4116 (6.7) Surgery procedure (30 days prior to index date) 14,630 (10.8) 2488 (5.0) 2510 (4.1) All results presented are N (%) unless otherwise noted Gabapentin initiators exceeding the maximum daily dose of 3600 mg (≤ 10,000 mg) at index (i.e., high-dose users) were identified ( N = 107). These high-dose users had shorter days of supply compared with those within the recommended dose range, and pain was their only documented recent medical history. The prevalence of opioid prescription during washout period was higher in the group with high-dose users (45.8%) compared with others (38.8%). However, the high-dose users (4.7%) had fewer opioid co-prescriptions dispensed at the index date (4.7% versus 13.2%) but were more likely to have ≤ 90 days of continuous use (77.6% versus 54.7%) (Supplementary Table S2).

[discussion] Discussion This study characterized gabapentin users and examined patterns of gabapentin use among Medicare FFS beneficiaries. Gabapentin use in this population increased from 2013 to 2021, apparently driven largely by prescribing to treat off-label conditions, such as chronic pain. Our findings highlight that gabapentin initiators had a high burden of chronic conditions and often had a history of opioid or antidepressant use. These patterns raise important concerns about the appropriateness and safety of gabapentin use in this population. The prevalence of gabapentin use among Medicare FFS beneficiaries aged 65 years and older increased from 2013 to 2021. This trend aligns with prior studies in other populations and older adults in the USA. For example, data from Medical Expenditure Panel Survey data (adults aged > 17 years) showed an increase in gabapentinoid prescriptions from 1.2% in 2002 to 4.7% in 2021, which was predominately driven by gabapentin use [ 33 , 34 ]. Similarly, analyses of IBM MarketScan claims (adults aged 18–64 years) reported an increase in gabapentin prescriptions from 1.2% in 2002 to 3.9% in 2015 and from 1.3% in 2009 to 2.7% in 2016, respectively [ 11 ]. Among older adults enrolled in cohort studies of brain aging, gabapentin use consistently increased from 2006 to 2019 across age, sex, and cognitive status groups [ 13 ]. A study from Denmark also observed that the most notable increase in gabapentin use was among the older adult population [ 12 ]. In this study, the FDA-approved indications of epilepsy and postherpetic neuralgia were reported in less than 1% of gabapentin initiators during the 3 months prior to the index date. In contrast, nearly 40% of these beneficiaries had a diagnosis of pain, with 32.6% having a documented diagnosis of chronic pain. However, previous studies have highlighted that postherpetic neuralgia is known to be underestimated in claims data owing to the absence of a unique ICD code [ 35 ]. To improve identification of beneficiaries with postherpetic neuralgia, some studies have incorporated criteria that include herpes zoster diagnoses [ 35 , 36 ]. Yet, the prevalence of herpes zoster was also very low (2.3%) among gabapentin initiators in our cohort. These findings suggest that gabapentin prescriptions are mostly off-label among older adults. This is consistent with previous studies that report up to 95% of the gabapentin prescriptions are for off-label use [ 2 ]. One study of nursing home residents with Alzheimer’s disease and related dementias (ADRD) found that between 2015 and 2019, the percentage using gabapentin increased among residents with reported pain, while the percentage of opioids declined [ 37 ]. Despite limited or absent evidence supporting gabapentin’s efficacy for many off-label conditions, a case study reported that informal prescribing practices, including reliance on colleagues or professional meetings, may contribute to the steady growth in gabapentin’s off-label use [ 38 ]. Compared with the general older adult population, gabapentin initiators in our study exhibited a higher prevalence of chronic conditions. For instance, 82.3% had hypertension, 34.3% had diabetes, 31.6% had depression, and 20.8% had congestive heart failure. These rates surpass those reported in the National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (NHANES) for adults aged 60 years and older (hypertension, 74.5%; diabetes, 20.5%; depression, 8.0%; and congestive heart failure, 9.1% for those aged 80+ years) [ 39 – 42 ]. The high prevalence of diabetes among initiators may be attributed to gabapentin use for pain related to diabetic neuropathy [ 43 ]. In addition, since this study included beneficiaries with Part D coverage, the study sample may include beneficiaries with a higher burden of chronic conditions. However, the prevalence of Alzheimer’s disease was low among gabapentin initiators (2.7%) compared with the general older adult population (10.9%) [ 44 ]. Similarly, we previously reported that gabapentin use was less common in older adults with dementia (approximately 5%) than in those with cognitive impairment or normal cognition in 2019 [ 13 ]. Further research is needed to determine whether these findings reflect actual prescribing patterns or potential selection bias. Nonetheless, our results suggest that gabapentin initiators tend to have more comorbidities than the general population of older adults, with the exception of conditions such as Alzheimer’s disease. Almost two in five beneficiaries initiating gabapentin had a history of opioid use during the 6-month washout period, and 13.2% were co-prescribed gabapentin and opioids on the index date. Given gabapentin’s frequent use as an alternative to opioids [ 2 , 45 ], future studies should investigate whether beneficiaries are transitioning from opioids to gabapentin or using both concurrently. In addition, approximately 30% of gabapentin initiators had a history of antidepressant use during the washout period. While some antidepressants are prescribed for pain management, the majority were SSRIs rather than SNRIs, such as duloxetine, which is an FDA-approved medication for neuropathic pain treatment. These patterns suggest that gabapentin was prescribed to older adults with psychotropic conditions, often in the absence of strong effectiveness. Recent research has raised concerns that gabapentin may be replacing opioids and antipsychotics in response to deprescribing initiatives in older adults [ 46 ]. One study found that as antipsychotic prescribing declined in nursing homes, gabapentin and other antiepileptics increased substantially—despite limited evidence of their efficacy for behavioral and psychological symptoms of dementia and growing evidence of harm [ 37 ]. This highlights the importance of critically evaluating the rationale for gabapentin initiation and considering the potential harms of substituting one class of risky medications for another without robust supporting evidence. The subgroup analysis based on continuous gabapentin use (≤ 90 days, 91–180 days, > 180 days) revealed minimal differences across groups in terms of demographics and prescription patterns. However, those with longer continuous use were more likely to have neuropathic pain, chronic conditions, and antidepressant use, and less likely to have opioid prescriptions at index, ED visits, hospitalizations, or surgical procedures. These findings align with prior research, which reported that prolonged gabapentin use was associated with a higher Charlson Comorbidity Index, suggesting greater overall disease burden in long-term gabapentin users [ 47 ]. The absence of major differences across subgroups suggests that factors influencing prolonged gabapentin use are complex and require further investigation. This complexity highlights the need for future studies to elucidate the factors associated with long-term gabapentin use among older adults. This study has several limitations. First, although we assessed for recent medical history during 3 months prior to initiating gabapentin, identifying the specific reasons for gabapentin use in this population remains challenging using claims data. Second, the ICD codes used to capture the recent medical care and chronic conditions may result in misclassification. Third, the small number of high-dose gabapentin users in the subgroup analysis limits our ability to draw definitive conclusions. Fourth, to reduce bias from prevalent users, we applied a 6-month washout period to identify new gabapentin users. While this does not guarantee that participants were never prior users, the definition is appropriate for describing utilization patterns, as the study was not a comparative analysis. Lastly, this study included only FFS beneficiaries and excluded those with Medicare Managed Care and those with dual eligibility for Medicare and Medicaid, which limits the generalizability of the findings. In conclusion, this study highlights the increasing prevalence of gabapentin use among older adults, primarily among those without an apparent approved indication. Future studies are needed to investigate factors associated with continuous use of gabapentin and evaluate the appropriateness and safety of gabapentin in this population.

[conclusion] Conclusions Gabapentin was frequently prescribed, apparently off-label, in older adults with a high burden of chronic pain and comorbidities; initiators often had co-prescriptions of gabapentin with opioids. Future research is needed to investigate factors associated with extended gabapentin use (> 180 days) and its appropriateness in this population.

retrieved_papers.title: [Mood stabilizers].
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2009]
[Abstract] Within recent years the diagnostic concept of bipolar disorders has profoundly changed. The original view of one manic-depressive illness has become more and more a spectrum disorder ranging from personality traits to the full clinical feature of manic-depressive illness. Therefore, prevalence has increased in the general population. However, for differential treatment approaches with mood stabilisers the clinical distinction between bipolar I and bipolar II disorders becomes more and more relevant and, importantly, rapid cycling is a critical criterion for a differential indication of mood stabilisers. In acute treatment the psychopathological features such as euphoric versus dysphoric mania, the severity and the frequency of episodes play an important role for the choice of the mood stabiliser. According to international guidelines Lithium and Valproat are first-line treatment options. In addition, Lamotrigin has become a first-line treatment in special issues such as long-term treatment of bipolar depression. Carbamazepin, however, has lost its first-line place due to the evidence-based data situation and due to the side-effect profile. Within recent years the atypical anti-psychotics were investigated in bipolar disorder. Meanwhile, most of them have an indication for the treatment of acute mania, some of them for bipolar depression. Some studies also point to an efficacy in long-term prophylactic treatment. In summary, the psychopharmacological indications for the differential use of mood stabilisers are becoming more and more complex, therefore clear guidelines are needed.

retrieved_papers.title: Korean Medication Algorithm Project for Bipolar Disorder 2022: Comparisons with Other Treatment Guidelines.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2023]
[Abstract] The objective of this study was to compare recommendations of the Korean Medication Algorithm Project for Bipolar Disorder 2022 (KMAP-BP 2022) with other recently published guidelines for treating bipolar disorder. We reviewed a total of six recently published global treatment guidelines and compared treatment recommendation of the KMAP-BP 2022 with those of other guidelines. For initial treatment of mania, there were no significant differences across treatment guidelines. All guidelines recommended mood stabilizer (MS) or atypical antipsychotic (AAP) monotherapy or a combination of an MS with an AAP as a first-line treatment strategy in a same degree for mania. However, the KMAP-BP 2022 recommended MS + AAP combination therapy for psychotic mania, mixed mania and psychotic depression as treatment of choice. Aripiprazole, quetiapine and olanzapine were the first-line AAPs for nearly all phases of bipolar disorder across guidelines. Some guideline suggested olanzapine is a second-line options during maintenance treatment, related to concern about long-term tolerability. Most guidelines advocated newer AAPs (asenapine, cariprazine, long-acting injectable risperidone, and aripiprazole once monthly) as first-line treatment options for all phases while lamotrigine was recommended for depressive and maintenance phases. Lithium and valproic acid were commonly used as MSs in all phases of bipolar disorder. KMAP-BP 2022 guidelines were similar to other guidelines, reflecting current changes in prescription patterns for bipolar disorder based on accumulated research data. Strong preference for combination therapy was characteristic of KMAP-BP 2022, predominantly in the treatment of psychotic mania, mixed mania and psychotic depression.

[Introduction] INTRODUCTION Bipolar disorder is characterized by diverse symptoms including manic, hypomanic, depressive, mixed and psychotic features, and by chronically debilitating patterns that create challenges for treatment. Medical practice has shifted from individual experience-based to more evidence-based approaches from the early 1990s [ 1 ]. This trend has contributed to the development of treatment algorithms or clinical practice guidelines in psychiatric [ 2 ], including several treatment algorithms for mood disorder [ 3 - 10 ]. However, the medical landscape varies between countries. Often, the use of treatment guidelines may be dictated by cultural differences in clinical environments and medical situations, different health insurance policies and economic states, or the culture-specific imperatives of clinicians and patients. In Korea, a medication algorithm project called the Korean Medication Algorithm Project for Bipolar Disorder (KMAP-BP) was initiated in 2001. KMAP-BP was published in 2002 (KMAP-BP 2002) and its reliability has been confirmed [ 11 , 12 ], Revised versions of KMAP-BP were released in 2006, 2010, 2014 and 2018 [ 13 - 16 ]. Due to rapid developments in the psychopharmacologic field, newer atypical antipsychotics (AAP), mood stabilizers (MS), and other agents have been introduced for the treatment of bipolar disorder. To reflect current changes in treatment options for bipolar disorder, it was felt the earlier algorithm needed to be revised, resulting in publication of this Korean Medi-cation Algorithm Project for Bipolar Disorder in 2022 (KMAP-BP 2022) [ 17 ]. By identifying similarities and differences across treatment guidelines, our goal was to identify potential deficiencies in KMAP-BP 2022 that may require additional attention or supplementary information to enhance its usefulness in clinical practice. In this review article, we compared recommendations of KMAP-BP 2022 [ 17 ] with those of other recently published global treatment guidelines ( Table 1 ) [ 18 - 28 ].

[introduction] INTRODUCTION Bipolar disorder is characterized by diverse symptoms including manic, hypomanic, depressive, mixed and psychotic features, and by chronically debilitating patterns that create challenges for treatment. Medical practice has shifted from individual experience-based to more evidence-based approaches from the early 1990s [ 1 ]. This trend has contributed to the development of treatment algorithms or clinical practice guidelines in psychiatric [ 2 ], including several treatment algorithms for mood disorder [ 3 - 10 ]. However, the medical landscape varies between countries. Often, the use of treatment guidelines may be dictated by cultural differences in clinical environments and medical situations, different health insurance policies and economic states, or the culture-specific imperatives of clinicians and patients. In Korea, a medication algorithm project called the Korean Medication Algorithm Project for Bipolar Disorder (KMAP-BP) was initiated in 2001. KMAP-BP was published in 2002 (KMAP-BP 2002) and its reliability has been confirmed [ 11 , 12 ], Revised versions of KMAP-BP were released in 2006, 2010, 2014 and 2018 [ 13 - 16 ]. Due to rapid developments in the psychopharmacologic field, newer atypical antipsychotics (AAP), mood stabilizers (MS), and other agents have been introduced for the treatment of bipolar disorder. To reflect current changes in treatment options for bipolar disorder, it was felt the earlier algorithm needed to be revised, resulting in publication of this Korean Medi-cation Algorithm Project for Bipolar Disorder in 2022 (KMAP-BP 2022) [ 17 ]. In this review article, we compared recommendations of KMAP-BP 2022 [ 17 ] with those of other recently published global treatment guidelines ( Table 1 ) [ 18 - 28 ].

[discussion] DISCUSSION Although a number of guidelines have been offered to improve clinical practice, their enforcement has been difficult, because they have different characteristics in terms of clarity, simplicity of recommendations, reliability and use of evidence-based medicine [ 6 , 21 , 41 ]. In this review, we compared the recommendations of KMAP-BP 2022 [ 17 ] with those of other widely used treatment guidelines. For initial treatment of mania, there were no substantial differences across treatment guidelines. All guidelines recommend MS alone, AAP alone, or MS + AAP as first- line treatment strategies for mania. However, other guidelines recommend mood stabilizer or AAP monotherapy and combination therapy as equivalent first-line modalities, while MS + AAP combination therapy for psychotic severe manic episodes was ranked as TOC in KMAP-BP 2022. This may reflect that Korean experts are generally doubtful regarding the clinical effectiveness of monotherapy, and instead laid an emphasis on the superiority of combination therapy over monotherapy in terms of efficacy for mania, a conclusion based on results from clinical trials and meta-analyses [ 42 - 47 ]. The WFSBP 2009 guidelines [ 25 ], BAP 2016 [ 18 ] and CINP-BD-2017 [ 21 ] recommend Val as the only first-line MS medication. This result seems to reflect concerns regarding the safety of Li. However, guidelines have also advised that Val should not be used for women of child bearing potential because of its unacceptable risk of teratogenesis and impaired intellectual development of the fetus [ 18 , 21 , 23 , 25 , 48 ]. NICE 2014 guidelines do not recommend Li or Val as first-line treatment strategies in drug-naïve manic patients [ 22 ]. However, newly published KMAP-BP 2022 [ 17 ], as well as RANZCP 2020 [ 24 ] and CANMAT 2018 [ 19 ] recommended Li and Val as first-line MS agents for treating manic episodes. This discrepancy is thought to be related to the fact that NICE guidelines targeted a group of professionals working in various psychiatrics fields, hence providing relatively simply recommendations for diagnosis and treatment, rather than offering a full range of treatments differentiated in accordance with supporting evidence and recommendation strength. ARP, OLZ and QTP were first-line AAPs for manic episodes across guidelines, However, CANMAT 2018 [ 19 ], CINP-BD-2017 guideline [ 21 ] and RANZCP 2020 [ 24 ] recommended OLZ as a second-line strategy. This might reflect safety concerns, as well as tolerability and adherence issues [ 49 , 50 ]. However, OLZ and RIS showed superior antimanic effects over other AAPs in a meta-analysis [ 51 ], and OLZ and QTP monotherapy are known to reduce the overall risk of relapse [ 19 , 52 ]. The authors recommend that these diverse opinions should be considered in clinical practice. Additionally, ASP and CAR were recommended as first-line options in newly published guidelines [ 19 , 21 , 24 ]. In cases of non-response or incomplete response to first-line strategies, guidelines recommended switching or adding another first-line agent. KMAP-BP 2022 [ 17 ] also recommended switching from an MS or AAP to a different agent of the same type. However, triple combinations such as MS + 2AAPs or Li + Val + AAP were also suggested as next-step interventions for severe psychotic mania. In severe mania, a closed inpatient setting is common, and rapid symptom improvement is often the first goal. Despite concerns about the safety concern of the polypharmacy, there is a report that suggests the initial triple combination reduced the overall use of neuroleptics and shortened the hospital stay in severe manic patients [ 53 ]. Also, the fact that the TOC in Korea for severe psychotic mania is MS + AAP may be one reason. ECT and CLZ were recommended in most guidelines, while chlorpromazine, clonazepam, tamoxifen and rTMS were only recommended in CANMAT 2018 [ 19 ]. KMAP-BP 2022 recommended monotherapy of MS or AAP as the first-line strategy for only mild to moderate depression. MS + AAP, MS + LTG and AAP + LTG combinations were preferred for moderate depression to severe psychotic cases. However, other guidelines recommended monotherapy and combination therapy to approximately similar degrees in the first-line strategy for bipolar depres-sion. Additionally, RANZCP 2020 guideline’s first-line strategy was a monotherapy of Li, LTG, Val, QTP, LUR and CAR, and also CINP guideline only recommended QTP, LUR and OFC as a first-line. Although MS and AAP monotherapy were supported by higher degrees of evidence for bipolar depression, Korean experts preferred combination treatment over monotherapy for treating bipolar depression as in mania. This might be because a high proportion (70.1%) of the Korean experts who participated in KMAP-BP 2022 worked at university hos-pitals. Their primary interests might lie in treatment-resistant cases that generally require combination therapies. More-over, there were methodological differences between KMAP-BP 2022 and other guidelines (e.g., expert consensus vs. evidence-based). Furthermore, polypharmace-utical approaches to psychotropic medication appear to be increasingly common in clinical practice [ 54 ], suggesting that it is difficult to apply research-based findings to real clinical fields. In KMAP-BP 2022, the use of ADs in depressive episodes was limited as a secondary option and only for combined use, and these results were similar to those of other guidelines [ 19 , 21 , 24 ]. These results reflect the controversy and debate surrounding the use of ADs for bipolar depression due to potentially increased risk of harms, such as affective switching, higher suicide risk, and increased risk of development of mixed states/rapid cycling, and a limited evidence for their efficacy [ 55 , 56 ]. More-over, two recent meta-analyses [ 57 , 58 ] reported that adjunctive ADs in bipolar depression yield only small, non- significant benefits without increased risk of switching into mania. The most recent meta-analysis concluded that their findings were supportive of guideline recommen-dations to use adjunctive AD as a second-line or lower-category treatment [ 58 ]. Strict prohibition of AD monotherapy and increasing preference for LTG were found in all guidelines. This increasing preference for LTG seems to reflect the finding that LTG is more effective than placebo in LTG monotherapy and in adjunctive therapies [ 59 ]. However, adjunctive AD use with MS or AAP was also widely recom-mended. ARP, QTP and OLZ were recommended as first-line monotherapies (mild to moderate episodes) or adjunctive therapy for bipolar depression in KMAP-BP 2022. How-ever, ARP was categorized a 3rd-line recommendation by CANMAT 2018 and CINP-BD 2017 guidelines. This reflects some results showing that ARP monotherapy is not superior to placebo [ 60 , 61 ]. However, due to partial agonism at the dopamine 2 receptor, aripiprazole is unlikely to result in affective flattening or cognitive problems determined by dopamine antagonism in the mesocortical pathways. Indeed, it has been observed that ARP is unlikely to precipitate depression, and has a relatively favorable tolerability profile, with a lower risk for weight gain, dyslipidemia, diabetes, and hyperprolactinemia in bipolar disorder [ 62 ]. Another meta-analysis suggested that ARP monotherapy could be effective for treatment of acute depression because combined data from two negative studies revealed a significant effect [ 55 , 63 ]. Reportedly in Korea, the proportion of patients with bipolar depression prescribed with ARP increased from 1.4% (2004−2006) to 8.5% (2011−2014), but the mean initial and maximum dose was 15 mg/day and 30 mg/day respec-tively in 2004−2006 and 6.3 mg/day and 16.8 mg/day respectively in 2011−2014 [ 64 ]. The high preference for, and use of ARP for treating bipolar depression in Korea could be based on some evidence that supports the efficacy of ARP for bipolar depression. Additionally, low do-ses of aripiprazole, (5 mg or less), were reportedly more effective and better tolerated than higher ones for treating bipolar depression [ 65 ] and it was suggested clinicians should start treatment with a very low dose and give patients time to respond. In KMAP-BP 2022, adjunctive use of CLZ, buspirone, stimulant and thyroid hormone, and ECT and rTMS were the 3rd-line strategy for bipolar depression.
